title: Simple way to temporarily turn off exclusions for QuickOpen files
document:
Simple way to temporarily turn off exclusions for QuickOpen files

Using a transpiled language I sometimes find the need to look at the transpiled code which typically is excluded from QuickOpen. Having a simple way to turn exclusions off would simplify this. Some options come to mind:
- Have a 'Turn Off Exclusions' entry at the end of the results list.
- Have an additional QuickOpen prefix that runs QuickOpen with exclusions turned off.
- Have an option toggle next to the QuickOpen entry field to turn exclusions off.
canonical: QuickOpen does not provide an easy temporary way to disable file exclusion filters, making excluded files hard to search when the user needs to inspect generated output.

title: Feature Request: Add "problems.exclude" setting
document:
Feature Request: Add "problems.exclude" setting

We have `files.exclude` which removes any ability to reference a file within the Workspace, as well as `search.exclude` to hide from search. In my case, I want to be able to view and search files within `vendor` directories, so I have not added them to either of the exclude settings, but as a result row upon row of issues flood the problems panel, which hide problems within my own code. It would be great to be able to exclude these directories only from appearing in the problems panel with a `problems.exclude` setting, which would still allow them to be used for search, go to definition and intellisense etc.
canonical: The problems panel lacks a way to exclude specific folders or files from problem reporting without also hiding them from search and code navigation, causing vendor issues to clutter the panel and obscure problems in the user’s own code.

title: Option to widen the command palette
document:
Option to widen the command palette

I have a number of files that are quite deep in the file structure and some of them share names at least partially. It's a common occurrence that I will search for a file, get several options, and be unable to discern which of the results are the exact one I'm looking for.

I want to be able to
a) Make the width of the command palette increase automatically to include the full path
b) Manually set a px width for the command palette

PS. If there is a way to do either of these already, I have been unable to find it, and would appreciate being told how.
canonical: The command palette is too narrow to show enough of each file path, making similar search results hard to distinguish because its width cannot be widened automatically or set manually.

title: Links/Samples where language auto-detection doesn't work
document:
Links/Samples where language auto-detection doesn't work

This issue is just to enumerate samples and things where auto-detect didn't work and why. There's likely not much that can be done beyond totally retraining the ML model... but at least we can have them in one place.

https://www.programiz.com/python-programming/examples/add-number
canonical: Language auto-detection fails on some sample pages such as the linked example because the page content does not provide enough reliable language-specific cues for the model to identify the correct language.

title: Add support for AAC codec in webviews to read MP4 audio correctly
document:
Add support for AAC codec in webviews to read MP4 audio correctly

Currently MP4 videos with an AAC audio codec don't play audio in VSCode. 

AAC is currently the most popular audio codec, it is the default on many softwares like OBS and other capture softwares.
As such it is used in screen recordings for documentations in many projects.

Supporting AAC in webviews would allow the user to preview documentation `.md` files containing videos. And also allow the user to use mp4s in python notebooks (eg: Machine learning / data science applications).

Currently it is impossible to make a video readable both by MacOS (QuickTime) and VSCode because there is no overlap in the supported audio formats. Supporting AAC is essential to make sure that the documentation is readable on all platforms.

VSCode includes FFMPEG as a dependency and it already includes the necessary AAC codecs. This proposal will not include new dependencies but only enable codecs already present in VSCode through FFMPEG.

---

Useful link: [Release Notes: supported audio and video formats](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-docs/blob/vnext/release-notes/v1_72.md#built-in-preview-for-some-audio-and-video-files)

After reading the release note above, I am curious as to why this hasn't been fixed at the same time.
canonical: Webviews cannot play MP4 files with AAC audio because the embedded media playback support does not enable the AAC codec already available through the existing FFMPEG dependency.

title: Aux window: allow detaching views and panels
document:
Aux window: allow detaching views and panels

I would like to detach bottom panel tabs (especially "terminal", but also "problems" and others) into a separate window that I can place on my secondary monitor, **but** still be attached to the same main window. Possibly the whole bottom panel could be detached as a single window, like developer tools in chrome. 

These now take valuable screen space in my main window, but if e.g. a monitor session is detached and attached to a different main window, ctrl-clicking filenames in error messages will open the file in that window, and not in my editing window, which defeats the purpose.

**Note**
This feature request is **only** about detaching bottom panels, specifically for dual monitor use. 

Please don't close this as a duplicate of #34442 (a 2017 issue which discusses using VS code's terminal as a standalone replacement for windows terminal apps, and is never going to get implemented so should be closed), nor #10121 (a 2016 issue which discussed a more general issue of moving all panels, primarily editor tabs, to new windows and was closed due to acrimony).
canonical: The app should let users detach bottom panel views into a separate window while keeping them linked to the same main workspace so actions like opening files still target the original editing window rather than the detached one.

title: Ship newer version of conpty
document:
Ship newer version of conpty

We currently depend on conpty as shipped by Windows. Unfortunately this means that fixes in conpty can take 1-2 years to make it to the user, provided they update to it. We want to ship conpty.dll similar to how Windows Terminal/wezterm does so we get all the latest improvements.

Many issues get closed as duplicates of this one as updating to conpty v2 will fix many problems in Windows terminals.

node-pty issue: https://github.com/microsoft/node-pty/issues/490
canonical: The terminal backend is missing fixes and improvements because it relies on the operating system’s built-in conpty instead of shipping a newer conpty library with the application.

title: Editor GPU: Support ligatures (editor.fontLigatures)
document:
Editor GPU: Support ligatures (editor.fontLigatures)

Ligatures aren't supported out of the box when using canvas. [The approach xterm.js uses](https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js/tree/master/addons/addon-ligatures) (which is not yet in vscode due to some bundling problems) is to read the font file to get the ligatures (requires a accepting permissions on web) and render those together using the concept of a "character joiner".
canonical: The editor does not render font ligatures when using the GPU canvas text path because ligature shaping is not supported there and would require a character-joiner style approach.

title: API to map auth server to auth provider
document:
API to map auth server to auth provider

## Context

In MCP land, and general OAuth land, we resolve what kind auth an MCP server needs by resolving to some OAuth Server Metadata. The important stuff in this is:

From this, we plan on mapping issuers to auth providers. So for example, if we see an issuer like so:

we will use the `github` auth provider.

`microsoft` is where it gets interesting... because technically it is N issuers all it one based on the path of issuer.

These are all valid issuers. By default, VS Code uses `organizations`, but you can override it with a secret scope: `VSCODE_TENANT:` so like `VSCODE_TENANT:common` or `VSCODE_TENANT:`.

The issuer is then used to make urls like:

which is the login page you see in your browser.
and 

which is what you use to get a token after your browser redirects back to VS Code with a code.

## Proposal

### Option 1: Leaning in to `VSCODE_TENANT`

We have a `resolveMcpAuthenticationScopes` which is passed in the `authorizationServer` (aka `issuer`) and the `scopes` (aka `scopes_supported`)

This will resolve the correct scopes. So:
* issuer: `https://login.microsoftonline.com/consumers/v2.0`
* `["api://foo/bar"]`
resolves to:

Then the Microsoft auth provider will have the correct scopes to build the request.

### Option 2: Making tenant first class

This is more work but maybe the direction we want to go... 

and then we implement this in the Microsoft auth provider where the tenant is extracted from `issuer` and used as a replacement for `VSCODE_TENANT`.
canonical: The bug is that the authentication system cannot reliably derive the correct Microsoft tenant-specific authorization and token endpoints from an issuer, so it may build OAuth requests with the wrong tenant unless the tenant is extracted or mapped explicitly.

title: Support Anthropic DXT Format For Packaging &  Installing MCPs
document:
Support Anthropic DXT Format For Packaging &  Installing MCPs

Please consider support Anthropic DXT packaging format for adding MCPs.

They gave us MCPs, now we get a packaging format from them - feels like a celebration! :-) 

Standardizing on the installation and packaging format for MCP's would be good for the community . If MS and Anthropic supported the format it would push other players to support it and greatly simplify all the problems we have installing MCP's and helping users diagnose their problems.

Now all we need is a discovery format using Gihub amd we will be set.

https://github.com/anthropics/dxt

Thanks for your consideration.
canonical: The app does not support the Anthropic DXT package format for installing and packaging MCPs, making MCP setup less standardized and harder for users to install and troubleshoot.

title: Meta: Sorry, your request failed. Please try again.
document:
Meta: Sorry, your request failed. Please try again.

This meta issue tracks scenarios where chat requests fail, with a variety of potential causes from the user's machine to a service disruption (especially for _preview_ models).

Error message:
> Sorry, your request failed. Please try again. …

Here are some potential workarounds you can try:
- Retry the request (🔄).
- Retry the request with a different model.
- Start a new chat (➕).
- Check your Copilot Chat output logs for details (`CTRL + SHIFT + U` or Command `Output: Show Output Channels…`, and then select `GitHub Copilot Chat`).

If the issue persists, please comment and attach the logs. We are continuously monitoring and improving service reliability, and any reports with logs will help us better understand and resolve potential causes.
canonical: Chat requests intermittently fail because the chat service cannot complete the request, often due to a transient backend or connectivity issue.

title: Windows: VS Code Insiders requires two restarts to update, if an update is already pending and another is available
document:
Windows: VS Code Insiders requires two restarts to update, if an update is already pending and another is available

Every time when I tried to update vs code insiders to new version through clicking the gear icon, it didn't install the update during the restarting time. Instead, after it restarted, it started to install the update in the background. After a few minutes, the "1" icon will pop up on the gear icon, and I need to restart it again to have it installed. I don't have this issue when using the stable version.
canonical: When an update is already pending, receiving another update causes the editor to only stage it after the first restart, so a second restart is needed to complete the install.

Investigate SIGINT from PromptInputModel being incorrectly used in chat terminal tool result

title: Revisit terminal tool running background terminals UX
document:
Revisit terminal tool running background terminals UX

- Some of the problems
 - Lots of background terminals get created
 - Cwd differs between terminals and confuses the agent
 - Cannot await for background terminals
 - Background status is fixed
 - Should almost all terminals run in background and allow the agent to await their status?
 - Foreground terminals that are actually never ending right now must be killed by user to manually stop waiting
canonical: The terminal tool’s background terminal handling is inconsistent and hard to control, causing many stray terminals, mismatched working directories, fixed background status, and no way for the agent to wait on or cleanly stop long-running processes.

title: Unable to add Gemini API key after clearing the existing key – “API key not valid” error persists

This started happening right after I cleared the existing Gemini API key (left it blank). Since then, I’m unable to add any Gemini API key again.

- Clearing temp/cache files

VS Code Insiders

canonical: Clearing the saved Gemini API key leaves the extension stuck treating the key as invalid, so it blocks adding a new Gemini key because the credential state is not reset properly.

title: For local MCP server installation, utilize runtime_hint
document:
For local MCP server installation, utilize runtime_hint

To pull a MCP server from a specific feed, we require usage of `npm exec` rather than `npx` as `npx` does not respect registry location. 

We're defining the server using `npm` though the execution is overwritten with `npx` instead:
canonical: Local MCP server installation uses the wrong executor for a specified feed because the runtime hint is ignored and `npx` is used instead of `npm exec`, so the registry location is not respected.

title: Failed to fetch models from Ollama even though ollama is running; Github Copilot Chat does not filter out Ollama Cloud Models
document:
Failed to fetch models from Ollama even though ollama is running; Github Copilot Chat does not filter out Ollama Cloud Models

I have ollama setup to use models locally, but vscode (snap), vscode-insiders (snap and apt variants) are unable to detect any models when ollama cloud models are installed. The continue.dev extension however works fine and is able to list ollama models with the autodetect defaults. I even tried disabling all extensions except for copilot chat. But I see the same issue. Also, the copilot extension is able to detect all cloud models I have access to on my github account.

- GitHub Copilot Chat Extension: 0.34.2025112701 (prod)
- VS Code: 1.107.0-insider (c9b7a5ad103c1460870ef24a236eab9e518961fc)
- OS: linux 6.14.0-1016-oem x64
- Ollama version: 0.12.10

User Settings:

Ollama endpoint settings are defaulted - `"github.copilot.chat.byok.ollamaEndpoint": http://localhost:11434`

Using [Network Proxy Test](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=chrmarti.network-proxy-test) shows that it should have worked fine 

Possible duplicates: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/279407 (but that one shows a different error log and is on a different os)

1. Pull any Ollama cloud model (Eg: `gemini-3-pro-preview:latest`) 
2. Open VSCode/Insiders -> Go to Chat -> Manage Models
2. Add Models -> Select Ollama. See that no ollama models show up. 

Update: When cloud models like `gemini-3-pro-preview:latest` were removed from ollama, Github Copilot Chat started listing all the models.
canonical: GitHub Copilot Chat fails to list local Ollama models when Ollama cloud models are present because it does not filter those cloud models out before fetching available models.

title: No vertical scrollbar for longer terminal outputs in panel chat
document:
No vertical scrollbar for longer terminal outputs in panel chat

Testing #280444

Prompt: "Run for loop that echos 1 to 100 inclusive"

The agent runs `for i in {1..100}; do echo $i; done` and successfully prints 1 to 100 inclusive, with each number on its own line, but I do not see a vertical scrollbar for that output. I am still able to use my mouse wheel to scroll through the output, so there is a workaround.

title: Slash Command /explain Missing From Inline Chat Suggestions in VS Code v1.107
document:
Slash Command /explain Missing From Inline Chat Suggestions in VS Code v1.107

- VS Code Version: v1.107
- OS Version: Edition Windows 10 Pro Version 22H2
 OS Build 19045.6691

(more details below)

1. Open VS Code v1.107 with GitHub Copilot Chat enabled.
2. Open a source file (for example, a TypeScript or Go file).
3. Place the cursor somewhere in the file, then invoke **Inline Chat** (for example, via the lightbulb/inline chat UI, or the inline chat keybinding).
4. In the inline chat input box, type `/` and wait for slash command suggestions.
5. Observe that `/explain` is **not** offered as a suggestion, and the inline chat UI does not surface it as an available command.

6. Now open the **normal Copilot Chat view** (the main chat/agent panel).
7. In the normal chat input box, type `/` and wait for slash command suggestions.

8. Observe that `/explain` **is** offered there, and can be used as expected.

Expected Behavior:

- The `/explain` slash command should be available and discoverable in **both**:
 - the normal Copilot Chat / agent text box, **and**
 - the inline chat box shown in the editor.
- Typing `/` in the inline chat input should offer `/explain` (and other supported Copilot slash commands) in the same way as the normal chat view, or at least behave consistently.
- From a user perspective, inline chat is just a different surface for the same chat experience, so the slash-command set should be consistent across both UIs.

Actual Behavior:

- In v1.107, `/explain` is:
 - **present and working** in the **normal Copilot Chat / agent text box** (it appears in the suggestions list after typing `/`, and runs as expected), but
 - **missing from the inline chat box** in the editor:
 - It does not appear in the slash command suggestions after typing `/`.
 - It is not discoverable via autocomplete in the inline chat input.
- This creates an inconsistent experience between the two chat entry points inside VS Code: users have to switch out of inline chat into the main chat view to use the same command.

Why this feels like a regression / issue:

- Historically, slash commands like `/explain` were a fast way to explain the code currently in context, with useful workspace awareness (active editor, local project context, etc.).
- Inline chat is the most natural place to use `/explain` on the code you are actively editing, but the command is no longer visible or suggested there, even though it is still supported in the main chat UI.
- This asymmetry makes it harder, and slower, to access the same Copilot Chat functionality directly from inline chat, and it is not obvious to users that they need to open the separate chat panel to regain the command.

Notes:

- The behavior is reproducible across restarts.
- The issue only makes sense with Copilot Chat enabled, and does **not** apply when all extensions are disabled, because then Copilot Chat (and its slash commands) are unavailable at all.
- If this is considered an extension-side issue rather than a core VS Code issue, please feel free to re-route it to the appropriate Copilot Chat component, or let me know where to file it instead.
canonical: The inline chat command picker is missing the explain slash command even though the main chat picker shows it, likely because the two chat surfaces are using different slash-command registries or filtering logic.

title: GPT‑5.2 often stops responding in the middle of multi‑step tasks
document:
GPT‑5.2 often stops responding in the middle of multi‑step tasks

Description
When using GPT‑5.2 via GitHub Copilot, the model frequently stops responding before completing all requested tasks.
I explicitly instruct GPT‑5.2 to execute all tasks in a single run, but it often halts partway through the work.
For example, when I provide five tasks, the response commonly ends with something like:

“Task 1 has been completed. Next, I will proceed with task X.”

At that point, the chat unexpectedly ends without continuing the remaining tasks.
This behavior occurs quite frequently with GPT‑5.2. In contrast, I rarely encounter this issue when using Claude Opus or Claude Sonnet under similar conditions.
Expected Behavior
GPT‑5.2 should continue executing and responding until all provided tasks are fully completed, especially when explicitly instructed to do so.
Actual Behavior
The response often stops after completing only the first task (or part of the tasks), requiring manual follow‑up prompts to continue.

(sorry for poor english )
canonical: GPT-5.2 sometimes prematurely stops midway through multi-step requests in Copilot instead of completing all tasks in one response, likely because the assistant’s turn is being cut off or truncated before finishing.

title: Quick Pick cannot be dismissed with mouse only (accessibility issue)
document:
Quick Pick cannot be dismissed with mouse only (accessibility issue)

Only Python (and Debugger, Pylance, Environment) are installed

**Environment:**
- VS Code Version: 1.107.1
- OS: Windows 10
- Extension: Python (triggered when clicking the Python version in the status bar)

**Steps to Reproduce:**
1. Click the Python version number in the bottom-right status bar.
2. The "Select Python Interpreter" Quick Pick dropdown appears at the top center of the window.
3. Try to dismiss it using only the mouse:
 - Click in the editor area
 - Click on the sidebar icons
 - Click the gear icon in the bottom-left
 - Switch to another application and back
 → None of these actions close the dropdown.

**Actual Behavior:**
- The dropdown cannot be dismissed with mouse-only actions.
- The only way to close it with the mouse is to select one of the options, which forces an interpreter change. (or need to find current interpreter)

**Expected Behavior:**
- The dropdown should be dismissible with mouse-only actions, such as clicking outside the box or having a visible "Cancel" button.
- This would prevent accidental interpreter changes and improve accessibility.

**Impact:**
- For most users this is a minor inconvenience.
- For users who rely solely on mouse input (e.g., some people with disabilities), this creates a significant accessibility burden because there is no way to cancel without a keyboard.

**Suggestion:**
- Add a visible "Cancel" button to the Quick Pick dropdown.
- Or allow mouse clicks outside the dropdown to dismiss it.
canonical: The Python interpreter selection quick pick cannot be dismissed with mouse-only interaction because outside clicks and other mouse actions do not close it, forcing a selection to cancel it.

title: Feature Request: Improve Visibility and Discoverability of Hidden Models
document:
Feature Request: Improve Visibility and Discoverability of Hidden Models

Description
When a model is marked as hidden, the current visual treatment (slightly dimmed text) is very subtle and easy to miss. It’s not always clear that the model has been intentionally hidden especially with certain themes.

Suggestion
Make hidden models more visually distinct—for example, by further dimming the row or applying a strikethrough across key fields—so it’s immediately obvious that the model is hidden.

Additionally, the column used to toggle model visibility does not have a label, which makes the feature less discoverable. Some users may not realize this functionality exists unless they’ve seen a tutorial.

Benefit
Improves clarity, reduces confusion, and makes the hidden-state and visibility controls more discoverable.
canonical: Hidden models are too subtly distinguished from visible ones and the unlabeled visibility toggle makes the hide/show feature hard to discover, likely because the current hidden-state styling is too weak and the control lacks an obvious label.

title: Adopt the new chat sessions controller api for Claude sessions
document:
Adopt the new chat sessions controller api for Claude sessions

We're replacing the chat sessions provider based API with a new controller based API (see `ChatSessionItemController` in `src/extension/vscode.proposed.chatSessionsProvider.d.ts`)

This API moves to a managed object model instead of a provider based model. This should be easier to work with and also let us expose new API functionality, such as callbacks when a chat session item is archived

General migration guide:
- Instead of calling `registerChatSessionItemProvider`, create a controller with `createChatSessionItemController`

- `provideChatSessionItems` becomes essentially the `refreshHandler`. Instead of returning the items, you should update `controller.items`

 - Whenever you create a chat session item, use `createChatSessionItem`. This makes sure the item is managed. You can set properties directly on it to update the item
 
Let me know if you run into any bugs or have any questions about using the new API!
canonical: The bug is that Claude sessions still use the old provider-based chat session API instead of the new controller-based managed API, so chat session items are not being created and updated through the new controller model.

title: Terminal chat streaming: Manual expand override not respected, panel auto-collapses on success
document:
Terminal chat streaming: Manual expand override not respected, panel auto-collapses on success

Testing #290471

## Issue Description
When a user manually expands the terminal output panel during command execution, the panel still auto-collapses upon successful completion. According to the feature specification, manual user toggles should override all automatic expand/collapse behavior.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open VS Code Chat
2. Run a long-running command with output, such as:
 
3. The panel will auto-expand when "starting" appears (correct behavior)
4. **While the command is still running**, observe that the panel is expanded
5. Wait for the command to complete successfully (exit code 0)
6. **BUG**: Panel auto-collapses despite not being manually toggled by the user

## Expected Behavior
Once the panel has auto-expanded (or if the user manually expands it), the panel should remain expanded after successful completion. The manual state should override the auto-collapse-on-success behavior.

## Actual Behavior
The panel auto-collapses on successful completion, ignoring that it was in an expanded state during execution.

## Impact
- User intent is not respected
- Users cannot keep output visible for long-running successful commands
- Breaks the documented override behavior: "Manual user toggle overrides all auto-expand/collapse behavior"

## Platform
- **OS**: Windows 11
- **Shell**: PowerShell
- **Date Tested**: January 27, 2026

## Related Settings
- `chat.tools.autoExpandFailures`: true (default)
- `chat.agent.thinking.terminalTools`: enabled
canonical: The terminal output panel auto-collapses after a successful command even when it has been manually or automatically expanded, because the user’s expanded state is not being respected as an override over the automatic collapse behavior.

title: Implementation handoff uses summary instead of actual plan file
document:
Implementation handoff uses summary instead of actual plan file

**Describe the bug**
When transitioning from local plan mode to background implementation, the background agent cannot find the generated plan file. Instead, it triggers a summarization of the conversation, which results in a summary that does not contain the real implementation plan details. This causes the background session to fail to locate and use the actual plan file, despite successful completion in plan mode.

**To Reproduce**
1. Complete a multi-turn conversation in plan mode using a prompt file and TPI URL.
2. After plan mode succeeds, initiate 'Start Implementation' in a new background session.
3. Observe in the background session logs (copilot logs) that the agent reports it cannot see the plan and performs a summarization instead.
4. The summary response does not correspond to the real plan file.

**Expected behavior**
The background agent should reliably locate and use the implementation plan file generated in plan mode, without falling back to summarizing the conversation.

**Screenshots or Logs**
- Example intent log: `"intent": "Reading implementation plan"`
- Example summary: Starts with `## User Prompt` and proceeds with a conversation review, not the actual implementation plan.

**Additional Context**
This results in failed handoff between modes and blocks users from initiating background implementation using completed plans.

**Environment**
- Repo: microsoft/vscode-copilot-chat
- Mode: Local plan mode to background implementation

**Suggested Fix**
Ensure the background implementation workflow uses the persistent plan file from plan mode, bypassing conversation summarization when a valid plan exists.

[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C09MVCZKLP4/p1769622835014959?thread_ts=1769622835.014959&cid=C09MVCZKLP4)
canonical: The background implementation handoff fails because it cannot find and use the generated plan file, so it falls back to summarizing the conversation instead of loading the real plan.

title: Terminal command edits are silently ignored when using Claude Agent SDK (works correctly with regular Copilot)
document:
Terminal command edits are silently ignored when using Claude Agent SDK (works correctly with regular Copilot)

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.37.1
- VS Code Version: 1.109.0
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200 using WSL2 Ubuntu
- Feature: Claude Agent SDK integration
- Selected model: Claude Opus 4.5

Description
When an agent proposes a terminal command, VS Code shows a prompt allowing the user to edit the command before executing. This works correctly in regular Copilot sessions — the edited command runs and the agent receives the edited output. However, when using Claude Agent SDK as the agent provider, user edits to the proposed command are silently ignored: the original agent-proposed command runs regardless, and the agent never sees the user's changes.

1. Open VS Code with Copilot extension (latest stable)
2. Switch to Claude Agent SDK as the agent provider
3. Start a task that causes the agent to run a terminal command (e.g., echo "Hello")
4. When the command approval prompt appears, edit the command (e.g., change to echo "Stop")
5. Click "Allow" to execute

Expected Behavior
- The edited command (echo "Stop") runs in the terminal
- The agent sees the edited command and its output
- Agent note appears: "Note: The user manually edited the command to echo 'Stop'..."
- This is how it works in regular Copilot sessions and is documented behavior that agents rely on for user steering.

Actual Behavior
- The original agent-proposed command (echo "Hello") runs instead
- User edits are silently discarded
- The agent has no awareness that the user attempted to modify the command

Real-World Impact
This is a significant workflow limitation. Users often edit terminal commands to:
- Steer the agent when it's about to do something slightly wrong
- Inject feedback mid-execution (e.g., editing echo "" to echo "stop, fix X first")
- Correct paths or flags before execution
Without this working, users lose a key interactive control mechanism and must wait for the agent to finish before providing feedback.

Concrete Example
During a multi-agent workflow, a subagent started reading irrelevant files. The user edited a terminal command to echo "End task" to signal the agent to stop. The edit was ignored — the original command ran, and the agent continued processing unrelated files, wasting context and tokens.

Notes
- Regular Copilot agent sessions handle command edits correctly
- This appears specific to the Claude Agent SDK integration path
- The edit UI prompt appears and accepts input, so the user believes their edit was applied
canonical: Claude Agent SDK integration ignores user-edited terminal commands at execution time, so the original proposed command runs and the agent never receives the modified command or its output.

title: Feature Request: Notification when AI Assistant Completes Task
document:
Feature Request: Notification when AI Assistant Completes Task

**Type:** Feature Request 
**Product:** VS Code AI Features (Claude, GitHub Copilot Chat, Kimi, etc.)

---

## Summary

Add a system notification when an AI assistant (Claude, Codex, Kimi) finishes a task that has been running in the background. Currently, users must manually check if a long-running task has completed, leading to wasted time either checking too early or forgetting and returning much later than necessary.

---

## Current Pain Point

### The "Waiting Game" Problem

When I send a complex request to an AI assistant in VS Code (e.g., "analyze this codebase and suggest refactoring opportunities"), it might take 6+ minutes to complete. This creates an awkward workflow:

1. **I wait for ~5 minutes**, get impatient, and check
2. **It's not done yet**, so I switch to another task to be productive
3. **I forget about it for 20+ minutes**
4. **I finally remember**, only to find it finished 15 minutes ago

**Net result:** Lost 15 minutes of potential productivity when I could have reviewed the results immediately and continued the conversation or implemented suggestions.

---

## Proposed Solution

### Primary: System Notifications

When an AI assistant completes a response (especially for long-running tasks), display a native system notification:

**Behavior:**
- Trigger when a response takes longer than X seconds (configurable, default: 30s)
- Clicking "View Response" brings VS Code to foreground and focuses the chat panel
- Respect OS Do Not Disturb settings
- Option to disable per-workspace or globally

---

## Prior Art / Inspiration

### Zed Editor Implementation

The Zed editor handles this beautifully:
- When an AI assistant is working, it shows a subtle indicator
- When complete, it plays a soft sound **and** shows a system notification
- Users can immediately jump back into flow without the "did it finish?" anxiety

---

## Alternative / Additional Ideas

### 1. Tab/Window Badge
Show a badge on the VS Code window icon or tab counter indicating "AI response ready":

### 2. Status Bar Indicator with Completion State
Current: `Claude is thinking...` → disappears when done 
Better: `Claude is thinking...` → `✅ Claude response ready` (clickable)

### 3. Optional Sound Notification
Soft, unobtrusive sound (like Zed's gentle "pop") when response completes. Respect system mute state.

### 4. "Notify when done" Toggle
Per-message opt-in checkbox: `[ ] Notify me when response is ready` 
Useful when you KNOW a request will take a while and you're about to context-switch.

---

## Configuration Suggestions

---

## Use Cases

| Scenario | Current Behavior | With Notification |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 10-min codebase analysis | User forgets for 20 min | Immediate notification, continue conversation |
| Multi-file code generation | Check every 2 min, distracting | Get notified, review immediately |
| Complex debugging help | Lose flow context | Seamless continuation |
| Running tests via AI | Unnecessary waiting | Productive context-switching with callback |

---

## System Information

- VS Code Version: 1.96+
- OS: macOS 15.2 / Windows 11 / Ubuntu 24.04 (all affected)
- AI Extensions: GitHub Copilot Chat, Claude, Kimi Code CLI, etc.

---

## Additional Context

This becomes increasingly important as AI tasks get more complex. With agentic features (multi-step code changes, test running, file analysis), tasks routinely take 2-10 minutes. The current "hope you remember to check" UX doesn't scale for this workflow.

This is a **quality-of-life** feature that would make VS Code's AI experience feel significantly more polished and professional, matching the bar set by newer editors like Zed.

---

**Would you be willing to submit a PR?** 
No (not familiar with VS Code internals), but happy to test and provide feedback.

**Does this affect multiple extensions?** 
Yes — ideally this would be a core VS Code API that any AI assistant extension can hook into (`vscode.ai.onRequestComplete` or similar), rather than each extension implementing its own notification logic.
canonical: AI assistant tasks can finish in the background without any completion notification, causing users to miss the result because the editor does not alert them when the response is ready.

title: Integrated terminal: Ctrl+C no longer interrupts running processes (regression in Insiders)
document:
Integrated terminal: Ctrl+C no longer interrupts running processes (regression in Insiders)

- VS Code Version: 1.110.0-insider (0366405821955682155a4507a122611c742df3fa)
- OS Version: macOS 26.1 (Build 25B78)

1. Open the integrated terminal in VS Code Insiders.
2. Use fish as the integrated terminal shell (fish at `/opt/homebrew/bin/fish`).
3. Run `sleep 100` (also seen with `top`).
4. Press Ctrl+C.

Expected:

`sleep` is interrupted (SIGINT) and the shell prompt returns.

Actual:

The process keeps running, and the terminal prints something like `[99;5u` (sometimes repeated).

Additional Information:

- Only reproduces with fish (fish 4.4.0). If I switch the integrated terminal profile to zsh, Ctrl+C works normally.
- Outside VS Code, Ctrl+C interrupts as expected in fish (Ghostty and macOS Terminal).
- `$TERM`:
 - VS Code: `xterm-256color`
 - Ghostty: `xterm-ghostty`
 - macOS Terminal: `xterm-256color` (Ctrl+C works)
- Ctrl+C also does nothing at the fish prompt (no running command).
- Disabling shell integration (`terminal.integrated.shellIntegration.enabled: false`) does not change the behavior.
- Reproducible with `fish --no-config`.
- `fish_key_reader` prints `bind ctrl-c 'do something'` in both VS Code and Ghostty.
- `[99;5u` looks like a kitty keyboard protocol "CSI u" sequence for Ctrl+C.

Repro videos:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c23fc089-2cec-4702-8f0b-41367749b007

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ce25877b-01ea-47ff-a8f2-14e07271b4f2
canonical: Ctrl+C is being sent as a kitty-style keyboard escape sequence instead of an interrupt signal in the integrated terminal, so fish receives it as text and does not stop the running process.

title: Terminal tool corrupts multiline commands exceeding 1024 bytes
document:
Terminal tool corrupts multiline commands exceeding 1024 bytes

## Bug

The terminal tool (`run_in_terminal`) corrupts **any multiline command exceeding 1024 bytes**. After byte 1024, content wraps around and replays earlier buffer content, destroying the remainder of the command and leaving the shell stuck in `quote>` or `heredoc>` mode.

## Minimal Reproduction

Ask Copilot to run this in the terminal (19 lines × 55 chars + wrapper = 1077 bytes):

**Expected**: Outputs `1064`
**Actual**: L19 becomes garbled (`L19 aaaaaL02 aaa...`), closing `'` is lost, shell stuck at `quote>`

Reduce to 18 lines (1021 bytes) → works perfectly. The boundary is exactly 1024 bytes.

## Verified Facts

| Test case | Result |
|---|---|
| 18 lines × 55 chars (1021 bytes), multiline | ✅ Works |
| 19 lines × 55 chars (1077 bytes), multiline | ❌ Corrupts at byte 1024 |
| Same 20-line content via `$'...\n...'` (1156 bytes, single-line) | ✅ Works |
| Single line of 2000+ chars | ✅ Works |
| `cat 1024 bytes | ❌ Corrupts at byte 1024 |
| `python3 -c "..."` multiline > 1024 bytes | ❌ Corrupts at byte 1024 |

The corruption only affects **multiline** commands (containing literal newline characters) and always occurs at **exactly byte 1024**.

## Why This Is a Serious Problem

The failure mode is **cascading and difficult to recover from**:

1. **Silent corruption**: The tool reports success ("The tool simplified the command to...") but the shell receives garbled data. The agent has no way to detect this before it happens.

2. **Shell gets permanently stuck**: The closing delimiter (`'`, `"`, `EOF`) is lost in the corruption, so the shell enters `quote>` or `heredoc>` mode. Every subsequent command the agent sends is swallowed as continuation of the broken quote — the terminal is bricked until a new one is opened.

3. **Extremely common trigger**: 1024 bytes is a very low threshold. Any of these routine operations can exceed it:
 - `gh issue create --body '...'` or `gh pr create --body '...'` with a detailed description
 - Heredoc writes (`cat > file << 'EOF'`)
 - Inline Python/Node scripts (`python3 -c "..."`)
 - Any multiline shell script pasted to the terminal

4. **Hard to diagnose**: The garbled output looks like random gibberish — fragments of earlier content mixed with later content. Without knowing about the 1024-byte boundary, this appears to be a non-deterministic, unreproducible glitch. (We initially filed and closed a report as "unreproducible" before discovering the byte-count trigger.)

5. **Recovery makes things worse**: When the agent sees an error or stuck shell, it typically retries. But the terminal is stuck in quote mode, so retries just feed more text into the broken quote, creating increasingly bizarre output and wasting tokens.

## Root Cause Hypothesis

The terminal tool uses a different input mechanism for multiline commands (likely bracketed paste or line-by-line writing to the PTY) compared to single-line commands. This multiline path appears to use a **1024-byte buffer** — a classic `PIPE_BUF` or PTY canonical-mode buffer size — that wraps on overflow rather than flushing and continuing.

## Environment

- VS Code Insiders
- macOS
- zsh
- Copilot agent mode (Claude)
canonical: The terminal tool corrupts multiline commands longer than 1024 bytes because its multiline input path uses a fixed 1024-byte buffer that wraps or truncates, causing lost closing delimiters and leaving the shell stuck in continuation mode.

title: Bug Report: `run_in_terminal` tool non-deterministically truncates command output
document:
Bug Report: `run_in_terminal` tool non-deterministically truncates command output

- **Copilot Chat Extension Version:** 0.37.8
- **VS Code Version:** 1.109.5 (x64)
- **OS Version:** Windows 11 (10.0.22621.0)
- **Feature:** Agent mode (tool: `run_in_terminal`)
- **Selected model:** Claude Sonnet 4.6 (reproduced across multiple models — model-independent)
- **Logs:** N/A (reproducible without logs)

---

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open VS Code with GitHub Copilot Chat
2. Ensure `"terminal.integrated.shellIntegration.enabled": true` in settings
3. In Copilot agent mode, ask it to run a command that produces multiple lines of output, e.g.:
 
4. Observe the output returned to the tool — it is frequently truncated
5. Repeat the same command 5+ times and note that the number of captured lines varies randomly between runs

---

## Expected Behavior

`run_in_terminal` should capture and return the **complete** command output on every run.

---

## Actual Behavior

Output is **non-deterministically truncated**. Across 10+ test runs of the same 3-line command, the tool returned:

| Captured lines | Frequency |
| ---------------------- | --------- |
| 0 lines (prompt only) | ~30% |
| 1 line | ~10% |
| 2 lines (missing last) | ~50% |
| All 3 lines (correct) | ~10% |

The **last line is most commonly missing**, but sometimes multiple lines or all output is missing. The truncation pattern is random across runs with no code or settings changes between them.

---

## Key Observations

- **Shell-independent:** Reproduced identically with PowerShell 7 (`pwsh`) and Git Bash — not a shell-specific issue
- **Model-independent:** Reproduced with multiple Copilot models — the AI model has no influence on this
- **Execution is always correct:** The `terminal_last_command` tool consistently returns the full output for the same commands, confirming the command executed successfully — only the live capture by `run_in_terminal` is broken
- **Panel visibility has some effect:** Output capture tends to fail more often when the terminal panel is visible/rendered, but also fails with the panel hidden — not the sole cause

---

## Example

**Command (PowerShell):**

**Tool output (typical failing case — missing last line):**

**`terminal_last_command` output for same run (always correct):**

**Command (Git Bash):**

Identical truncation pattern observed.

---

## Root Cause Hypothesis

Shell integration works by injecting escape sequences marking "command start" and "command end" boundaries. The `run_in_terminal` tool appears to snapshot the terminal buffer immediately upon detecting the "command end" marker — but at that point, the terminal renderer still has pending output in its pipeline that hasn't been painted to the buffer yet. The last line(s) are dropped because they haven't been rendered into the buffer by the time the snapshot is taken.

This is a **race condition** between:

- The shell writing the "command end" marker (triggering the snapshot)
- The terminal renderer flushing the final output lines into the visible buffer

**Suggested fix:** The tool should wait one render tick / frame after detecting the "command end" marker before reading the terminal buffer.

---

## Additional Issue: Misleading "blocked" UI label

The Copilot Chat UI labels `run_in_terminal` tool calls as **"Tried to run command but was blocked"** even when the command ran successfully after user approval. The label should read "required approval" or similar — "blocked" implies the command did not run, which is incorrect and confusing.

---

## Workaround

Use the `terminal_last_command` tool as a fallback after `run_in_terminal` to retrieve the full output. It reads the stored command result rather than a live buffer snapshot and returns complete output reliably.

---
canonical: The terminal command tool sometimes returns incomplete output because it snapshots the terminal too early after command completion, before all buffered output has been fully rendered.

> The keypresses meant for the first chat are instead sent to the other chat

title: Allow runSubagent tool to specify a language model
document:
Allow runSubagent tool to specify a language model

## Problem

When the LLM invokes the `runSubagent` built-in tool, the subagent always inherits the parent agent's model (or the custom agent's configured model). There is no way for the LLM to explicitly choose a different model for a subagent invocation, even when the user asks for it (e.g. "use Claude to research this").

Why:
- Multi-model consensus-based review, multiple models running in different subagents; has a growing fanbase
- Picking a faster model for quick research tasks

## Proposal

Add an optional `model` parameter to the `runSubagent` tool schema that accepts a qualified model name in `"Model Name (Vendor)"` format. The schema should dynamically enumerate available agent-mode-suitable models so the LLM knows which values are valid.

### Model resolution priority
1. **Explicit `model` parameter** — if provided and resolved
2. **Agent-configured model** — from the custom agent's `.agent.md` file
3. **Main model** — the model running the parent agent
canonical: The subagent tool always uses the parent or configured agent model and needs an explicit model parameter so a caller can choose a different available model for that subagent.

title: [042/d9f] potential listener LEAK in chatTerminalToolProgressPart.ts
document:
[042/d9f] potential listener LEAK in chatTerminalToolProgressPart.ts

## Error Message

## Stack Trace

**Primary bucket (`f9a4e690`):**

**Related bucket (`ea8af029`) — same file, different leak path:**
`

## Details
| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Primary Bucket ID | [`f9a4e690-4f46-5720-8ddc-ad6e4362233e`](https://errors.code.visualstudio.com/bucket/f9a4e690-4f46-5720-8ddc-ad6e4362233e?product=VSCode) |
| Related Bucket ID | [`ea8af029-2149-4b37-513c-8d86d2220d1b`](https://errors.code.visualstudio.com/bucket/ea8af029-2149-4b37-513c-8d86d2220d1b?product=VSCode) |
| Version | 1.112.0 |
| Commit | [`07ff9d61`]((insiders.vscode.dev/redacted) |
| Last Seen | 2026-03-23 |
| Combined Hits | 2.2M |
| Combined Affected Users | 142.5K |
| Platforms | Linux, Mac, Windows |
| Product | VSCode |

## Analysis

Multiple listener LEAK errors originate from `chatTerminalToolProgressPart.ts` in the chat terminal tool progress component. Two distinct leak paths are observed:

1. **Bucket `f9a4e690`** (1.2M hits / 64.9K users): Leak occurs during construction at line 1082 and line 356, triggered via `createToolInvocationSubPart` in `chatToolInvocationPart.ts`.
2. **Bucket `ea8af029`** (1M hits / 77.6K users): Leak occurs in `_initializeTerminalActions` at line 596, triggered via the `onDidContinueInBackground` event listener.

Both are new in 1.112.0 and appear across insiders and stable builds. The trend is **increasing** — this is a significant leak introduced with recent chat terminal tool progress changes.

---
*This issue was automatically created from error telemetry triage*

> Generated by [errors-triage](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-errors/actions/runs/23552236369) · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3Amicrosoft%2Fvscode+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+microsoft%2Fvscode-errors%2Ferrors-triage%22&type=issues)
canonical: The chat terminal tool progress component is leaking event listeners because listeners created during construction and terminal action initialization are not being properly disposed.

title: “Closing the window is taking a bit longer…” since upgrade to v1.113.0

“Closing the window is taking a bit longer…” since upgrade to v1.113.0

- VS Code Version: 1.113.0
- OS Version: macOS 26.3.1 (a)

1. In v1.113.0, open a workspace with a normal amount of extensions (I have 43 installed, and 29 listed in the "Running Extensions" window)
2. Close the workspace
3. The window takes longer than expected to close, and first displays a "Closing the window is taking a bit longer..." modal that reads that "Stopping Extension Hosts" is still running.
4. Downgrade to v1.112.0.
5. Open and close the workspace as normal.

Watching the exthost.log file, it looks like the exact same steps happen between both versions, specifically showing that there are a handful of extensions that do something such that "Error: Channel has been closed" shows up for them in the log (Gitlens, Copilot Chat, JSON language features, SonarQube IDE). But the difference is that in v1.112.0, the window closes before these errors are emitted to the log file; in v1.113.0, the window remains open until each of these errors is emitted (it's about a second between each), and THEN the window closes. And after some portion of that wait time, the above modal warning shows up.
canonical: Closing a workspace became noticeably slower because shutdown now waits for extension host errors and delayed extension cleanup to finish before the window closes, causing the closing dialog to appear.

title: "Configure Tools" rewrites YAML block sequence as inline array, corrupting .agent.md frontmatter
document:
"Configure Tools" rewrites YAML block sequence as inline array, corrupting .agent.md frontmatter

## Bug: "Configure Tools" UI does not preserve YAML block sequence format

### Environment
- VS Code: 1.112.x (Insiders)
- OS: Windows 11

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a `.agent.md` file with tools in YAML block sequence format:

2. Click the "Configure Tools" codelens / quick action in the frontmatter
3. Toggle any tool (enable or disable), or simply close the dialog

### Expected Behavior
The file should preserve the block sequence style:

### Actual Behavior
The write-back serializes as an inline `[]` array but keeps the key on its own line, producing:

This is either invalid YAML (the array is not properly indented as a value of `tools:`) or at minimum a destructive reformat that breaks version-controlled files and loses any YAML comments.

### Analysis
The read path correctly parses both inline (`tools: [a, b]`) and block sequence (`tools:\n - a\n - b`) formats. The write path always emits inline `[]` format regardless of the original style. The serializer does not preserve the source YAML style.

### Additional context
- If the file uses toolset names (e.g. `search`, `edit`), Configure Tools expands them to individual child tools, further corrupting intent
- YAML comments (e.g. `# --- category ---` or `# - disabled/tool`) are stripped entirely
- Related issues: #295404 (fixed quoting), #280817 (fixed quoting), #260556 (respecting configured tool set — still open)
- None of the above address format preservation during write-back
canonical: Using Configure Tools rewrites the frontmatter tools list into a different YAML style and strips comments, corrupting the file because the save path does not preserve the original block-sequence formatting.

title: Renderer hangs at 100% CPU after repeated Copilot agent terminal tool invocations (listener leak)
document:
Renderer hangs at 100% CPU after repeated Copilot agent terminal tool invocations (listener leak)

## Environment
- **VS Code:** 1.114.0
- **OS:** macOS 26.3.1 (25D2128), Apple Silicon (ARM64)
- **Extensions:** github.copilot, github.copilot-chat (latest)

## Summary
VS Code becomes unresponsive / hangs after Copilot agent runs several terminal commands in a session. The renderer process spins at ~100% CPU until the window is reloaded.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open VS Code and start a Copilot agent session (agent/edit mode).
2. Ask Copilot to perform a task that involves multiple terminal tool invocations (e.g. run tests, install deps, build project).
3. After ~5–15 terminal commands, VS Code UI freezes and becomes unresponsive.

## Expected Behavior
VS Code remains responsive throughout the agent session.

## Actual Behavior
The renderer process (`Code Helper (Renderer)`) pegs at ~99–100% CPU and the UI hangs. Only `Developer: Reload Window` recovers it.

## Root Cause (from logs + sampling)
The renderer log repeatedly shows listener leaks, each correlated with a Copilot terminal tool invocation:

A `sample` of the hung renderer PID confirms the main thread is stuck in a tight JS microtask loop (`v8::MicrotasksScope::PerformCheckpoint` → recursive `???` frames) — consistent with cascading accumulated event listeners firing.

Each terminal tool invocation appears to register new listeners on the terminal instance / tool session without disposing the previous ones. Over time the accumulated listeners cause the renderer to spin.

## Workaround
`Cmd+Shift+P` → `Developer: Reload Window` clears the listeners and restores responsiveness.

## Related
- microsoft/vscode-copilot-release#13945 — similar freeze on terminal approval prompt, closed as "not reproducible" Nov 2025. This issue is distinct: it occurs during normal terminal command execution, not the approval prompt, and reproduces reliably after multiple invocations.
canonical: Repeated Copilot terminal tool invocations leak event listeners in the renderer, eventually causing a tight microtask loop that pegs CPU and freezes the window.

title: `send_to_terminal` progress message repeats first question when accepting all defaults in interactive commands
document:
`send_to_terminal` progress message repeats first question when accepting all defaults in interactive commands

## Bug

When an interactive command like `npm init` is run and the user accepts all defaults via the `askQuestions` carousel, every `send_to_terminal` progress message displays the first question's text (e.g. "Package name?") instead of the correct question for each prompt.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a chat session in default approvals mode
2. Ask the agent to run `npm init` in a terminal
3. The agent detects the interactive prompts and shows an `askQuestions` carousel with multiple questions (package name, version, description, entry point, etc.)
4. Accept all defaults by leaving every field empty and submitting the carousel
5. The agent sends each default answer via `send_to_terminal` (pressing Enter for each prompt)

## Expected Behavior

Each `send_to_terminal` progress message should show the corresponding question:

- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name?)
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: version?)
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: description?)
- …

## Actual Behavior

Every progress message repeats the first question:

- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name? (default: @xterm/xterm))
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name? (default: @xterm/xterm))
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name? (default: @xterm/xterm))
- …

## Cause

`_getQuestionContextForTerminal` matches the sent command text against carousel answer values to find the corresponding question. When all answers are identical (empty string for defaults), the loop iterates questions in order and always matches the first one.

## Fix

Use positional matching: count prior `send_to_terminal` tool invocations after the carousel in the response parts to determine the question index, rather than relying solely on answer-value matching.
canonical: The terminal progress messages incorrectly reuse the first interactive prompt for every default answer because the question lookup matches on identical empty responses instead of the prompt’s position in the sequence.

title: [Terminal] Massive black block obscures input line in integrated PowerShell terminal
document:
[Terminal] Massive black block obscures input line in integrated PowerShell terminal

Description:
In the latest version of VS Code, a large black rectangular block completely obscures the input line and cursor in the integrated PowerShell terminal. It renders the terminal unusable as I cannot see what I am typing.

Environment Details:

Terminal Profile: Windows PowerShell 5.1 / PowerShell 7

Open VS Code.

Launch a new Integrated Terminal using the default PowerShell profile.

Observe the input area. A massive black block covers the prompt and cursor.

Troubleshooting Steps I Have Already Taken (and failed to resolve the issue):
To isolate the issue, I have performed the following advanced troubleshooting steps:

GPU Acceleration: Changed terminal.integrated.gpuAcceleration to off and canvas. (Did not work)

Terminal Minimum Contrast Ratio: Set terminal.integrated.minimumContrastRatio to 1. (Did not work)

Cursor Settings: Reset cursor style and enabled blinking. (Did not work)

External Terminal Test: Opened external native Windows PowerShell (conhost.exe / Windows Terminal) -> Works perfectly with no black blocks.

Different Shell Test: Changed default profile to Command Prompt (CMD) within VS Code -> Works perfectly. 6. No Profile Test: Launched PowerShell with powershell -NoProfile -> Issue persists.

Conclusion:
Since external native PowerShell works flawlessly, and internal CMD works flawlessly, this strongly suggests a regression bug in the latest VS Code update related to xterm.js rendering, Chromium WebGL updates, or ConPTY's handling of PowerShell's ANSI escape sequences / PSReadLine module.

Screenshots:
canonical: The integrated PowerShell terminal is rendering a large black block over the prompt and cursor, making input unreadable because of a regression in terminal rendering or PowerShell/ConPTY text handling.

title: Agent host: support GitHub Enterprise (GHE) for Copilot auth
document:
Agent host: support GitHub Enterprise (GHE) for Copilot auth

Today the agent host's Copilot auth path is hardcoded to github.com end-to-end. To use Copilot from a GHE instance, two layers need to change in lockstep:

## 1. Agent host: protected-resource metadata is github.com-only

The Copilot agent's `getProtectedResources()` only advertises github.com:

This drives which OAuth server the CLI / client performs the device-code flow against. For a GHE user it needs to advertise `https:///api/v3` (or equivalent) and `https:///login/oauth`. The GHE host should be **configurable by the agent host operator** (config file / env / launch flag) so a single agent-host binary can be pointed at any GHE instance without a rebuild.

## 2. `CopilotApiService`: token mint URL is hardcoded to `defaultChatAgent.tokenEntitlementUrl`

See the TODO in `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/shared/copilotApiService.ts` (`_buildCapiInit`):

For GHE this needs to point at `api./copilot_internal/v2/token` instead of `api.github.com/...`. The enterprise host has to thread through the public `ICopilotApiService` surface (extra param on `messages`/`models`, a per-host instance, or a `create(enterpriseHost?)` factory) — `defaultAccount.ts` in the workbench already derives this URL the same way for the main auth path and is a good reference.

## Why this is one issue

(1) and (2) are coupled — fixing only the mint URL doesn't help if the OAuth flow still hits github.com, and vice versa. Both should land together with a shared "GHE host" configuration source.

## Out of scope

- The Copilot CLI's own OAuth handling (Rust, in `cli/`) is github.com-hardcoded but is a separate consumer; this issue is specifically about the agent host being usable against a GHE Copilot.

Co-authored-by: Copilot
canonical: The agent host cannot authenticate Copilot against a GitHub Enterprise instance because both the OAuth resource metadata and the token mint URL are hardcoded to github.com instead of using a configurable enterprise host.

title: Allow Claude Agent to run through Anthropic subscription instead of Copilot quota
document:
Allow Claude Agent to run through Anthropic subscription instead of Copilot quota

I would like VS Code to support using **Claude Agent (Preview)** directly through an **Anthropic / Claude subscription or Anthropic API credentials**, instead of only consuming the user’s **GitHub Copilot subscription**.

This feature request comes from a real misunderstanding I had while using the feature. Since Claude Agent is described as using the official Anthropic agent harness / Claude Agent SDK, I initially assumed that the Claude usage would be tied to my existing Claude/Anthropic subscription, or at least that there would be a way to use it directly through Anthropic.

The confusion mainly came from the agent/model selector, where seeing Claude Agent listed made me assume that it could be used through my Claude/Anthropic subscription.

Because I misunderstood this billing model, I ended up spending around **$300 extra in requests in a single week**. This is not meant as a complaint, but as context for why making the billing/authentication model more explicit, or supporting direct Anthropic usage, would be valuable.

Ideally, Claude Agent could offer a provider/authentication option such as:

- Use via GitHub Copilot subscription
- Use via Anthropic / Claude subscription
- Use via Anthropic API key or Claude Code authentication

This would allow users to choose explicitly how Claude Agent usage is authenticated and billed.

If supporting direct Anthropic authentication inside the existing Claude Agent option is not desirable, I would also see it as a viable alternative to add a separate option to the current agent/model selector, clearly indicating that this version runs through the user’s Anthropic/Claude subscription instead of Copilot.

For example, the selector could include two clearly separated entries:

- Claude Agent — via Copilot subscription
- Claude Agent — via Anthropic subscription

This would make the billing model explicit and avoid confusion, while still preserving the current Copilot-based integration as the default or recommended option.

From a user perspective, this seems potentially feasible, since the existing Claude extension for VS Code already allows users to work with Claude through their own Claude/Anthropic access. I understand there may be product, technical, or legal considerations on the VS Code/Copilot side, but having a similar option in the integrated chat/agent selector would be much more convenient.

The main benefit would be that developers who already use Claude or Claude Code could use the full Claude Agent SDK experience inside VS Code without consuming Copilot quota or premium requests. It would also make the integration more flexible and clearer, especially for users who want to keep Copilot and Anthropic usage separate.

Even if direct Anthropic authentication is not planned, it would be very helpful to make the billing/usage model more explicit in the UI, so users do not mistakenly assume that Claude Agent usage is covered by their Claude subscription.
canonical: Claude Agent in VS Code is misleadingly presented as a Copilot-backed feature, causing users to think it can be used with an Anthropic subscription when it only consumes Copilot quota, and the confusion is compounded by the UI not clearly separating the billing/authentication model.

title: Feature Request: Improve VSCode Speech Input Accuracy and Polishing for Specialized Terms and Conversational Expressions
document:
Feature Request: Improve VSCode Speech Input Accuracy and Polishing for Specialized Terms and Conversational Expressions

### **Description**
The vscode speech input functionality currently struggles with:
1. **Inaccurate Recognition of Proper Nouns and Specialized Terms**  
   When mixing two or more languages in speech, ‘claude code’ is often recognized as ‘cloud code’, and ‘microsoft foundry’ as ‘microsoft factory’.
2. **Overly Conversational Transcriptions**  
   Speech-to-text inputs include filler words such as "um" and "ah", resulting in:
   - Excessive invalid tokens in the context.
   - The final transcription being verbose and lacking precision.

---

### **Proposed Feature Enhancement**
Introduce a post-processing step to optimize and refine speech input before it is processed further. The feature should:
1. Automatically **clean up invalid or meaningless content**:
   - Remove filler words like “um” and “ah”.
   - Omit redundant or irrelevant sections from the transcription.
2. **Reorganize and polish the input**:
   - Restructure conversational input into a concise, professional written format.
   - Summarize and distill the core meaning of the input.
3. Maybe consider **upgrading the speech recognition model**:
   - using the operating system’s built‑in ASR.
   - allowing users to configure local third‑party speech recognition engines.
 
---

### **Benefits**
- Enhanced accuracy and clarity of speech-to-text transcription.
- Reduced latency and token costs from irrelevant inputs.
- Improved collaboration with GitHub Copilot for contextual tasks.
- Better user experience for those relying on speech input.

---

### **Additional Context**
- This feature would greatly benefit users whose workflows depend on efficient and precise speech-to-text processes within VSCode.
- Supporting this enhancement could better assist developers who are accustomed to using voice interactions in their workflow.
canonical: VS Code speech input misrecognizes specialized proper nouns and includes filler words and other conversational noise instead of producing clean, concise text, likely because the current speech-to-text processing lacks domain-aware recognition and post-processing cleanup.

title: Security: minimumReleaseAge setting for mitigating supply chain attacks on extensions
document:
Security: minimumReleaseAge setting for mitigating supply chain attacks on extensions

In the last years, supply chain attacks have increased dramatically. A few examples in the VS Code extension ecosystem:

- [AI-Slop ransomware test sneaks on to VS Code marketplace - BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/ai-slop-ransomware-test-sneaks-on-to-vs-code-marketplace/)
- [Malicious VS Code AI Extensions With 1.5M Installs Found Exfiltrating Code - The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/01/malicious-vs-code-ai-extensions-with-15.html)
- [Fake Clawdbot VS Code Extension Installs ScreenConnect RAT - Aikido](https://www.aikido.dev/blog/fake-clawdbot-vscode-extension-malware)
- [Solidity Devs Targeted Again: Malicious VS Code Extension Drops ScreenConnect RAT - Checkmarx](https://checkmarx.com/zero-post/solidity-devs-targeted-again-malicious-vs-code-extension-drops-screenconnect-based-remote-access-trojan-rat/)
- [VS Code extensions contain trojan-laden image - ReversingLabs](https://www.reversinglabs.com/blog/malicious-vs-code-fake-image)
- [Malicious VSCode extensions on Microsoft's registry drop infostealers - BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/malicious-vscode-extensions-on-microsofts-registry-drop-infostealers/)
- [Malicious VSCode extensions infect Windows with cryptominers - BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/malicious-vscode-extensions-infect-windows-with-cryptominers/)
- [Nx Compromised to Steal Wallets and Credentials - Semgrep](https://semgrep.dev/blog/2025/security-alert-nx-compromised-to-steal-wallets-and-credentials)
- [Supply Chain Risk in VS Code Extension Marketplaces - Wiz](https://www.wiz.io/blog/supply-chain-risk-in-vscode-extension-marketplaces)
- [3,800 internal repos at GitHub breached via a malicious VS Code extension: why developer devices are the real target](https://www.aikido.dev/blog/github-breached-vs-code-extension)

Allow for `minimumReleaseAge`-style security config from other package managers / updaters, to require a minimum age on VS Code extensions before they are installed (also when installed via CLI, etc) when either:

1. Installing the latest version
2. Updating an extension manually
3. Updating an extension automatically

**Default:** Also, similar to the pnpm v11 note in Prior Art below, consider making a small delay the default (somewhere between 1 and 3 days), so that there is time for:

1. security scanners such as [Socket](https://socket.dev/) to find and report malicious extensions
2. VS Code Marketplace and OpenVSX to remove the malicious extensions

**Exclude mechanism:** There should be a way of excluding certain package names, package name patterns and publishers from the `minimumReleaseAge` setting, eg. like the pnpm [`minimumReleaseAgeExclude`](https://pnpm.io/settings#minimumreleaseageexclude) setting.

**Centralized management via enterprise policy:** Some users have commented that a centralized management via eg. GPO or MDM would be helpful:

> @SherwoodFold in [comment 4510110043](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/316867#issuecomment-4510110043): Additionally, this setting needs to be manageable by MDM profiles/configs so admins can actually manage their org's exposure.

@HendrikJan opened a similar issue https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/272765 which collected 12 upvotes. It was marked as a duplicate of https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/79689, but https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/79689 is not related to security (and is closed).

## Prior Art

- JavaScript / TypeScript package managers
 - [npm `min-release-age`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v11/using-npm/config#min-release-age)
 - [Yarn `npmMinimalAgeGate`](https://yarnpkg.com/configuration/yarnrc#npmMinimalAgeGate)
 - [pnpm `minimumReleaseAge`](https://pnpm.io/settings#minimumreleaseage)
 - [Bun `minimumReleaseAge`](https://bun.com/docs/runtime/bunfig#install-minimumreleaseage)
 - [Deno `--minimum-dependency-age`](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/reference/cli/install/)
- CI update bots / update tools
 - [Dependabot `cooldown`](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/reference/supply-chain-security/dependabot-options-reference#cooldown-)
 - [Renovate `minimumReleaseAge`](https://docs.renovatebot.com/key-concepts/minimum-release-age/)
 - [Snyk automatic dependency upgrade PR 21-day cooldown](https://docs.snyk.io/scan-with-snyk/pull-requests/snyk-pull-or-merge-requests/enable-automatic-upgrade-prs-for-new-dependency-upgrades#:~:text=Snyk%20does%20not%20recommend%20upgrades%20to%20versions%20that%20are%20less%20than%2021%20days%20old)
 - [npm-check-updates `--cooldown`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/npm-check-updates#cooldown)
- Other package managers
 - [pip `--uploaded-prior-to`](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/user_guide/#filtering-by-upload-time)
 - [uv `exclude-newer`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/reference/settings/#exclude-newer)
 - [Haskell Cabal `--index-state`](https://cabal.readthedocs.io/en/stable/cabal-commands.html#cmdoption-index-state) (only absolute timestamps)
 - [R / Posit Package Manager snapshot identifiers](https://docs.posit.co/rspm/admin/repositories.html#snapshot-identifiers) (only absolute dates)

A lot of these have appeared lately because of the supply chain attacks over the last years.

**Default:** pnpm v11 recently changed the default to 1-day `minimumReleaseAge` without any configuration:

> Supply-chain protection on by default. [`minimumReleaseAge`](https://pnpm.io/settings#minimumreleaseage) defaults to 1440 (1 day)

Source: https://pnpm.io/blog/releases/11.0
canonical: The extension installation and update flow lacks a configurable release-age delay and exclusion policy to block recently published extensions by default, leaving users and organizations exposed to supply-chain attacks.

title: Chronicle: SQLite store is unreliable on SSH-Remote workspaces
document:
Chronicle: SQLite store is unreliable on SSH-Remote workspaces

## Problem

The chronicle local store uses SQLite for persistence. On SSH-Remote workspaces (Linux), SQLite encounters cascading failures due to network filesystems not providing the POSIX locking guarantees that SQLite requires.

The failure pattern is:
1. Initial locking protocol errors or disk I/O errors
2. Escalates to `database disk image is malformed`
3. Then `file is not a database` (corruption is now permanent)
4. All subsequent flush operations fail until the database file is deleted

This disproportionately affects heavy users in remote development scenarios — exactly the cohort most reliant on session history.

## Observed error categories (flush operation)

- `database disk image is malformed` — persistent corruption
- `file is not a database` — corruption past recovery
- `locking protocol` — concurrent access on non-POSIX FS
- `cannot rollback - no transaction is active` — state desync
- `database is locked` — contention

## Suggested mitigations

1. **Detect remote filesystem** and switch to WAL mode or a non-SQLite fallback (e.g., append-only JSON log) for workspaces where POSIX locking is not guaranteed
2. **Auto-recover from corruption** — detect malformed DB at init and recreate (with optional backup of raw data)
3. **Add retry with backoff** for transient locking errors before failing the flush

## Environment

Primarily affects: Linux + SSH-Remote (vscode-server)
Also observed in smaller numbers: Linux + tunnel connections
canonical: Chronicle’s SQLite-backed local store can become corrupted and permanently fail on remote workspaces that do not provide reliable POSIX file locking, causing repeated flush failures until the database is recreated.

title: getDisplayMedia called at workbench boot → KDE screen-share dialog on every new window
document:
getDisplayMedia called at workbench boot → KDE screen-share dialog on every new window

1. Linux KDE Plasma 6 Wayland session, xdg-desktop-portal-kde running
2. Launch Code-Insiders fresh profile, no extensions:
`code-insiders --user-data-dir=/tmp/vsci-test --extensions-dir=/tmp/vsci-ext-empty`
3. Workbench finishes boot → KDE "Choose what to share" dialog appears

Expected: No screen-share prompt unless user explicitly invokes screen recording.

Actual: ScreenCast portal `CreateSession` + `SelectSources` + `Start` fired by main Electron process at workbench startup. dbus trace:

`sender=:1.X -> org.freedesktop.portal.ScreenCast.CreateSession
 session_handle_token="webrtc_session"`
`sender=:1.X -> org.freedesktop.portal.ScreenCast.SelectSources
 types=1 (monitor), cursor_mode=4, persist_mode=1`
`sender=:1.X -> org.freedesktop.portal.ScreenCast.Start`
Analysis:

Only `getDisplayMedia` caller in `workbench.desktop.main.js` is `RecordingService.startRecording` (used by `IssueReporterEditorPane` wizard).
Wizard's _onDidRequestStartRecording should only fire from captureStripRecordBtn.onDidClick, but fires at boot.
Bundle also present in `vs/sessions/sessions.desktop.main.js` with same pattern.
Tried `--disable-features=WebRTCPipeWireCapturer,WebRTCPipeWireCamera`, `--skip-sessions-welcome`, `--skip-welcome`, `--disable-extensions`, fresh `--user-data-dir` — none suppress.
Build:

xdg-desktop-portal-kde active
canonical: The application is incorrectly starting screen recording during workbench startup, which triggers the desktop share picker on every new window because the issue reporter recording flow is being invoked automatically instead of only when the user explicitly requests it.
