title: Semantic search / workspace indexing returns "Unauthorized" for Azure DevOps repo after successful Microsoft account sign-in
document:
Semantic search / workspace indexing returns "Unauthorized" for Azure DevOps repo after successful Microsoft account sign-in

## Environment
- VS Code version: 1.128.0 (commit fc3def6774c76082adf699d366f31a557ce5573f), arm64
- OS: macOS (Darwin 25.5.0)
- Repository host: Azure DevOps (visualstudio.com / dev.azure.com), work/org account (AAD)
- Copilot license: GitHub account with an org-provided Copilot Business subscription
- Semantic index auth: separate Microsoft account sign-in (AAD), triggered from within VS Code

## Description
Copilot's workspace-context semantic search feature fails to retrieve the indexing status for an Azure DevOps repository, logging repeated `Unauthorized` errors:

Per [the workspace-context docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agents/reference/workspace-context#_semantic-search), Azure DevOps repos are supported for remote indexing when signed in with a Microsoft account in VS Code. I confirmed:

- I use a GitHub account with an org-provided Copilot Business subscription as my Copilot license/identity.
- Clicking the Copilot icon in VS Code shows "Code Semantic Index - Not Authorized" with a "Sign in?" link.
- Clicking "Sign in?" prompts a separate Microsoft account sign-in flow (AAD, same tenant as the Azure DevOps organization) — screenshots of the full popup sequence attached.
- The sign-in completes successfully, but the three `Unauthorized` errors below appear in the log immediately afterward, and the semantic index status still does not resolve.
- Git operations against the same repo work correctly over HTTPS using Git Credential Manager with the same Microsoft account (OAuth flow), confirming my own repo permissions are valid at the git level.
- The Unauthorized errors persist even after fully restarting VS Code (not just reloading the window) and re-signing in.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Open a workspace backed by an Azure DevOps git repository, using GitHub Copilot (Business) as the license/identity.
2. Click the Copilot icon in VS Code; observe "Code Semantic Index - Not Authorized" with a "Sign in?" link.

3. Click "Sign in?" and complete the Microsoft (AAD) account sign-in flow for an account that has access to the Azure DevOps organization.

4. Observe repeated `Unauthorized` errors in the Copilot log for `CodeSearchChunkSearch::getIndexedStatus`, occurring immediately after the successful sign-in.

## Expected behavior
Indexing status should be retrievable and the repository should be indexed for semantic search, per the documented Azure DevOps support.

## Actual behavior
Every `getIndexedStatus` call fails with `Unauthorized`, and no index appears to be built.

## Additional notes
- This behavior is observed after updating to the latest VS Code release (1.128.0) — noting this in case it correlates with a recent change to the workspace-context/semantic-search or auth flow.
- Unsure whether this is a client-side auth/session issue or requires an organization-level policy to be enabled for Copilot code search on Azure DevOps — happy to provide more logs or org details if useful.
canonical: After a successful Microsoft account sign-in, Azure DevOps repositories still fail semantic search authorization and the workspace index status cannot be retrieved, likely because the indexing request is using the wrong or incomplete authentication context.

title: frontier_llm refusal for Claude Fable 5 results in an error
document:
frontier_llm refusal for Claude Fable 5 results in an error

With VSCode Copilot, when working on a repository about LLMs (such as https://github.com/iree-org/iree) and asking Claude Fable 5 a prompt, an error message appears and nothing is returned.

When looking at the log, this warning can be seen:

This warning is described here : https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/refusals-and-fallback
> The request could assist the development of competing AI models, which is restricted under Anthropic's commercial terms. Benign machine learning work can also trigger this category.

Instead of returning nothing, such request should either fallback to another model, or fail with a more explicit error message.

How to reproduce:

git clone https://github.com/iree-org/iree

Load the directory into VSCode

Ask Claude Fable 5 this : 
> I want to dive into IREE microkernel support. How does it work? Can I plug kernels written outside of IREE into it? Ideally, I would like to compile kernels from outside of IREE, pack them in a .so, and then use them from IREE.
>
> Please note that my target is an offload RISC-V CPU running on Linux ; we have a custom offload stack for communicating with that CPU (through PCI) and we also already have a HAL driver (not currently in this repo) for IREE. Through the offload stack we can dynamically send a .so from the host then load it.
canonical: Claude Fable 5 can trigger a refusal on legitimate machine-learning-related prompts and the client surfaces it as a vague error instead of falling back to another model or reporting a clearer refusal reason.

title: Tree View API: allow extensions to control item indentation/icon-twistie alignment (TreeViewOptions.indentation)
document:
Tree View API: allow extensions to control item indentation/icon-twistie alignment (TreeViewOptions.indentation)

### Summary

Custom `TreeView`s currently have no way to control how leaf-item icons align relative to sibling twisties. The built-in file Explorer makes a single, container-wide decision driven by the icon theme:

Custom views can't rely on that assumption because their collapsible items may individually have or lack icons. Any heuristic that infers alignment from sibling analysis (see #307350 and PR #308165) inevitably trades one inconsistency for another: aligning labels in one part of a view can misalign equal-depth items elsewhere in the same view. As discussed at length in #308165, this appears to be a *logical* limitation of inferring a global layout decision from per-item data, not an implementation bug.

### Proposal

Add an explicit opt-in on `TreeViewOptions` so the extension declares the intent:

- `undefined` — current default behavior (backward compatible)
- `'explorer'` — infer alignment from the active file icon theme exactly as the Explorer does (best for file-system-like trees)
- `boolean` — force icon/twistie alignment on or off, giving a fully consistent, icon-independent layout regardless of theme

(Exact shape — enum vs. mixed union — is open for design discussion; @n-gist suggested an enum may read cleaner.)

### Motivation / context

- Downstream bug: #307350 (sibling items shift when a collapsible item without an icon is added).
- PR #308165 improves the default heuristic but, as its review thread documents, cannot fully satisfy "an item's icon should never affect its siblings" while also matching Explorer behavior — the two goals are mutually exclusive for mixed-icon custom trees.
- @n-gist has done extensive testing (with a reusable test-extension kit) and is willing to stay involved in the API design and validation.

Happy to implement this behind a proposed API (`treeViewIndentation`) if the team is open to the direction. Flagging for guidance on the API shape first, per the proposed-API process.
canonical: Tree view indentation is currently inferred globally from item icons, which causes sibling items in custom trees to shift or misalign when some collapsible items have icons and others do not, and the API needs an explicit setting to control icon and twistie alignment per view.

title: Reduce the Frequency of Stable VS Code Releases
document:
Reduce the Frequency of Stable VS Code Releases

Thank you for all the work that goes into VS Code.

I would like to express my frustration with the current release cadence. Stable updates are arriving so frequently that they have become an interruption rather than a benefit. It feels like there is another update and another set of release notes almost every time I open the editor.

I understand that rapid development is valuable, but many users simply want a stable development environment without constant prompts to update. I would much rather receive fewer, more substantial releases than a continuous stream of incremental ones.

Please consider slowing the stable release cadence or providing an official "slow" update channel for users who prefer stability over receiving every feature as soon as it is available.

At the current pace, the update frequency feels excessive.
canonical: The problem is that stable releases are being published too frequently, causing constant update interruptions and prompting users to want a slower release channel.

title: Custom chat model settings collide when multiple providers expose the same model id
document:
Custom chat model settings collide when multiple providers expose the same model id

# Custom chat model settings collide when multiple providers expose the same model id

## Description

When configuring multiple custom chat language model providers, model-specific settings appear to be keyed only by the model `id`. This causes settings to collide when different providers expose models with the same `id`.

For example, I have two custom providers. Both define a model with `"id": "gpt-5.5"`.

Provider A:

Provider B:

After selecting `reasoningEffort = high` for Provider A's `gpt-5.5` model, VS Code writes the setting under:

However, if I then select Provider B's `gpt-5.5` model and change `reasoningEffort` to `low`, the existing setting for Provider A is modified instead of creating or updating a setting scoped to Provider B.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure multiple custom chat language model providers.
2. In both providers, define a model with the same `id`, for example `"gpt-5.5"`.
3. Select the model from Provider A and set `reasoningEffort` to `high`.
4. Select the model from Provider B and set `reasoningEffort` to `low`.
5. Inspect the persisted `chatLanguageModels.json` configuration.

## Actual Behavior

The persisted setting is keyed only by model id:

This makes the setting ambiguous and causes one provider's model setting to overwrite another provider's model setting when both models share the same `id`.

## Expected Behavior

Model settings should be scoped by a unique model identity that includes the provider identity, not only the model `id`.

For example, settings could be keyed by a provider-scoped identifier such as:

or stored inside each provider entry so that model settings are local to that provider.

## Why This Matters

It is common for different providers or custom endpoints to expose models with the same model id, especially OpenAI-compatible or gateway-style endpoints. The current behavior makes it unclear which provider/model is affected by a setting and can silently apply the wrong reasoning effort to another provider's model.

## Additional Notes

This appears to be caused by using only `model.id` as the settings key. Since `model.id` is not globally unique across providers, the configuration should probably use a provider-qualified key or persist settings under the corresponding provider object.
canonical: Model-specific chat settings are keyed only by model id, so when different providers expose the same model id they overwrite each other instead of being stored separately per provider.

title: Add smoke coverage for terminal sandbox temporary directory isolation
document:
Add smoke coverage for terminal sandbox temporary directory isolation

## Description

Add end-to-end smoke coverage for terminal sandbox temporary directory behavior on Linux and macOS.

The sandbox creates a custom temporary directory and exposes it through `$TMPDIR`. Commands running in the sandbox should be able to write there, while writes to the host OS temporary directory (for example `/tmp`) should remain blocked.

## Expected coverage

- Verify `echo test > "$TMPDIR/test.txt"` succeeds.
- Verify an equivalent write to the host OS temporary directory does not create a file.
- Run the probes through the chat `run_in_terminal` tool so the complete terminal sandbox integration is exercised.

Duplicate of #325052
canonical: The terminal sandbox is not properly isolating temporary directory access, allowing or failing to block writes to the host temporary directory while still exposing its own temporary directory through the sandbox environment.

title: Feature Request: Official remote MCP server for core VS Code documentation (code.visualstudio.com)
document:
Feature Request: Official remote MCP server for core VS Code documentation (code.visualstudio.com)

### Description

Context & Current Limitation
Microsoft recently released the official remote Microsoft Learn MCP server ([https://learn.microsoft.com/api/mcp](https://learn.microsoft.com/api/mcp)), which allows external AI agents and clients to query general documentation over Streamable HTTP.

However, canonical VS Code documentation—specifically the core editor docs, configuration guides, and Extension API schemas—is hosted separately on code.visualstudio.com (backed by the microsoft/vscode-docs repository). Currently, developers building or using third-party MCP clients (such as external coding agents or CLI tools) must rely on community web scrapers or locally cloning markdown repositories to give their models accurate VS Code context.

Question / Feature Request
Now that Microsoft has released the official Microsoft Learn MCP server ([learn.microsoft.com/api/mcp](https://learn.microsoft.com/api/mcp)) for querying general documentation, is there an official remote MCP endpoint available or planned specifically for core VS Code documentation ([code.visualstudio.com/docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs)) and extension API guides?

### Issue Details

Item|Value
---|---
Issue Category|Feature Request
Target|Visual Studio Code
VS Code Version|Code 1.127.0 (4fe60c8b1cdac1c4c174f2fb180d0d758272d713, 2026-06-30T10:52:33+02:00)
OS Version|Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200
canonical: There is no official remote MCP endpoint for core VS Code documentation yet, so external AI tools must still rely on unofficial scrapers or local docs, likely because the docs are hosted separately from the new Microsoft Learn MCP service.

title: Rendering Fail After Zoom+Scroll
document:
Rendering Fail After Zoom+Scroll

Reproduction Steps

1. Enable `editor.experimentalGpuAcceleration`.
2. Open a relatively large source file (mine is approximately 700 lines).
3. Use Ctrl + Mouse Wheel to zoom in or out.
4. Scroll through the file repeatedly, changing the zoom level occasionally.
5. After some time, portions of the editor begin rendering incorrectly.

Actual Result

* Large rectangular sections of the editor become completely black.
* Multi-line inline AI suggestions are also affected; instead of rendering the suggested text, a solid black rectangle is drawn over both the suggestion and the underlying code.
* Once the issue starts, the Developer Console is repeatedly flooded with:

 * `Attempt to create a texture atlas page past the limit 16`

Expected Result

* The editor should continue rendering correctly regardless of scrolling or zoom level.
* Inline suggestions should render normally.
* No texture atlas errors should be logged.

Additional Information

* Setting `editor.experimentalGpuAcceleration` to **Off** immediately resolves the issue.
* I have not been able to reproduce the problem with GPU acceleration disabled.
* The issue appears to be related to glyph texture atlas exhaustion, based on the repeated "Attempt to create a texture atlas page past the limit 16" errors.
canonical: GPU-accelerated editor rendering can break after repeated zooming and scrolling in large files because the glyph texture atlas runs out of pages, causing black rectangles to replace parts of the editor and inline suggestions.

Dropping a URL into the editor from Firefox requires holding shift

- drag a url into this editor from Firefox's address bar (without holding shift)

Expected:

Actual behaviour:

- on hover with the URL, the editor displays "hold shift to drop into editor" (like when dragging a file)
- dropping without shift does nothing
- dropping with shift pastes the url

This does not happen when dragging from Chrome (it inserts a markdown link without the need for shift). On Linux, it seems fine as well, it just pastes without shift (tested with same Code version, 1.127.0). 
Tested with Firefox 152.0.4 and 152.0.5, since it updated while writing this.

Extensions: none
canonical: Dragging a URL from Firefox into the editor is being treated like a file drop that wrongly requires holding Shift instead of inserting the URL normally, likely because the drop source is not recognized as plain text or link data.

title: `list.find` doesn't match by file's directory path in reference view
document:
`list.find` doesn't match by file's directory path in reference view

Yes.

- VS Code Version: 1.128.0
- OS Version: Linux Mint 22.3 (Ubuntu noble, kernel 6.8)

1. Trigger the reference view on a symbol by these commands:
 - `editor.action.referenceSearch.trigger`
 - `editor.action.goToImplementation` 
2. Press F3 (`list.find`)
3. Try to match by the files' directory path.

In the views above, I can only match by two criteria:
 1. The filename which are all the same, **`servers.go`** in this case
 - _unhelpful_
 2. Whatever text becomes visible under the expanded list entry
 - _unhelpful_, since just like the filename, they're usually the same function signature or the type header

So the most differentiating factor in reference view is usually the directory path which is the package/module name; not the filename and not the visible text under the expanded entry.
canonical: The reference view search does not match items by their parent directory path, making it hard to distinguish entries with the same filename or visible text.

4. 🐛 When I then later come back to this session the model picker shows model B pre-selected
canonical: Starting a rubber duck review switches the session’s selected model to the review model instead of preserving the original session model, so returning to the session shows the wrong model preselected.

title: MCP tools and vscode.lm.registerTool tools silently dropped in Agent mode on Remote SSH host (regression of #317992, still broken in 1.128)
document:
MCP tools and vscode.lm.registerTool tools silently dropped in Agent mode on Remote SSH host (regression of #317992, still broken in 1.128)

## MCP Tools and Extension-Registered Tools Not Injected into Agent Session on Remote SSH (VS Code 1.128 — Regression of #317992)

**Type:** Bug

---

### Description

On VS Code **1.128.0**, both MCP server tools and tools registered via `vscode.lm.registerTool` are silently absent from the Copilot Agent session when running under a **Remote SSH** extension host. The servers start successfully, tools are discovered, but none are injected into the agent context.

This appears to be a regression — issue #317992 was marked as fixed in 1.126, but the problem persists on 1.128.

---

### Affected Tool Sources

| Tool Source | Type | Works? |
|---|---|---|
| `github/github-mcp-server` (Docker stdio, remote ext host) | MCP | ❌ |
| `sehejjain.lsp-mcp-bridge` (via `vscode.lm.registerTool`) | Extension API | ❌ |
| `context7` MCP (local extension host) | MCP | ✅ |

The common factor for the broken tools is that they are both **spawned from / registered in the remote extension host** (Remote SSH). The working tool (context7) runs on the **local extension host**.

---

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Connect to a remote machine via **Remote SSH**
2. Configure an MCP server in workspace/remote settings (e.g. GitHub MCP via Docker stdio):

3. Install an extension that registers tools via `vscode.lm.registerTool` on the remote (e.g. `sehejjain.lsp-mcp-bridge`)
4. Open Copilot Chat in **Agent mode**
5. Observe that neither the MCP tools nor the extension-registered tools appear in the agent session

---

### Expected Behaviour

All discovered/registered tools from the remote extension host should be injected into the agent session, as they were prior to 1.126.

---

### Actual Behaviour

- MCP server starts correctly, 38 tools discovered (confirmed in output log)
- Extension-registered tools visible in Tool Configuration UI
- **Neither set of tools appear in the agent debug tool list**
- A locally-hosted MCP server (context7) works correctly in the same session

Server log confirms healthy startup:

Agent debug view shows **54 built-in tools only** — no `github/*` or LSP tools present.

---

### Attempted Workarounds

- `"github.copilot.chat.virtualTools.threshold": 0` — no effect
- Developer: Reload Window — no effect
- Tools picker shows toolset enabled with checkmark — no effect

---

### Environment

- **VS Code:** 1.128.0 (`fc3def6774c76082adf699d366f31a557ce5573f`, 2026-07-07)
- **Remote OS:** Linux x64 6.8.0-90-generic (Intel Xeon, 8 CPUs, 31GB RAM, ~40% VM)
- **Local OS:** Windows 11 ARM64 (Snapdragon X Elite, 12 CPUs, 31.56GB RAM)
- **Remote extension:** Remote - SSH `0.124.0`
- **GitHub Copilot Chat:** (see extensions list)
- **Relevant extensions on remote:** `sehejjain.lsp-mcp-bridge` 1.0.1, `github.vscode-pull-request-github` 0.156.0

---

### Related Issues

- #317992 — marked fixed in 1.126, appears incomplete for Remote SSH configuration

Remote OS version: Linux x64 6.8.0-90-generic
canonical: Tool servers and extension-registered tools created on a remote SSH extension host are discovered but not injected into Copilot Agent sessions, likely due to a regression in remote tool propagation.

title: MCP tool names collide when multiple servers report the same serverInfo.name, producing unstable _2/_3 prefixes
document:
MCP tool names collide when multiple servers report the same serverInfo.name, producing unstable _2/_3 prefixes

- VS Code Version: 1.127.0
- OS Version: Windows 11

1. In `mcp.json`, configure two or more stdio MCP servers under **distinct keys** (e.g. `svc-a`, `svc-b`) that each launch a backend which reports an **identical** `serverInfo.name` in its `initialize` response. Real-world example: the [Azure DevOps MCP server](https://github.com/microsoft/azure-devops-mcp), run as one instance per organization — every instance reports `serverInfo.name = "AzureDevOps.Mcp"` regardless of which organization it targets.
2. Reload the window and let VS Code discover tools.
3. Observe the exposed tool names: `mcp_azuredevops_2_*`, `mcp_azuredevops_3_*`, … `mcp_azuredevops_m_*` — **not** `mcp_svc_a_*` / `mcp_svc_b_*`.
4. Reload again and note the `_N` suffix→server assignment can change; there is no config-readable way to know which suffix corresponds to which `mcp.json` entry.

---

### Summary

When several configured MCP servers (each under a **distinct key** in `mcp.json`) report the **same** `serverInfo.name`, VS Code disambiguates their tools with numeric/letter suffixes (`mcp__2_*`, `_3_*`, … `_m_*`). That suffix→server mapping is **not stable across reloads** and is **not derived from the unique `mcp.json` server key**, so an agent/client cannot reliably route a tool call to a specific server by tool name.

### Expected

Each server's tools get a **stable, self-describing** prefix derived from its unique `mcp.json` server key (which is guaranteed unique), giving every configured server its own predictable tool namespace regardless of what `serverInfo.name` the backend reports.

### Actual

The tool prefix is derived from the backend-reported `serverInfo.name`. When several servers report the same name, they collide and VS Code appends disambiguation suffixes (`_2`, `_3`, … `_m`) that are unstable across reloads. A client must **probe** each server (call a tool and inspect the response) to discover which suffix maps to which server — and a wrong guess can send a call (including a write) to the wrong backend instance.

### Impact

Any setup that runs multiple instances of the same MCP server — e.g. one per account/organization/tenant — cannot be addressed deterministically by tool name. This is common for multi-tenant backends. Agents that need to target a specific instance must add a probing step, and mis-routing risks silent no-ops against the wrong instance.

### Proposed fix

Derive the tool namespace/prefix from the unique `mcp.json` server **key** rather than the backend-reported `serverInfo.name` (or otherwise guarantee a stable, key-based per-server prefix even when `serverInfo.name` values collide).
canonical: When multiple configured MCP servers report the same backend name, VS Code assigns colliding tool names with unstable disambiguation suffixes instead of using a stable prefix from each server’s unique configuration key.

title: Feature Request: Add an interactive Inline Command History Search/Filter UI for Integrated Terminal
document:
Feature Request: Add an interactive Inline Command History Search/Filter UI for Integrated Terminal

### Type of Feature Request
Terminal UX / Productivity Improvement

### Description
Currently, searching through the terminal command history relies heavily on shell-specific shortcuts like `Ctrl + R` (reverse search) or cycling through previous commands using the Up/Down arrow keys. While this works, it can become inefficient and cluttered when dealing with hundreds of complex commands across multiple sessions.

I propose adding an interactive, user-friendly **Inline Command History Search / Filter** directly within the Integrated Terminal panel (potentially via a search icon or a dedicated keybinding like `Ctrl + Alt + H`). 

### Proposed Behavior
1. **Interactive Dropdown/Quick-Pick:** When triggered, a lightweight filter bar or Quick-Pick UI populates a searchable list of recently executed commands specific to that terminal profile (e.g., PowerShell, bash, zsh).
2. **Fuzzy Search:** Users can start typing a keyword (e.g., `npm`, `docker`), and the list instantly filters down using fuzzy matching.
3. **Quick Execute/Paste:** Selecting a command from the list can either paste it directly into the active prompt or execute it immediately upon pressing `Enter`.

### Why this is useful
This feature would significantly improve productivity for developers who repeatedly run complex or long-string commands (e.g., Docker runs, deep-nested scripts, multi-flag CLI commands) without needing to leave the keyboard or guess the exact syntax from memory. It bridges the gap between shell-level utilities and a modern IDE interface.

### Additional Context / Examples
Something similar to the current `Create New Terminal (With Profile...)` dropdown interface or the Command Palette layout, but dedicated purely to filtering and extracting the chronological history stream of the active terminal shell.
canonical: The integrated terminal lacks a built-in interactive, searchable history picker for filtering and reusing past commands, forcing users to rely on shell-specific history navigation instead.

title: UI performance degrades after opening Hover, Command Palette, or main menu
document:
UI performance degrades after opening Hover, Command Palette, or main menu

- VS Code Version: 1.128.0
- OS Version: Windows 11 26200.8655

1. Launch VS Code.
2. Open a relatively large or complex workspace (the issue is easier to observe on large projects or lower-performance machines).
3. Trigger one of the following UI components:
 - Hover (outside the editor)
 - Context Menu
 - Command Palette
4. Close the overlay UI component.
5. Continue interacting with the workbench, for example:
 - Scrolling the editor
 - Resizing panels

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e03dc1fd-07e0-454b-b024-ded15b2db136

Expected Result:

UI performance should remain consistent before and after opening these UI components.

Actual Result:

After one of these UI components has been opened once, subsequent workbench interactions become noticeably less smooth. Editor scrolling, layout changes, and other UI operations consistently drop frames and feel sluggish.

The issue is barely noticeable in an empty window, but becomes very obvious in larger workspaces or on lower-performance devices, where it significantly affects normal usability.

Notably, this does **not** appear to be triggered by every overlay. For example, editor overlays and the editor context menu do not seem to reproduce the issue.
canonical: Opening the hover, command palette, or main menu causes the workbench to remain in a slower rendering state afterward, making later interactions like scrolling or resizing consistently less smooth, especially in larger workspaces.

title: Feature Request: Chat conversation outline & turn navigation (like Ophel Atlas)
document:
Feature Request: Chat conversation outline & turn navigation (like Ophel Atlas)

## Problem Statement

When using Copilot Chat in **Agent mode** (or Edit mode), long conversations become extremely difficult to navigate:

### Pain Point 1: Uncontrollable auto-scroll
In Agent/Edit modes, the chat **constantly auto-scrolls to the bottom** whenever tools execute or responses stream. There is no setting to disable this behavior. The auto-scroll logic in the source code is hardcoded:

This means if you want to read the model's earlier reasoning while tools are running, you have to constantly fight the scroll. Manually scrolling up does trigger `_suppressAutoScroll` temporarily, but it resets on the next response.

### Pain Point 2: No way to navigate between conversation turns
Within a single long chat session, there is **no way** to quickly jump to a specific previous exchange. You can't:
- Jump to "the 3rd question I asked" or "the 5th response"
- See an outline/TOC of the conversation
- Navigate between turns with keyboard shortcuts (Alt+↑/Alt+↓ just switch between different sessions, not turns within a session)

The only navigation options are `Ctrl+F` (search) and `Ctrl+Home`/`Ctrl+End` (top/bottom), which are insufficient for long Agent sessions.

---

## Inspiration: Ophel Atlas Chrome Extension

The Chrome extension [Ophel Atlas](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/ai-chat-organizer-outline/lpcohdfbomkgepfladogodgeoppclakd) (4.9★, 9,000+ users) provides exactly this kind of functionality for web-based AI chats (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude):

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Real-time outline** | Auto-generated TOC from conversation, click to jump to any section |
| **Turn navigation** | Easily navigate between question/response pairs |
| **Position tracking** | Highlights current reading position so you know where you are |
| **Global search** | Search across all sessions, prompts, and settings |
| **Session organization** | Folders, tags, pinning for managing many sessions |

---

## Proposed Solution for VS Code Copilot Chat

### Minimum Viable (Quick Wins)
1. **Add a `chat.scrollToBottom` setting** with options: `"always"`, `"ifAtBottom"`, `"never"` — so users can control auto-scroll behavior
2. **Add commands to navigate between turns** within the current session (not between sessions):
 - `workbench.action.chat.previousRequest` — jump to previous user request
 - `workbench.action.chat.nextRequest` — jump to next user request
 - `workbench.action.chat.previousResponse` — jump to previous agent response
 - `workbench.action.chat.nextResponse` — jump to next agent response

### Ideal (Full Solution)
3. **Conversation Outline sidebar** — an auto-generated table of contents showing all turns in the current session:
 - Each turn shown as a collapsible heading (user question / agent response)
 - Click to scroll to that position
 - Highlights current position
 - Could be integrated into the existing chat view or as a separate panel

### Nice to Have
4. Search across sessions, export conversations, session folders/tags

---

## Related Issues
- #304608 — "Prevent scroll jumping in agent chats" (open, labeled `ux` + `polish`, only 2 👍)
- #264602 — "Chat sessions editor should auto-scroll" (closed, related but opposite problem)

---

## Why This Matters

As AI-assisted development becomes mainstream, users are having **longer and more complex conversations** with Copilot Agent. A 20-turn debugging session is common. Without proper navigation tools, users waste significant time scrolling and searching — undermining the productivity gains that Copilot is supposed to provide.

This is not just "polish" — it's a core UX gap that affects every heavy Copilot user daily.
canonical: Copilot Chat in agent or edit mode always forces the view to the latest message and lacks navigation within a long conversation, making it hard to stop auto-scrolling and jump between earlier turns.

title: Agent believes tools are disabled after usage budget runs out, and doesn't recover when budget is restored
document:
Agent believes tools are disabled after usage budget runs out, and doesn't recover when budget is restored

## What happened

During a long agent-mode session, my additional usage budget ran out mid-session. I did not change any tool or chat settings at any point. This happened several times over the session.

## Symptoms

1. When the budget ran out, the response stopped with a quota error.

2. After topping the budget back up and continuing, the agent stopped calling tools and told me they were disabled:

 > "The file-reading and terminal tools are showing as disabled again (same as earlier), so I can't start the work right now. It looks like tool availability is being toggled on your side — possibly a mode or settings switch in VS Code chat."

 > "I'm realizing the read tools are disabled, so I can't view the file content directly."

 > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are disabled right now so I can't rebuild or verify."

3. The agent did not retry tools on its own. It only resumed using them after I explicitly told it to try running tools again, and this repeated on each budget top-up.
canonical: After the usage budget is exhausted and later restored, the agent incorrectly treats tools as disabled and stops retrying them until prompted again.

title: Copilot Chat side-panel input font is too small and cannot be independently resized
document:
Copilot Chat side-panel input font is too small and cannot be independently resized

Title:
Copilot Chat side-panel input font is too small and cannot be independently resized

VS Code 1.125.1 on Linux (x64)
GitHub Copilot Chat installed

Problem:
The Copilot Chat input box in the right-side panel is too small to comfortably use. The text in the dialog/input box is much smaller than the editor text and is difficult to read.

What I expected:
I expected the chat input box font to be independently adjustable, or at least for the panel chat input to respect the configured chat font settings.

What happened:
- Changing chat.fontSize did not reliably enlarge the side-panel input box.
- Changing chat.editor.fontSize affects Chat-in-Editor mode, not the side-panel input.
- Changing window.zoomLevel enlarges the entire UI, which is too broad and makes the rest of the workspace less usable.
- The side-panel input remains too small compared with the rest of the interface.

Repro steps:
1. Open VS Code.
2. Open Copilot Chat in the right-side panel.
3. Try adjusting chat.fontSize and chat.editor.fontSize in settings.
4. Observe that the side-panel input remains too small or behaves inconsistently.
5. Try window.zoomLevel and observe that it affects the entire UI, not just the chat input.

Impact:
This makes the chat input uncomfortable or nearly unusable for long sessions and forces undesirable global UI zoom changes.

Environment:
- Linux
- VS Code 1.125.1
- Copilot Chat side panel
- Workspace-specific and user settings were tested
- .vscode/settings.json was also checked

Notes:
The issue appears to be that the side-panel chat input does not have an independently supported font-size control in this build.
canonical: The Copilot Chat side-panel input font is not properly controlled by its own chat font setting, leaving it too small unless the entire interface is globally zoomed.

Copilot Chat side-panel input font is too small and cannot be independently resized

Title:
Copilot Chat side-panel input font is too small and cannot be independently resized

VS Code 1.125.1 on Linux (x64)
GitHub Copilot Chat installed

Problem:
The Copilot Chat input box in the right-side panel is too small to comfortably use. The text in the dialog/input box is much smaller than the editor text and is difficult to read.

What I expected:
I expected the chat input box font to be independently adjustable, or at least for the panel chat input to respect the configured chat font settings.

What happened:
- Changing chat.fontSize did not reliably enlarge the side-panel input box.
- Changing chat.editor.fontSize affects Chat-in-Editor mode, not the side-panel input.
- Changing window.zoomLevel enlarges the entire UI, which is too broad and makes the rest of the workspace less usable.
- The side-panel input remains too small compared with the rest of the interface.

Repro steps:
1. Open VS Code.
2. Open Copilot Chat in the right-side panel.
3. Try adjusting chat.fontSize and chat.editor.fontSize in settings.
4. Observe that the side-panel input remains too small or behaves inconsistently.
5. Try window.zoomLevel and observe that it affects the entire UI, not just the chat input.

Impact:
This makes the chat input uncomfortable or nearly unusable for long sessions and forces undesirable global UI zoom changes.

Environment:
- Linux
- VS Code 1.125.1
- Copilot Chat side panel
- Workspace-specific and user settings were tested
- .vscode/settings.json was also checked

Notes:
The issue appears to be that the side-panel chat input does not have an independently supported font-size control in this build.
canonical: The Copilot Chat side-panel input text is too small and does not respond to chat font settings independently, so it cannot be resized without zooming the entire interface.

title: BYOK PDF support requires a hardcoded model family
document:
BYOK PDF support requires a hardcoded model family

VS Code 1.128 gates PDF attachment support through `modelSupportsPDFDocuments`, which falls back to an Anthropic/GPT family allowlist when no explicit endpoint capability exists.

For custom BYOK endpoints, the family is derived from the configured model ID. A compatible proxy or another model that accepts PDF documents therefore has to use an allowlisted-looking ID (or a global family capability override) to unlock PDFs. Family overrides affect many unrelated prompt and capability heuristics. Models outside the allowlist cannot advertise PDF support independently.

It would be useful for each language model endpoint to explicitly advertise accepted file MIME types, for example `fileInputMimeTypes: ['application/pdf']`, with the existing family check retained as a compatibility fallback.

#324960 fixes the separate Anthropic Messages API conversion bug but intentionally does not change capability advertisement or the public API surface. The broader approach was previously explored in #323836.
canonical: Custom BYOK model endpoints cannot independently advertise PDF attachment support and instead depend on a hardcoded family allowlist derived from the model ID, forcing incompatible capability overrides or allowlist-like IDs to enable PDFs.

title: Copilot Chat: All previous session history silently lost after upgrade (.json → .jsonl format migration not performed)
document:
Copilot Chat: All previous session history silently lost after upgrade (.json → .jsonl format migration not performed)

- VS Code Version: 1.127.0 (Universal)
- OS Version: macOS 26.5.1 (25F80)

## Bug Description

After upgrading VS Code, all previous Copilot Chat session history is completely
gone from the Chat History panel. No warning, no migration, no recovery option.

## Root Cause (Observed)

The Copilot Chat extension changed its chat session storage format from `.json` to
`.jsonl` between versions. Sessions in the old `.json` format still exist on disk at:

~/Library/Application Support/Code/User/workspaceStorage//chatSessions/.json

...but the new extension silently ignores them. They are never read, migrated, or
displayed in the UI.

Related: #307761 (chat history not loading from .jsonl files — different root cause, closed as not planned)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Use VS Code Copilot Chat for multiple sessions across several workspaces
2. Upgrade VS Code to a version that changed the session format to `.jsonl`
3. Open VS Code and check the Chat History panel
4. All prior sessions are gone

## Expected Behavior

One of:
- The extension **migrates** existing `.json` session files to `.jsonl` on first run after upgrade
- The extension **reads both formats** for backward compatibility
- Users are shown a **warning** before upgrading and offered an export/backup option

## Actual Behavior

Previous chat sessions vanish silently. No warning, no migration, no recovery UI.
Months of conversation context (debugging sessions, architectural decisions, code
explanations) is permanently lost.

## Impact

- Sessions represent significant complex work context that cannot easily be reconstructed
- The change was made without any migration path, user notice, or changelog callout

## Environment

- OS: macOS 26.5.1 (25F80)
- Old format: `.json` (files still present on disk, confirmed)
- New format: `.jsonl`

## Requested Fix

Add a one-time startup migration that converts `.json` chat sessions to `.jsonl`,
or at minimum add a "Restore legacy sessions" command to the Command Palette.
canonical: Copilot Chat loses all previously saved chat history after an upgrade because it stops reading the old session files and does not migrate them to the new storage format.

title: Agent believes tools are disabled after usage budget runs out, and doesn't recover when budget is restored
document:
Agent believes tools are disabled after usage budget runs out, and doesn't recover when budget is restored

## What happened

During a long agent-mode session (Copilot Chat, macOS), my additional usage budget ran out mid-session. I did not change any tool or chat settings at any point. This happened several times over the session.

## Symptoms

1. When the budget ran out, the response stopped with a quota error.

2. After topping the budget back up and continuing, the agent stopped calling tools and told me they were disabled:

 > "The file-reading and terminal tools are showing as disabled again (same as earlier), so I can't start the work right now. It looks like tool availability is being toggled on your side — possibly a mode or settings switch in VS Code chat."

 > "I'm realizing the read tools are disabled, so I can't view the file content directly."

 > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are disabled right now so I > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are disabled right now so I > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are disabled right now so I > "The edit is in, but up > "The edit t c > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are disabled right now so I > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are disabled right now so I > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are esp > "The thi > "The edit is in, but terminal/browser tools are disabled right-session, error blames the user — different trigger
EOF
)
canonical: The agent incorrectly treats tools as permanently disabled after a usage quota interruption and does not restore tool access when the quota is replenished, likely because the quota error leaves the session’s tool state stale.

title: Use QuickPick for the blocked sessions context view
document:
Use QuickPick for the blocked sessions context view

The blocked sessions context view should use the QuickPick UI instead of its current custom implementation.

Adopting the shared QuickPick primitive would reduce custom UI code and give us consistent styling, keyboard navigation, filtering, and accessibility behavior for free.

### Task
- Replace the custom blocked sessions context view with a QuickPick-based implementation.
canonical: The blocked sessions context view is still using a custom UI instead of the shared QuickPick component, causing inconsistent styling, keyboard navigation, filtering, and accessibility behavior.

title: Every VS Code update causes my extension tests to fail until I restart every vscode instance.
document:
Every VS Code update causes my extension tests to fail until I restart every vscode instance.

- VS Code Version: 1.128.0 (from 1.127.0)
- OS Version: Windows 11

1. Wait for a VS Code update to be released on 'stable', but don't install it (e.g Open VS Code is on 1.127.0)
2. Run some vscode tests using vscode-test CLI
3. Watch it fail to install VS Code for the test with "checkInnoSetupMutex" (e.g. as it tries and fails to install 1.128.0)

I would expect it to just work, given the installation of the vs code version for test is in a separate location.

I'm developing a VS Code extension, and this behaviour is very frustrating, especially given the recently increased release cadence of VS Code, given it suddenly breaks my workflow and forces me to re-start all of my sessions every week (and forces me to re-sign-in #322699)

Log:
canonical: A running instance of the editor holds a Windows installer mutex that blocks the test runner from installing the needed version, so extension tests fail until all editor sessions are restarted.

title: MCP auth re-validation drops `authorizationServer`/`resource`/`clientId`, causing wrong-authority token requests (and server teardown for B2B guests)
document:
MCP auth re-validation drops `authorizationServer`/`resource`/`clientId`, causing wrong-authority token requests (and server teardown for B2B guests)

**VS Code Version:** Root cause, patch, and controlled before/after traces (Evidence 1) are on Code - OSS Dev built from source at commit `0d2dfb2e` (Electron 42.2.0). The real-world cold-start teardown (Evidence 2) reproduces on both Stable (1.127.0) and Insiders (1.129.0-insider); the trace excerpt shown is from the Stable capture.
**OS Version:** Windows 25H2 (OS Build 26200.8457)
**Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?** No. The Microsoft Authentication extension and an Entra-protected MCP server are required.

_TL;DR: An app registration in tenant A lists VS Code as a trusted/authorized client application; users from tenant B are added as B2B guests. Authenticating to an MCP server via VS Code's built-in Entra auth support causes VS Code to stop the server immediately after the first successful sign-in, requiring a manual restart, after which it works fine._

## Summary

`MainThreadMcp._onDidChangeAuthSessions` re-validates every tracked, running MCP server whenever the core `AuthenticationService` fires `onDidChangeSessions` for a provider, regardless of why the event fired (for example a different, unrelated account being added or removed). The re-validation call passes `authorizationServer: undefined` and does not pass `clientId`/`resource`/`audience`/`clientSecret`, instead of the values used when the server's session was originally created and tracked.

For a server whose provider resolves the tenant from `authorizationServer` (an Entra ID tenant-specific authority), losing that context causes the silent token lookup to run against the `organizations` authority instead of the tenant GUID. Depending on the account/tenant relationship this either silently succeeds against the wrong authority (masking the bug) or fails outright, in which case the server is stopped with `"Authentication session for {0} removed, stopping server"`.

This report combines two captured observations of the same defect. Evidence 1 is a controlled before/after on a from-source build: the re-validation call drops `authorizationServer`, the silent lookup runs against `organizations`, and the patch restores the correct authority. In that setup MSAL's cache still returns a token, so the wrong authority is benign. Evidence 2 is a real-world capture (reproduced on both Stable 1.127.0 and Insiders 1.129.0) where the same wrong-authority silent call is not benign: for a single-tenant app with a B2B guest it fails with `AADSTS650059` and the server is torn down. Trace-level logging on the cold start shows the fatal silent call is the one that lost the tenant authority, while the surrounding calls that kept the correct authority succeeded. The teardown is observed; that the patch prevents it is inferred (see the note at the end of Evidence 2), not yet run end-to-end.

## What appears to be the root cause (code)

1. `src/vs/workbench/api/browser/mainThreadMcp.ts:71`: any session-list change for any provider invokes `_onDidChangeAuthSessions` with only `providerId`/`providerLabel`:

2. The three call sites that establish and track a session (`:384`, `:392`, `:441`) only pass `scopes` to the tracker, even though `authorizationServer`, `clientId`, `resource`, `audience`, `clientSecret` are all in scope (they were just used to obtain the session via `_getSessionForProvider(...)` at `:333`):

3. `McpServerAuthTracker` (`:644`) stores only `{ serverId, scopes }`.

4. The re-validation loop (`:470-501`) destructures only `{ serverId, scopes }` and passes `undefined` for `authorizationServer`:

5. In the Microsoft provider (`extensions/microsoft-authentication/src/common/scopeData.ts`), the tenant is derived from `authorizationServer` when present and falls back to `DEFAULT_TENANT = 'organizations'` otherwise. So the first session creation targets the tenant-specific authority, but re-validation targets `organizations`.

## Evidence 1: wrong-authority defect and patch (controlled before/after)

Setup: ExampleServer (C#, dotnet MCP SDK, HTTP), single-tenant app, tenant `11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111`, scope `api://33333333-3333-3333-3333-333333333333/user_impersonation`, Microsoft provider. With ExampleServer authenticated and running, signing in a second, unrelated account under a different scope set triggers `_onDidChangeAuthSessions` re-validation of ExampleServer's still-valid session.

Unpatched, re-validation requests the wrong authority:

The original session was created against the tenant authority (`.../11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111`). In this account/tenant combination MSAL's cache still returned a valid token, so no visible failure occurred, but the authority sent to Entra is provably wrong.

Patched (diff below), identical test, authority is preserved:

("and resource" confirms `resource` is now carried through as well.)

## Evidence 2: real-world hard failure outcome with Trace, cold start, single-tenant B2B guest

A separate deployment reaches the failing branch of the same defect. The app registration is single-tenant in tenant A and lists VS Code's client ID as a trusted/authorized client application; the user signs in with a native tenant-B account resolved to a B2B guest in tenant A. On cold start (from VS Code, not Azure Container App cold-start) the MCP server is stopped by VS Code.

Trace-level logging of the Microsoft Authentication extension for a single cold start (MCP resource scope `api:///user_impersonation`, guest account ``, correct tenant-A authority ``) shows two different authorities used for the same resource within the same sequence.

Correct tenant authority, with resource, succeeds (representative; occurs repeatedly, including immediately before the fatal call):

Fatal call, authority falls back to `organizations`, resource dropped (note the trace line lacks "and resource"):

MCP server log, about one second later:

The fatal silent call is the one that lost the tenant authority (`organizations` instead of ``) and the resource, and it fails with AADSTS650059 because the app is single-tenant and cannot be reached via `organizations`. The immediately preceding calls, which kept the correct authority and resource, were returning tokens successfully. Restoring the tracked `authorizationServer`/`resource` on re-validation (the patch below) would keep this call on the tenant-specific authority, where it was already working, so the teardown would not occur.

(The Evidence 2 cold-start traces read "with force refresh", while the Evidence 1 account-change re-validation reads "without force refresh". This is a genuine difference between the two code paths, cold-start validation versus account-change re-validation, not a copy/paste artifact between runs.)

This is reproducible across sessions and days. In one case the server authenticated and discovered all tools, then was torn down with the same message a few minutes later.

Note on the scope of the fix: the patch is proven to correct the dropped-authority behavior and to preserve the tenant authority on re-validation (Evidence 1). Evidence 2 shows the teardown-triggering call is exactly that dropped-authority call, so the patch is expected to prevent the teardown, but this has not yet been verified end-to-end by running the patched build against the single-tenant B2B-guest setup.

## Expected behavior

Re-validation triggered by an unrelated auth-session change should reuse the `authorizationServer`/`resource`/`clientId` the server's session was created with, so the silent token request targets the correct tenant authority. A silent-acquisition miss for a B2B guest should not tear down an otherwise-working MCP connection with no actionable prompt.

## Steps to reproduce

Wrong-authority defect (Evidence 1):

1. Connect and authenticate an Entra-protected HTTP MCP server whose resource metadata yields a tenant-specific `authorizationServer`.
2. With it running, sign a second, unrelated Microsoft account into the same provider under a different scope set.
3. Observe re-validation of the first server's session request the `organizations` authority instead of the original tenant authority.

Hard-failure teardown (Evidence 2):

1. Configure the app registration as single-tenant in tenant A.
2. Sign in with a native tenant-B account that is a B2B guest in tenant A.
3. Connect the MCP server and confirm it works (interactive auth succeeds, tools are discovered).
4. Trigger a cold start with no warm cached token.
5. Observe (at Trace log level for the Microsoft Authentication extension) a silent call for the resource scope fall back to the `organizations` authority and fail with AADSTS650059, followed by the MCP server being stopped.

## Patch

Applied against commit `0d2dfb2e`, `src/vs/workbench/api/browser/mainThreadMcp.ts`. The tracker caches the `ISecretStorageService` lookup key rather than the client secret value, so a secret rotated via the "Set Client Secret" code lens is re-read fresh on each re-validation instead of replaying a stale value. `URI` and `ISecretStorageService` are already imported/injected; compiles clean (`npm run compile`, 0 errors).

## Relation to existing issues

Related to #283453 (MCP + Entra authority resolution), which is an authority-string configuration case rather than tracked context being dropped on re-validation.
canonical: MCP auth re-validation is recreating tracked server sessions with missing authority and client context, causing silent token requests to fall back to the generic authority and sometimes stop the server for guest users instead of reusing the original tenant-specific authorization details.

title: GitHub Copilot Chat – Multiple bugs with third-party Claude model integration (VS Code)
document:
GitHub Copilot Chat – Multiple bugs with third-party Claude model integration (VS Code)

Subject: GitHub Copilot Chat – Multiple bugs with third-party Claude model integration (VS Code)

Summary:
Over the course of a single day, I encountered a series of related bugs when using Claude models (via GitHub Copilot Chat / Copilot's third-party Claude agent integration) in VS Code. These appear to be distinct but related failures in Copilot's Claude integration layer, not issues with my configuration.

Environment:
- Editor: VS Code
- Extension: GitHub Copilot Chat
- Subscription: GitHub Copilot (no separate Anthropic/Claude subscription)
- Model(s) affected: Claude (Opus/Sonnet, exact version selected via Copilot's model picker)

Issues encountered (in order):

1. Chat stuck indefinitely on "Evaluating" / "Analyzing"
 Requests would hang indefinitely at the "Evaluating" or "Analyzing" stage with no response and no error message. This occurred consistently across all requests, not just complex ones.
 Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/312526
 Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/311840

2. 400 error: "thinking.type.enabled" is not supported for this model
 Every request to the Claude model failed immediately with:
 "thinking.type.enabled" is not supported for this model. Use "thinking.type.adaptive" and "output_config.effort" to control thinking behavior.
 This indicates Copilot's Claude integration is sending a deprecated API parameter format that the current model no longer accepts. This is a client-side compatibility bug, not something a user can fix via settings — there is no UI option in Copilot to control this parameter.
 Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/312290
 Related: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/49219
 Related: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/51439
 Related: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/63546

3. "Error: Error during execution"
 After working around issue #2, subsequent requests to Claude models failed with a generic "Error during execution" message, while the same prompts worked fine with GPT-based models.
 Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/303965
 Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/300226

4. Chat session history lost when using Claude (third-party) models
 Sessions using Claude via Copilot are not reliably preserved in the Sessions panel — reopening a session that previously used Claude causes the agent to lose all prior context and reprocess/re-read the entire project from scratch, effectively discarding the conversation history.
 Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/295745

Workaround applied:
Enabling the following user settings routed Claude requests through VS Code's newer "Agent Host" subsystem instead of Copilot's own proxy layer, which resolved issue #2 (the thinking.type.enabled error):

"chat.agentHost.enabled": true,
"chat.editor.claude.preferAgentHost": true,
"chat.agents.claude.preferAgentHost": true

However, this setting change itself caused all pre-existing chat sessions to be invalidated, forcing a full restart of context for ongoing work (related to issue #4 above).

Impact:
- Claude models were completely unusable via Copilot for most of a working day.
- Multiple hours of lost productivity due to repeated failures across different error types.
- No official acknowledgment or fix timeline found on any of the linked issues as of this writing.

Request:
Given the number and severity of these issues, could you please:
1. Confirm whether these are known/tracked issues on your end.
2. Provide a timeline or status update on fixes for the thinking.type.enabled parameter mismatch (issue #2), as this makes Claude models unusable in Copilot entirely.
3. Advise on preventing chat session/context loss when using Claude models (issue #4).

Happy to provide additional logs or reproduction steps if helpful.
canonical: GitHub Copilot Chat’s Claude integration is sending incompatible request parameters and handling sessions incorrectly, causing Claude requests to fail, hang, or lose chat history.

title: Copilot Agent built-in list_dir returns unbounded output for large directories and freezes/poisons the chat session
document:
Copilot Agent built-in list_dir returns unbounded output for large directories and freezes/poisons the chat session

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: GitHub Copilot Chat built-in extension version `0.52.0`
- VS Code Version: `1.124.2`
- VS Code Commit: `6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c`
- VS Code Architecture: `arm64`
- OS Version:
 - Local client: macOS / Darwin `24.1.0` arm64
 - Remote tunnel host: Rocky Linux 9.6 / Linux
- Remote setup: VS Code connected to a remote machine through a VS Code Tunnel
- Feature: Agent mode, built-in filesystem tool `list_dir`
- Selected model: GPT-5.5, BYOK
- Logs: attached below

## Summary

Copilot Agent's built-in `list_dir` tool appears to return an unbounded directory listing for very large directories. If the directory contains tens of thousands of files, the full list is inserted into the chat/tool history. After that, the chat session becomes extremely slow or frozen. Reloading the window temporarily recovers the UI, but continuing the same contaminated chat can reproduce the slowdown, which continues even after tools are later disabled, suggesting the oversized `list_dir` result has already poisoned the chat context.

This is problematic in workspaces where dataset directories may contain tens or hundreds of thousands of files, and Agent mode may choose `list_dir`.

## Concrete case

The directory that triggered this contains 87,348 entries. Agent debug view showed a built-in tool call like:

The response contained a very large list of filenames like:

After this, the chat and the window became extremely slow. UI animations still ran, but actions requiring server or extension-host communication stalled and then occasionally flushed all at once.

1. Open a VS Code workspace through a Remote Tunnel
2. Have a dataset directory visible/readable to Agent with ~100k files
3. Ask Agent to start work involving the dataset
4. Agent autonomously calls the built-in list_dir tool on the large dataset folder
5. The list_dir response injects the large directory listing into chat context
6. The chat session becomes extremely slow and/or freezes. The bad performance persists even after stopping the chat. Reloading window fixes the freeze.

The issue also persisted in the old chat even when I later disabled all tools, suggesting the oversized context contaminating the session.

## Relevant log excerpts

Before identifying the exact list_dir problem, the affected chat showed large Agent rendering/tool/context setup:

After disabling tools, the old contaminated chat was still slow and still involved history rendering:

In contrast, a new chat without the contaminated tool history responded normally.
canonical: The built-in directory listing tool returns the full contents of very large folders without pagination or truncation, which can overwhelm the chat context and freeze or poison the session, likely because the huge result is retained in history.

title: UX: Inability to switch between Microsoft and GitHub accounts without first turning off Settings Sync is highly unintuitive
document:
UX: Inability to switch between Microsoft and GitHub accounts without first turning off Settings Sync is highly unintuitive

### Description

The current account switching flow in VS Code is extremely unintuitive and provides no clear affordance for users who want to switch between a GitHub account and a Microsoft account.

### Steps to Reproduce

Initially sign in to VS Code and turn on Settings Sync using a GitHub account.

Later, decide to switch to a Microsoft account for synchronization.

Click the Accounts icon in the bottom-left corner and select "Sign Out".

Click "Sign in to Sync Settings" again.

### Actual Result

VS Code automatically redirects to the previously used GitHub OAuth flow without ever presenting an option to choose a different account provider (Microsoft). The user is effectively locked into the first account type they chose.

### Expected Result

After signing out, users should be presented with a clear choice of which account provider (e.g., "Sign in with GitHub" or "Sign in with Microsoft") to use for the new session. The current requirement to know and execute the undocumented step of "Settings Sync: Turn Off" via the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) is a significant UX failure. A simple "Switch Account" option in the Accounts menu would be a much more intuitive solution.

### Workaround Found (Undocumented)

The only way to trigger the account provider selection is to:

Open Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P).

Run Settings Sync: Turn Off.

Click the Accounts icon again and select "Sign in to Sync Settings".

Only then does the UI present the option to choose between GitHub and Microsoft.

### Suggested Fix

Add a "Switch Account" or "Change Account Provider" option directly in the Accounts menu that does not require turning sync off.

Alternatively, always show the provider selection screen upon signing out and attempting to sign back in.

### Related Issues

This appears to be a recurring pain point for many users, as evidenced by similar reports:

#192969: "Settings Sync" fails to switch from Github account to Microsoft account

#198868: Not able to delete/switch account (sign out Microsoft and then sign in GitHub)

#213851: Can't sync settings to Microsoft account after trying Github one

#213714: Cannot log into a different github account after signing into one

### VS Code Version

ALL Version

### OS Version

ALL OS
canonical: Signing out of Settings Sync does not properly clear the previous account/provider choice, so signing back in silently reuses the last used provider instead of offering a way to switch accounts.

title: Modernize: Add a compact mode option for smaller laptop screens
document:
Modernize: Add a compact mode option for smaller laptop screens

## Feedback

> I brought this up previously, but making more focussed feedback here. I would love an option for a 'compact' mode.
> The padding between the windows are great in my large monitor. But when I'm on my laptop, I feel like I am losing screen real-estate that I would choose to have over window padding. (it feels worse than it is because I go from like a wide screen to a 14 inch)

**Source**: #workbench-refresh-feedback — VB on 2026-06-26 ([Slack](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C0BCLPWGMJQ/p1782486168517709))

Related report — tyler on 2026-06-26 ([Slack](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C0BCLPWGMJQ/p1782516460985979)), 👍 ×1:

> just so much padding on my 16in mbp...

> **Thread — Lee Murray:** I have reduced the gaps from `12px` to `8px` — anything smaller & it starts to feel too cramped. Please try it out & let me know if it helps

---

## Context

The Modern UI window padding is well-received on large external monitors but costs valuable horizontal/vertical real estate on smaller laptop screens (14–16"). While default gaps were reduced from 12px to 8px, multiple users want an explicit, opt-in "compact" mode that trims inter-panel padding further for constrained displays without everyone self-hosting individual settings.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Provide an opt-in compact mode (single setting) that reduces Modern UI inter-panel padding for smaller screens
- [ ] Compact mode is discoverable and does not require manually tuning multiple individual settings
- [ ] Default (non-compact) spacing is unchanged
canonical: The Modern UI layout uses too much inter-panel padding on smaller laptop screens, and it needs an opt-in compact mode to reduce spacing without changing the default layout.

With the Modern UI padding increases, panel header actions can overflow at narrower widths / higher zoom levels, causing primary actions (e.g. the debug view Run/play button) to disappear. Rather than only reducing padding, this points to a need for responsive panel-header behavior that prioritizes key actions when horizontal space is constrained.

- [ ] The debug view play/Run action remains reachable at high zoom and narrow panel widths
- [ ] Panel header adopts responsive behavior (e.g. collapse/hide the title before dropping actions) when space is constrained

title: Utility model routing should follow the selected BYOK/local model, not only a global setting
document:
Utility model routing should follow the selected BYOK/local model, not only a global setting

I think the recent BYOK utility model change is fixing the wrong layer of the problem.

I understand why silent fallback to Copilot utility models is being changed. If a user selected a BYOK model, hidden Copilot usage can be surprising because it may cross a billing or data boundary. Making that explicit is reasonable.

But making utility model selection mostly global is not the right long-term shape.

One of the things I really like about the current local model support is how simple it can be. For example, with Ollama, I can just pick Ollama from the model/provider setup UI, select a local model, and it appears in VS Code. That simplicity is great. It makes local models feel like a first-class path.

The new utility-model behavior makes that path feel much worse.

A simple local model setup should be:

 choose Ollama as a local model provider
 select an Ollama model as the Agent model
 Agent mode works

It should not become:

 choose Ollama as a local model provider
 select an Ollama model as the Agent model
 start Agent mode
 fail because a separate global small utility model is not configured
 discover another setting
 configure another model
 deal with cold starts, VRAM pressure, or model unload/reload behavior

That failure mode is especially confusing because the user already selected a valid main model. From the user’s point of view, Agent mode should have everything it needs.

For local inference, a “small” utility model is not automatically cheap or fast. The real cost may be loading another model, moving VRAM around, or evicting the model that is already running. In many local setups, the best utility model is simply the currently selected main model.

That is why I think utility routing should follow the selected model or provider.

For example:

 local provider:
 utility model = self
 small utility model = self

 remote BYOK provider:
 utility model = self, provider-specific small model, or Copilot
 small utility model = self, provider-specific small model, or Copilot

 Copilot provider:
 utility model = Copilot utility model

The exact implementation does not matter to me. It could live in the Language Models UI, a provider profile, chatLanguageModels.json, or some other model configuration layer. The important part is that this should not be only one global setting.

A single global chat.utilitySmallModel cannot express normal workflows like:

 when I select a local model, reuse that selected model
 when I select a remote BYOK model, use that provider’s small model
 when I explicitly allow Copilot utility models, use Copilot utility models

Those are different model environments, not just different model names.

I think the default for Ollama/local-style setups should be “self”, or at least there should be an easy way for the provider/model entry to say “use the selected model for utility tasks”.

That preserves the simple local-model setup path that currently makes local/BYOK support feel good in VS Code.

The current global setting feels like a workaround for the immediate error, not the right abstraction.

Please consider making utility model routing configurable per model or per provider, so switching the selected Agent model also switches the effective utility routing.

Related:

- #324007
- #324570
- #324268
canonical: Utility model routing is incorrectly controlled by a single global setting instead of following the selected agent model or provider, causing valid local and BYOK setups to fail when a separate utility model is not configured.

title: Integrated browser scaling issue when tooltips are visible
document:
Integrated browser scaling issue when tooltips are visible

This issue can be reproduced in any JavaScript codebase that launches the integrated browser.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open the integrated browser.
2. Launch the browser's Developer Tools.
3. Dock the Developer Tools within VS Code so they appear beside the integrated browser.
4. Open a source file in split view alongside the integrated browser.
5. Hover over a function or method that displays a large documentation tooltip.

### Expected behavior

The integrated browser should remain the same size and position regardless of editor tooltips.

### Actual behavior

When hovering over a function or method with a sizeable documentation tooltip, the integrated browser unexpectedly scales up and shifts, causing it to overlap the Developer Tools. As soon as the cursor is moved away and the tooltip disappears, the integrated browser immediately returns to its normal size and position.
canonical: The integrated browser incorrectly resizes and shifts when a large editor tooltip appears, causing it to overlap nearby panels, likely because tooltip rendering affects the browser layout calculation.

title: Allow custom media preview extensions to hide built-in image preview actions
document:
Allow custom media preview extensions to hide built-in image preview actions

Please add a context key that lets third-party custom media/image preview extensions hide the built-in media preview editor title actions, similar to how the built-in Markdown extension supports `hasCustomMarkdownPreview`.

Today, the built-in Markdown extension contributes its preview actions with `!hasCustomMarkdownPreview` in the `when` clause. Extensions such as Markdown Preview Enhanced can set that context key to `true`, which cleanly hides the built-in Markdown preview buttons when a custom Markdown preview is installed.

The built-in media preview extension does not appear to expose an equivalent opt-out key. For SVG files, `extensions/media-preview/package.json` currently contributes actions such as:

This means an extension that provides its own SVG preview custom editor can add its own editor title action, but cannot hide the built-in `imagePreview.reopenAsPreview` action. Users then see two visually similar preview buttons in the editor title area.

A possible solution would be to add a context key, for example `hasCustomImagePreview` or `hasCustomMediaPreview`, and include it in the relevant media-preview `when` clauses:

Third-party preview extensions could then opt in with:

This would match the existing Markdown preview integration pattern and allow extensions to provide a cleaner editor title experience without overriding built-in commands or changing user editor associations.
canonical: The built-in media preview actions cannot be conditionally hidden by third-party custom media preview extensions because there is no context key equivalent to the one used by the Markdown preview to suppress built-in preview buttons.
