title: Python notebook cell editor context menu is rendered behind the bottom panel on Windows
document:
Python notebook cell editor context menu is rendered behind the bottom panel on Windows

- VS Code Version: 1.127.0 (user setup)
- Commit: 4fe60c8b1cdac1c4c174f2fb180d0d758272d713
- Date: 2026-06-30T10:52:33+02:00
- Electron: 42.2.0
- ElectronBuildId: 14159160
- Chromium: 148.0.7778.97
- Node.js: 24.15.0
- V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0
- OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Open VS Code on Windows.
2. Open a Python notebook file, for example a `.ipynb` file.
3. Open the bottom panel where you can see `Terminal`, `Problems`, `Output`, and `Debug Console`, etc.
4. In the notebook editor, right-click on the code cell itself, outside the editable code text area.
5. Observe that the cell-level context menu is displayed correctly and is not blocked by the bottom panel.

Cell-level context menu is displayed correctly:

6 Then click inside the editable code area of the notebook cell.
7. Right-click on code/text inside the cell.
8. Observe that the code editor context menu appears, but the lower part of the menu is hidden behind the bottom panel. Options near the bottom of the code editor context menu, such as `Cut`, `Copy`, and `Paste`, cannot be seen or selected unless the bottom panel is closed, moved, or resized.

Lower menu is hidden behind the bottom panel:

All menu options are visible after resizing the bottom panel:

**Expected behavior:**

Both notebook context menus should be rendered above the bottom panel. The context menu for editing code inside a notebook cell should remain fully visible and selectable, just like the cell-level context menu.

**Actual behavior:**

On Windows, the cell-level context menu appears correctly. However, the context menu shown when right-clicking inside the editable code area of a notebook cell is partially rendered behind the bottom panel. This makes lower menu options such as `Cut`, `Copy`, and `Paste` blocked and inaccessible.

**Additional information:**

- This issue appears to be specific to the notebook cell editor context menu on Windows. In a regular Python file, such as a `.py` file, right-clicking on code shows the code editor context menu correctly, and the menu is not blocked by the bottom panel.

Menu options are visible in a regular `.py` file:

- Please also note that this issue does not affect the cell-level context menu in notebooks, as mentioned above. If I right-click on the notebook code cell itself, outside the editable code text area, the cell-level context menu is displayed correctly. The problem only happens when I right-click inside the editable code area of a notebook cell.

- A similar issue was previously investigated here: (https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/184455), but it appears the verification was done only on macOS. The issue still persists on Windows.

**Workaround:**

Closing, moving, or resizing the bottom panel makes the full code editor context menu visible. However, the code editor context menu inside notebook cells should not be blocked by the bottom panel in the first place.

Thank you for looking into this. I appreciate the maintainers’ work on VS Code. This is my first time opening an issue here, so please let me know if you need any additional information.
canonical: The notebook cell editor context menu is being positioned behind the bottom panel on Windows, likely due to incorrect z-order or overlay handling for embedded notebook editors.

title: AutoApprove rules are not honored under Default Approvals for terminal commands
document:
AutoApprove rules are not honored under Default Approvals for terminal commands

# `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` rules are not honored under "Default Approvals" for terminal commands like `git diff`

Summary

When Copilot Chat is in "Default Approvals" mode, terminal commands that are explicitly allowed by `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` still show the "Run pwsh command?" confirmation dialog instead of auto-running.

This makes `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` ineffective for the expected user scenario: allowing safe terminal commands without repeated prompts.

Environment

- VS Code: 1.127.0
- GitHub Copilot Chat / Copilot: 0.55.0
- OS: Windows 10 Pro
- Shell: PowerShell
- Chat permission mode: "Default Approvals"

Step 1. Configure `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` with an allow rule for `git diff`, for example:

Step 2. In Copilot Chat, keep the permission mode set to "Default Approvals".

Step 3. Ask Copilot Chat to run this command:

Actual Result

A confirmation dialog appears:

- "Run pwsh command?"
- User must click "Allow" manually

Expected Result

Because the command matches an explicit allow rule in `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove`, it should auto-run without showing the confirmation dialog while in "Default Approvals".

Additional Notes

- The command is safe/read-only and is exactly the kind of command users expect to whitelist via `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove`.
- The command runs successfully after manual approval, so the problem is not command validity.
- From a user perspective, "Default Approvals" appears not to actually "use configured rules" for terminal commands in the expected way.
- This seems like either a regression or a behavior change that makes `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` much less useful than before.
- `Bypass Approvals` is not an acceptable workaround, because it auto-approves all tool calls, not only the commands explicitly allowed in `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove`.

Why this is a problem

Users configure `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` specifically to avoid repeated prompts for safe commands such as `git diff`, `git status`, etc. If those commands still trigger the top-level approval dialog in "Default Approvals", the setting no longer provides the expected value.
canonical: Terminal commands explicitly allowed by `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` are still prompting for approval in Default Approvals mode instead of auto-running, indicating the approval mode is not honoring the configured allow rules.

title: Codex app-server crashes with SIGTRAP on any Agent task after updating VS Code to 1.127.0 (Intel macOS Monterey)
document:
Codex app-server crashes with SIGTRAP on any Agent task after updating VS Code to 1.127.0 (Intel macOS Monterey)

Environment
VS Code: 1.127.0
ChatGPT Extension: openai.chatgpt

Codex CLI (bundled by extension): Native macos-x86_64
Machine: MacBook Air (Early 2015)
CPU: Intel Core i5-5350U
Architecture: x86_64

Problem

After updating VS Code to 1.127.0, the ChatGPT extension's Codex Agent consistently crashes whenever it starts performing any repository-related task.

The extension loads successfully, authentication works, and normal chat functions correctly.

However, as soon as the Agent begins interacting with the workspace (for example, inspecting files, reading the repository, or creating a file), the bundled Codex app-server terminates unexpectedly.

Open VS Code 1.127.0.
Open any repository (also reproducible in a brand-new empty folder).
Open the ChatGPT extension.
Start a Codex Agent session.
Ask the agent to perform any workspace task such as:
"Inspect this repository."
"Read package.json."
"Create a README.md."
Observe that the Codex process crashes immediately.
Expected Behavior

The Codex Agent should inspect the repository and complete the requested task.

Actual Behavior

The native Codex process exits unexpectedly.

The extension reports:

Codex process errored:
Codex app-server process exited unexpectedly
(code=unknown, signal=SIGTRAP)

The last CLI message before the crash is:

codex_rollout::list:
state db discrepancy during find_thread_path_by_id_str_in_subdir:
falling_back

After the crash, the extension repeatedly reports:

Codex process is not available
Attempted to send app-server message but stdin is destroyed
Troubleshooting Already Performed

I have already verified the following:

Recreated ~/.codex from scratch.
Tested with multiple unrelated repositories.
Tested with a completely empty workspace.
Reinstalled the ChatGPT extension.
Downgraded the ChatGPT extension to a previous version.
Restarted VS Code multiple times.
Verified the correct Intel (macos-x86_64) Codex binary is being launched.

The issue persists in all cases.

Crash Report

The macOS crash report shows the native codex process terminating with:

EXC_BREAKPOINT (SIGTRAP)

The fault occurs during V8 initialization:

The crash occurs before any repository analysis appears to begin
canonical: The bundled Codex app-server crashes during V8 startup when the agent begins any workspace-related task, likely due to an incompatibility or corrupted state in the native binary triggered by the VS Code update.

title: v1.127: Claude models -  200k context size option removed, only 1M available

v1.127: Claude models -  200k context size option removed, only 1M available

With v.127, the 200k context size option has been removed for Claude models.
Only 1M is now shown (marked as "Default"), with no way to select a smaller context window.

Open VS Code Copilot Chat
Click the model selector → Claude Sonnet 4.6 or Opus 4.8
Click the context size button (bottom bar, shows "1M")
→ Only 1M is listed; no 200k option
Expected Behavior
The 200k option should be available as it was for previous versions, allowing users to control cost vs. context tradeoff.

Actual Behavior
Only 1M available. With every turn, the context cost increases - till 1M context window is reached.

Especially in combination with Cold Cache , this can lead to significant increase of costs!

Yes - the /compact @ 200k hurt! -- but it's an easy way to give beginners access to Github - without the risk of burning money in long coding sessions.

https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/186494#discussioncomment-17502233
canonical: Claude model selection no longer offers the smaller context window option, forcing only the larger default context size and causing higher token costs over long conversations.

title: Allow Repository inheritence of MCP Servers like Skills
document:
Allow Repository inheritence of MCP Servers like Skills

Right now you have to repeat yourself for every workspace within a repo (especially painful with a mono-repro). It would be good if we had an option similar to chat.useCustomizationsInParentRepositories that allowed yo uto specify that it will walk the stack and look for mcp servers up to the top of the repro not just what's in the workspace. This also has issues because of multi-root workspace files which have no "root" to look in.

Ideally this would walk upwards looking for both .mcp.json and the vs code specific one (but you should just standardize on .mcp.json) and add them all together to create all of the mcps available. This would look in every folder in the workspace file, and then in the same folder as the workspace file, and then all parent folders to the root of the repo.

If just opening a folder, it would look in the current and walk upwards obviously.

This eliminates the need for duplicate .mcp.json files everywhere.
canonical: MCP server configurations are only discovered from the current workspace instead of inheriting and merging configurations from parent repository folders, forcing duplicate setup across nested or multi-root workspaces.

title: On 'manageCheckboxStateManually', refreshing node with new checkbox state flips all parent checkbox states recursively
document:
On 'manageCheckboxStateManually', refreshing node with new checkbox state flips all parent checkbox states recursively

# Background 

I am working on a custom vsocde extension. This extension uses a `treeView` and a `treeDataProvider`. Each node has a checkbox which I have to manually update (`manageCheckboxStateManually` is set to `true`), as I want the following behaviour, which differs from the default behaviour :
- Children are always update with the new state of the target clicked
- Parents are always checked, as long as one of its children is checked.

Attached is a minimum working example, based on the `vscode-tree-view` sample. In this example, I am keeping track of the checkbox states of all nodes via a simple map. 

# Observed

Now, when I uncheck an item, all of its parent items checkbox state gets set to `unchecked`, even though my code clearly doesn't set the checkbox state to `unchecked.` All parents are update even though, only the affected parents are given to `onDidChangeTreeData.fire` event, here the `refresh` method.

Similar thing seems to be happening when checking but not sure

# Expected

I would expected vscode to just update the node and its children which I provided to the refresh method. Additionally, I would expecte the parent checkboxes to not get flipped/changed.

# Steps to reproduce

1. Open the attached example. 
2. `npm install`
3. Run the extension with F5 and navigate to the `Test View`
4. Check all checkboxes of node `a`
5. Expanded node `aa`
6. Now unchecked `aaa`, `aab`, and finally `aac`.
7. After unchecking the last node, both `aa` and `a` will be unchecked, even though only `aa` should be unchecked.

An even better example is to expand the complete tree and just uncheck `aaaaaa`. This will trigger a recursive update of the parents and its checkbox states.

# Potential Workaround

It seems like, when I pass the parent of the node that changed, meaning a node that in theory didn't change, then it seems to work? To me, this doesn't make much sense but maybe I am overlooking something and it is an error on my side?

# Attachments
[checkboxTest.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29593677/checkboxTest.zip)

# Further info

This function handles the checkbox change. `affectedKeys` holds all nodes that changed and must be update.
First, all children/descendants are updated via the `setDescendants` function
Second, we travers upwards the parents/ancerstors and break early, if the parent checkbox state did not change.
Then we pass the affectedKeys to the refresh. We could theoretically also just pass the highest parent here but doesn't matter for the example.
To add the workaround comment in `affectedKeys.add(ancestorKey);`.

 

# Environment
canonical: Refreshing a tree node with manually managed checkboxes can incorrectly propagate checkbox state changes up through all ancestor nodes, causing parents to flip based on refreshed descendants even when only the targeted node was meant to update.

title: NES xtab: gather language context and neighbor snippets concurrently instead of sequentially
document:
NES xtab: gather language context and neighbor snippets concurrently instead of sequentially

In `XtabProvider.provideNextEdit` (`extensions/copilot/src/extension/xtab/node/xtabProvider.ts`), the two context-gathering steps are awaited one after the other:

`gatherLanguageContext()` and `gatherNeighborSnippets()` are independent, so awaiting them sequentially adds their latencies together on the critical path before the prompt can be built. They should be started together (e.g. `Promise.all`) so they run concurrently.

Notes for whoever picks this up:
- This pattern appears in both the global-budget branch and the legacy/prod `else` branch, so both should be updated.
- Preserve the existing cancellation checks and their distinct `GotCancelled` reasons (`afterLanguageContextAwait`, `afterNeighborSnippetsAwait`) for telemetry continuity, checking cancellation after the combined await.
- Preserve the `nLinesOfCurrentFileInPrompt` telemetry timing in the prod branch (the current-file clip happens before these awaits there).

Surfaced during review of #323948.
canonical: The next-edit provider gathers language context and neighbor snippets one after the other instead of in parallel, unnecessarily increasing latency before building the prompt.

Environment

When utility small model is not selected, Copilot fails to use BYOK provider

related to https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/322751: Idea is good, but implementation is flawed: currently if no utility model selected, Copilot just fails to respond, showing the error

> No utility model is configured for 'copilot-utility-small' while the selected main model is BYOK.

Moreover, I'm using llama.cpp with the model fitted tightly to VRAM, so re-loading the model is much higher overhead than just using the currently selected one for small requests. But if I have Qwen3.6 selected, and my utility model is, for example, Gemma 4 12B, its usage will require re-loading the model. To avoid that, there should be an option "Use same model as the main selected one", and a way to select models of a different provider (currently, dropdowns for utility models only list models of the BYOK provider).

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: builtin to VS Code
- VS Code Version: 1.128.0-insider (7e7f9505b9ef0a7a0899794997d4b1cb608084fc)
- OS Version: 10.0.26200.8655
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Agent
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): BYOK
- Logs:

1. Configure `chatLanguageModels.json` to include

2. Open Agentic panel, select Qwen3.6-28B-REAP20-A3B-256k
3. Say "Hi", Enter

you'll get the error

> No utility model is configured for 'copilot-utility-small' while the selected main model is BYOK.

4. Configure `unsloth_gemma-4-12b-it-GGUF:Q6_K_XL-256k` in vscode-insiders://settings/chat.utilitySmallModel
5. Retry the response

You'll see the local engine will load the second model (along with the first one if limit permits, or will unload the first model) for the utility use, then switch back to the first model, which is much slower than just generating everything with the same main model.

You may ask, why would I select qwen while gemma is configured as the utility? I frequently switch between models, including a few of my BYOK and models provided by Copilot.

The previous behavior of always using copilot models for utility worked for me mostly, but it is flawed in some parts and for example prevented me from ever using commit message generation with copilot. Also it fails when internet connectivity is missing. This in an opportunity to fix both issues!

Also perhaps, the utility model should be configurable per provider in `chatLanguageModels.json`, with same options as available in the user `settings.json`, so a cloud BYOK configuration allow setting up different `chat.utilityModel` and `chat.utilitySmallModel`, while llama.cpp config having `main` there (or some other non-model-name keyword) to avoid re-loading.

Either way, utility models should not be restricted to the same provider as the main selected model.
canonical: Copilot chat fails to use a configured utility model when the main model is a BYOK provider, because utility-model selection is incorrectly restricted to the same provider or left unset instead of falling back to the active model or allowing cross-provider utility models.

title: Plan mode re-sends the "Start with Auto Copilot" command after switching back from Agent mode, causing duplicate execution and extra credit consumption
document:
Plan mode re-sends the "Start with Auto Copilot" command after switching back from Agent mode, causing duplicate execution and extra credit consumption

## Description

I found a reproducible issue when using the Plan and Agent workflow.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open Copilot Chat.
2. Switch to **Plan** mode.
3. Ask Copilot to generate an implementation plan.
4. After the plan is generated, click **Start with Auto Copilot**.
5. Wait until Auto Copilot finishes executing all tasks.
6. Switch from **Agent** mode back to **Plan** mode.

## Expected Behavior

Switching back to Plan mode should simply display the completed plan and allow me to continue planning or make changes. No additional action should be triggered automatically.

## Actual Behavior

As soon as I switch back to Plan mode, Copilot automatically sends the previous **"Start with Auto Copilot"** implementation request again, causing the implementation process to be triggered a second time.

This happens even though the previous Auto Copilot task has already completed successfully.

## Impact

This issue has two negative effects:

* The implementation is executed twice unnecessarily.
* The unintended second execution also consumes additional AI credits.

Because the second execution is triggered automatically when switching back to Plan mode, there is no opportunity to cancel it beforehand. This can lead to unnecessary credit usage and may quickly consume a user's available quota.

## Environment

* VS Code Version: 1.127.0 (Universal)
* Commit: 4fe60c8b1cdac1c4c174f2fb180d0d758272d713
* Date: 2026-06-30T10:52:33+02:00
* Electron: 42.2.0
* Electron Build ID: 14159160
* Chromium: 148.0.7778.97
* Node.js: 24.15.0
* V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0
* OS: macOS 13.6 (Darwin x64 22.6.0)

## Additional Notes

It appears that switching back to Plan mode replays or re-executes the previous **"Start with Auto Copilot"** action instead of simply restoring the completed planning session.

In my opinion, switching between **Plan** and **Agent** modes should not automatically trigger any new execution. It should only restore the previous conversation state unless the user explicitly starts a new Auto Copilot run.

I can provide a screen recording if that would help reproduce the issue.
canonical: Switching back from Agent mode to Plan mode incorrectly replays the previous Auto Copilot start action, causing the task to run a second time and consume extra credits.

title: Copilot Chat: first invocation of a remote MCP tool fails with "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'invoke')"
document:
Copilot Chat: first invocation of a remote MCP tool fails with "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'invoke')"

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.55.0
- VS Code Version: 1.127.0 (also reproduced on Insiders 1.128.0-insider)
- OS Version: Linux (Ubuntu-based) x64
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Agent mode, tool invocation of a remote MCP server (GitHub MCP Server, `mcp_github_mcp_se_*` tools)
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): Claude Sonnet 5
- Logs: see excerpt below

### Description

The first time a **remote MCP tool** is invoked right after a window/session becomes active (extension host activation, new login, or after "Workspace folders changed"), the call fails with:

Built-in tools (`read_file`, `run_in_terminal`, etc.) are **not** affected — only MCP-server-provided tools fail on this first call. Subsequent calls to the same tool a few seconds later succeed without any user action.

1. Configure a remote MCP server (e.g. GitHub MCP Server) so its tools show up in the agent's tool list.
2. Open a new window / re-activate the extension host (e.g. reload window, or let it sit idle until it re-authenticates).
3. In the very first agent turn that calls a tool provided by that MCP server, the call fails with the `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'invoke')` shown above.
4. Ask the same thing again (or wait ~5-10s) — the same MCP tool now invokes successfully.

### Log excerpt (timeline)

### Analysis

It looks like the MCP tool's schema/entry becomes visible in the agent's tool list before the underlying invocation handler (the object whose `.invoke` is being read) has finished being wired up for that server connection. The tool list is populated slightly ahead of the connection being fully ready, so the very first invocation attempt hits a race where the handler object is still `undefined`.

This has been reproduced consistently across multiple sessions, always as the *first* MCP tool call after the extension host / MCP connection becomes active, and always resolves itself on the next attempt.

### Expected Behavior

The tool list should only expose an MCP tool for invocation once its handler is fully initialized, or `invokeTool` should await/retry the connection instead of throwing a `TypeError` when the handler is momentarily `undefined`.

### Workaround

- Wait a few seconds / send a non-tool message first after opening a new window before invoking any MCP-provided tool.
- Retry the same request; the second attempt succeeds.
canonical: A remote MCP tool can be shown as available before its invocation handler is fully initialized, causing the first call after activation to fail because the handler is still undefined.

- [x] Reinstalled the entire language pack

What I have tried (issue not reproducible / works as expected):

*I hope the above information is clear. Please let me know if there is anything else I can provide.*

Original Chinese text for reference:

我尝试了以下方法来试图修复，问题均可以复现也不不符合预期：

title: Agent Host: tool responses with long outputs create an extremely wide file
document:
Agent Host: tool responses with long outputs create an extremely wide file

Tool responses with long outputs are saved into a file with just 6 lines, but the 4th line is extremely long (68K character long) with literal newline characters, instead of a multi-line file. This forces the agent to use Python to parse the JSON and make sense of it:

Maybe for text outputs we should just have the text itself as a regular multi-line file instead of turning the file into a single-line literal string inside a JSON file? That could make it easier for the agent to parse the file contents via simpler methods that don't require Python, such as terminal tools.

Attaching the file shown in the screenshot above in case it helps:

[toolu_bdrk_01YFqGWBza4BwKULSuztbzzv.json](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29577921/toolu_bdrk_01YFqGWBza4BwKULSuztbzzv.json)

title: Track full session porting for Continue in Agent Host
document:
Track full session porting for Continue in Agent Host

Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/315193
Related attachment tracking issue: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323969
Related scoped PR: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323773

## Context

PR #323773 handles a narrow attachment case: when a user attaches a Copilot CLI session from Agent Host chat, Agent Host can forward the session's `events.jsonl` as context.

This issue tracks a different problem: full session porting for `continue in` flows. In this path, the user is not merely attaching a previous session via the `+`/context UI. Instead, the old session should become, or be imported into, the active Agent Host conversation.

## Attachment vs porting

| Flow | User intent | Mechanism | Current PR #323773? |
|---|---|---|---:|
| Session attachment | Reference another session as context in the current chat | Visible session chip plus model-readable attachment, currently `events.jsonl` for Copilot CLI | Yes, only for Copilot CLI `events.jsonl` sessions |
| Full session port / `continue in` | Continue/resume an existing session in Agent Host | Convert/import/resume the previous session as the active Agent Host conversation | No |

## Porting questions to resolve

- Which source session types should be portable into Agent Host?
- What is the canonical source of truth for each source session type?
 - Copilot CLI `events.jsonl`
 - `IChatModel` / workbench chat model state
 - provider-specific session content
 - AHP session state
- How should tool calls, tool results, file edits, approvals, working directory, model selection, and session metadata map into the target Agent Host session?
- Should porting preserve original session identity or create a new Agent Host session linked back to the source?
- What should happen when source session data is partial, provider-specific, or unavailable?

## Initial matrix

| Source session type | Example URI | Possible source of truth | Porting status |
|---|---|---|---|
| EH Copilot CLI | `copilotcli:/` | `~/.copilot/session-state//events.jsonl` | Needs design |
| Local Agent Host Copilot CLI | `agent-host-copilotcli:/` | AHP state plus Copilot CLI `events.jsonl` | Needs design |
| Remote Agent Host Copilot CLI | `remote--copilotcli:/` | Remote AHP state plus remote `events.jsonl` | Needs design |
| Other Agent Host/AHP sessions | provider-specific Agent Host session URI | AHP state/provider state | Needs design |
| Local/workbench chat sessions | workbench chat session URI | `IChatModel` / chat session model | Needs design |
| Claude Code / other providers | provider-specific URI | Provider-specific session state | Needs design |

## Non-goal

This issue does not track using a previous session as an attachment/context item. That matrix is tracked in #323969.

/cc @connor4312 @roblourens
canonical: Agent Host does not yet support importing or resuming an entire previous session as the active conversation for continue-in flows, instead only treating prior sessions as attachable context.

title: Track session-reference attachment support across chat session types
document:
Track session-reference attachment support across chat session types

Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/315193
Related PR: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323773

## Context

PR #323773 is intentionally scoped to Agent Host session references that can resolve to a Copilot CLI `events.jsonl` file. That gives Agent Host Copilot a concrete model-readable attachment source without introducing a new AHP attachment kind or attempting broader session migration.

This issue tracks the broader attachment matrix for session references across chat/session providers.

## Current scoped behavior in #323773

- Agent Host-backed chats only show attachable Copilot CLI sessions in the `Sessions...` picker.
- Supported session references are converted into:
 - a visible session chip (`Simple` attachment) for UI/replay metadata
 - a hidden `Resource` attachment pointing at the host-local `events.jsonl`
- Unsupported session references are not converted as model-readable attachments by Agent Host.

## Session attachment matrix

| Referenced session type | Example URI | Readable history source | Supported by #323773? | Follow-up path |
|---|---|---|---:|---|
| EH Copilot CLI | `copilotcli:/` | Local `~/.copilot/session-state//events.jsonl` | Yes | Keep using `events.jsonl` as attachment source |
| Local Agent Host Copilot CLI | `agent-host-copilotcli:/` | Local `~/.copilot/session-state//events.jsonl` | Yes | Keep using `events.jsonl` as attachment source |
| Remote Agent Host Copilot CLI | `remote--copilotcli:/` | Remote host `~/.copilot/session-state//events.jsonl`, when connection/home resolves | Yes, when resolvable | Keep using host-local `events.jsonl` path |
| Other Agent Host/AHP sessions | provider-specific Agent Host session URI | No shared `events.jsonl` contract | No | Need first-class AHP session-reference support or an `IChatModel`/session-content path |
| Claude Code sessions | provider-specific session URI | Not Copilot CLI `events.jsonl` | No | Need provider-specific or common session model access |
| Local/workbench chat sessions | workbench chat session URI | `IChatModel`/chat session model, not Copilot CLI `events.jsonl` | No | Need `IChatModel`/session-content attachment path |
| Other extension-host chat sessions | extension/provider-specific URI | Provider-specific, no shared `events.jsonl` contract | No | Need provider-specific or first-class session-reference contract |

## Open questions

- Should session references become a first-class AHP attachment concept instead of being represented through `Simple` + `Resource`?
- Should non-Copilot-CLI sessions expose model-readable attachment content through `IChatModel`, `IChatSessionsService`, or provider-specific session content APIs?
- What UI affordance should distinguish a visible session chip from hidden model-readable context resources?

## Non-goal

This issue is about attaching a referenced session as context. Full session migration/porting for `continue in` should be tracked separately.

/cc @connor4312 @roblourens
canonical: Session references are only attachable as model-readable context for Copilot CLI sessions because other chat session types lack a shared session-content path or attachment contract, so they cannot be converted into readable attachments.

title: .gitignore will prevent an extension from launching.
document:
.gitignore will prevent an extension from launching.

- VS Code Version: 1.127
- OS Version: windows 10

When an extension uses workspaceContains as an activation event and the path uses global wildcard with a folder, .gitignore with a certain glob pattern will prevent the extension from launching.

It seems that workspaceContains search respects the .gitignore on some edge cases and uses Use Ignore Files setting (on by default) so the file cannot be found, and the extension will fail to launch. I would assume that this is not intended behavior?

project structure:

1. git has been initialized and Use Ignore Files enabled
2. extension has "activationEvents": [ "workspaceContains:**/Content/test123.json"]
3. test123.json inside Content folder
4. **/Content or /Content inside .gitignore

however, with **/Content/* inside .gitignore this will work and the extension is launched.

This also works (extension will launch with these settings):
setting in package.json: "activationEvents": [ "workspaceContains:Content/test123.json"]
test123.json or **/Content inside .gitignore
canonical: A workspace file search used for extension activation can incorrectly honor ignore patterns and fail to find a matching file, preventing the extension from activating when a globbed folder path is involved.

title: Unable to copy or capture whole chat session content.
document:
Unable to copy or capture whole chat session content.

The following report was generated by Visual Studio Code Ask Agent.

Here is a concise internal engineering report you can send:

Subject: Internal Engineering Report — Inability to Capture Full Chat Session Content and Transcript-Generation Loop

Summary:
During a recent Copilot chat session, the user reported inability to capture the full chat session content through the available chat UI controls. Attempts to use “Copy all”, “Copy conversation”, and “Export chat” were unsuccessful or ineffective. In addition, when asked to generate a clean transcript of the entire session, the assistant entered a repetitive response loop and required a stop command to terminate the behavior.

Observed Behavior:

The chat UI did not provide a reliable mechanism to copy or export the full session content.
Ctrl+A did not select the complete chat history as expected.
Copying only captured a partial segment of the conversation rather than the whole session.
Transcript generation repeatedly re-emitted similar content rather than producing a one-shot full transcript.
Impact:

Users cannot reliably export or copy complete chat sessions for documentation, review, debugging, or support purposes.
The transcript-generation behavior can create a poor user experience and appears to be unstable under certain prompts.
Requested Action:

Investigate and improve whole-session copy/export support in the chat interface.
Investigate the repetitive transcript-generation behavior and implement a guard against looping or repeated content generation.
Consider adding a reliable export path for full conversation history.
canonical: The chat interface cannot reliably copy or export the entire conversation history, and transcript generation can get stuck repeating content instead of producing a complete one-shot transcript.

title: Audit deprecated Copilot SDK terminal content usage
document:
Audit deprecated Copilot SDK terminal content usage

Follow-up from https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323922 and https://github.com/github/copilot-sdk/issues/1803.

Copilot SDK now exposes `shell_exit` for shell command exit metadata, and SDK `terminal` content is deprecated for that purpose. I want to make sure we do not keep depending on SDK `type: "terminal"` content for new shell status behavior.

From a quick audit, the Agent Host `ToolResultContentType.Terminal` usages are not the same thing as the deprecated SDK terminal content. Those are VS Code/AHP terminal resource references with a terminal URI, and should stay separate from SDK `shell_exit` metadata.

Actual deprecated SDK terminal-content dependency found:

- `formatSearchToolInvocationCompleted` still reads SDK `result.contents[0].type === 'terminal'` and `text` for glob/grep/rg result parsing:
 - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9d925988ae356620214d52776fba0a4bb486fe1f/extensions/copilot/src/extension/chatSessions/copilotcli/common/copilotCLITools.ts#L1267-L1268
- Tests still encode that legacy SDK `terminal` content shape for glob/grep/rg:
 - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9d925988ae356620214d52776fba0a4bb486fe1f/extensions/copilot/src/extension/chatSessions/copilotcli/common/test/copilotCLITools.spec.ts#L912-L982

Places checked that are probably not deprecation debt:

- Agent Host custom terminal content uses `ToolResultContentType.Terminal` as a live terminal resource reference (`resource`, `title`), not SDK terminal output text:
 - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9d925988ae356620214d52776fba0a4bb486fe1f/src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotAgentSession.ts#L637-L640
 - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9d925988ae356620214d52776fba0a4bb486fe1f/src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotAgentSession.ts#L2672-L2677
- Chat-side terminal rendering reads Agent Host terminal resource blocks by URI, not deprecated SDK terminal output blocks:
 - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9d925988ae356620214d52776fba0a4bb486fe1f/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/stateToProgressAdapter.ts#L144-L150
 - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/9d925988ae356620214d52776fba0a4bb486fe1f/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/stateToProgressAdapter.ts#L718-L722

Work:

- Confirm whether the internal Copilot CLI extension path should keep supporting SDK `terminal` content for glob/grep/rg result parsing as legacy compatibility.
- If not, migrate search result parsing to `result.content` or another non-deprecated SDK content shape.
- Keep `shell_exit` limited to shell command exit metadata; do not treat it as display output or as a live terminal resource.
- Rename or update tests so any remaining SDK `terminal` coverage is clearly marked as legacy compatibility.
- Make sure Agent Host `ToolResultContentType.Terminal` remains documented as a VS Code/AHP terminal resource, not deprecated SDK terminal content.

This is mostly cleanup/debt after adopting `shell_exit`; I do not think it blocks the command decoration fix.
canonical: The Copilot CLI extension still depends on deprecated SDK terminal content to parse search tool results, instead of using the newer shell exit metadata or another supported result shape.

title: Revert isExternalFocusAllowed escape hatch in base dialog.ts
document:
Revert isExternalFocusAllowed escape hatch in base dialog.ts

🤖 AI Assisted Bug Report

## Summary

PR #323914 adds an \isExternalFocusAllowed\ option to \src/vs/base/browser/ui/dialog/dialog.ts\. This is a temporary workaround to allow focus to leave the dialog for external popup targets (context views, action widgets, select boxes) that mount at the window root rather than inside the dialog DOM.

## Why this exists

The automations create/edit dialog embeds \ChatInputPart\, which uses platform overlay services (\IContextViewService\, \IActionWidgetService\) that render popups outside the dialog element. The base Dialog class has a focus trap that immediately pulls focus back when it leaves. Without this escape hatch, all pickers in the dialog are unusable.

## Why this is temporary

The base Dialog class is designed for simple confirmations, not rich embedded editors. Using it for the automations dialog is a known architectural shortcut to ship the feature. The proper fix is to determine the right UX surface for automation management (editor pane, sidebar, or dedicated view) and move the UI there, eliminating the need for a focus-trapping modal entirely.

## Resolution

Once the automations UI has a proper home, revert the \isExternalFocusAllowed\ addition from \dialog.ts\ and remove the \isAutomationDialogPopupTarget\ predicate.
canonical: The dialog’s focus trap was changed to allow focus to escape for external popup controls, and that workaround should be removed because the dialog is being used for a richer embedded editor than it was designed to support.

title: Close single-select pickers on selection (match context-menu behavior)
document:
Close single-select pickers on selection (match context-menu behavior)

Summary

When a picker that acts as a single-choice context menu contains only one pickable group, selecting an item should close the picker automatically (like a normal context menu). Currently some pickers remain open after selection, forcing the user to click elsewhere to dismiss them.

Current behavior

- In some parts of the UI a picker/dialog used to choose an option stays open after the user selects an item even when only a single choice is expected. This is different from the typical context-menu behavior where choosing an item dismisses the menu.

Proposed behavior

- If a picker contains only a single pickable group (i.e. it functions as a single-select context menu), the picker should close immediately when an item is selected.
- If the picker is intentionally multi-select (contains independent toggles or multiple items that are frequently changed together), it should continue to remain open after selections.
- Accessibility: ensure keyboard users can still perform multi-selection tasks. The behavior should be based on picker semantics (single-select vs multi-select), not on how the user opened it.
- Optional: consider supporting a modifier (e.g. hold Cmd/Ctrl) to keep the picker open when desired, but do not introduce a modifier-based-only workflow that breaks discoverability for keyboard users.

Why

- Closes the gap between expected context-menu behavior and current picker behavior, making the UI feel more natural and efficient when only a single selection is required.
- Reduces extra clicks for users in common single-choice flows.

Reproducible example

1. Open a configuration/context picker that presents a small list of mutually exclusive choices (e.g. choosing an effort level or a context menu with only one pickable group).
2. Select any option.
3. Observe whether the picker closes (expected) or remains open (current problematic behavior).

Acceptance criteria

- Single-select pickers close when an item is selected.
- Multi-select pickers remain open after selections.
- Keyboard navigation and accessibility features continue to work; no regressions for screen-reader users or keyboard-only users.
- Unit or integration tests added where appropriate to verify the behavior.

Notes

This request is motivated by internal discussion where "local" behavior closed the picker on selection while another variant kept it open. The change should be scoped to pickers whose semantics are single-select rather than globally changing all pickers.

[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C0AKR94N3CK/p1782397912807339?thread_ts=1782397912.807339&cid=C0AKR94N3CK)
canonical: Single-select pickers stay open after an item is chosen because they do not dismiss themselves based on single-choice semantics, unlike normal context menus.

title: Starting MCP servers block starting sessions
document:
Starting MCP servers block starting sessions

I started an agent host session, and the logs showed that it took 30s from sending the message to actually starting the session, and that time was spent starting mcp servers.

Not sure whether or not we know that the sdk is spending time in this state? 

Maybe we can at least show a progress message like the local harness does?

---

Yes. There was a real **~31.6-second delay before the model request began**.

### Timeline

| Event | Time after Send |
|---|---:|
| AHP turn dispatched | 0.0s |
| Agent Host session ready | 1.18s |
| SDK accepted `session.send()` | 1.22s |
| MarkItDown MCP became ready | 31.24s |
| First model request began | 31.60s |
| First assistant output | 36.13s |

### Explanation

The Copilot CLI blocked model startup while initializing MCP servers. Specifically:

- `microsoft/markitdown` was launched through `uvx markitdown-mcp@0.0.1a4`, configured in `/Users/roblou/Library/Application Support/Code - Insiders/User/mcp.json:38`.
- It took **28,245ms** to connect: `/Users/roblou/slow/copilot-logs/process-1782921317193-91410.log:187-189`.
- The first model request began immediately afterward at line 210.
- `events.jsonl` confirms the SDK session began at `16:52:34.489Z`, but the system/user messages were not processed until `16:53:04.920Z`—about 30.4 seconds later.

This matches the implementation: the provisional session is materialized before sending (`copilotAgent.ts:1468-1522`), MCP/plugin directories are passed into the SDK (`copilotSessionLauncher.ts:473-519`), and then the request is sent through the SDK (`copilotAgentSession.ts:1134-1137`).

There is also a suspicious secondary issue: every MCP server appears to have been started twice, roughly 100ms apart. Only one AHP turn and one session were created, so this looks SDK-side, but the logs do not conclusively explain it. The duplicate launches overlapped, so they were not the main 30-second cost.

The simplest confirmation would be to temporarily disable the MarkItDown MCP server and repeat the test. The other MCP failures were noisy but completed quickly.
canonical: Starting a session is delayed because MCP server initialization blocks the first model request, likely due to the SDK waiting for servers to become ready before sending the prompt.

title: Retained custom editor webview briefly renders at default iframe size when switching from a text editor
document:
Retained custom editor webview briefly renders at default iframe size when switching from a text editor

## Summary

A retained custom editor webview can briefly paint at a tiny/default iframe-sized viewport when switching back to it from a normal text editor tab.

This is especially visible for complex webview editors with multiple layout regions: canvas/content area, docked side panels, bottom panels, toolbars, persisted panel layout, and components that derive layout from `window.innerWidth` / `window.innerHeight`. A transient tiny viewport causes the editor to compose itself incorrectly for a frame or two, then snap back once the final editor bounds are applied.

This does not appear to be the extension resetting the webview HTML or losing React/app state. The webview context is retained. The issue appears to be the retained iframe-backed webview becoming visible before its host overlay has fully resolved to the active editor bounds.

## VS Code version

## Minimal repro

Create an extension that registers a custom editor with `retainContextWhenHidden: true`:

The webview content should have a viewport-derived layout with multiple regions and log its size across resize/reveal frames, for example:

Use simple HTML/CSS with a header, main content area, right docked panel, and bottom panel so incorrect intermediate sizing is visible.

Steps:

1. Open a file handled by the custom editor.
2. Open a normal text file in the same editor group.
3. Switch from the text file back to the custom editor.
4. Repeat the tab switch a few times.

## Expected behavior

When a retained custom editor webview is revealed, it should either already have the final editor dimensions before painting, or remain hidden until its host overlay has those final dimensions.

The webview should not briefly lay out against a tiny/default/stale viewport during reveal.

## Actual behavior

For one or more frames after switching from a text editor back to the retained custom editor, the webview can paint as if its viewport is much smaller than the editor area, then resize to the final editor bounds.

For complex viewport-derived layouts, this creates a visible top-left/cropped composition before the UI snaps to the correct layout.

## Relevant source-level observation

At the VS Code commit above, retained overlay webviews appear to follow this sequence:

- `OverlayWebview.release()` hides the overlay content with `visibility: hidden`; with `retainContextWhenHidden`, it keeps the underlying webview alive.
- `OverlayWebview.claim()` calls `_show(targetWindow)` before reapplying saved anchor state.
- `_show()` makes the retained overlay content visible again.
- `OverlayLayoutElement` sizes the overlay using CSS anchor positioning: `anchor-size(width)` / `anchor-size(height)`.
- The inner webview iframe is `width: 100%; height: 100%`.

Relevant files:

- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a/src/vs/workbench/contrib/webview/browser/overlayWebview.ts
- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a/src/vs/base/browser/overlayLayoutElement.ts
- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a/src/vs/workbench/contrib/webview/browser/webviewElement.ts

So the suspected failure mode is: the retained iframe-backed webview can become visible while the CSS-anchor-sized overlay is still stale or unresolved for the newly active editor location.
canonical: A retained custom editor webview can briefly become visible at a tiny default iframe-sized viewport when switching back from a text editor because it is shown before its overlay host has updated to the final editor bounds.

title: Allow vscode:// (file / folder open) URL to specify non-destructive window re-use options
document:
Allow vscode:// (file / folder open) URL to specify non-destructive window re-use options

### What I want

A `vscode://` URL handler option (probably a query parameter) like `windowReuseLogic="reuse-matching-workspace-else-new" | "reuse-matching-workspace-else-warn" | "reuse-matching-workspace-else-replace-active" | "replace-active"` or, simpler, `noReplaceActive=1`

This complements the existing `windowId=_blank` (force-new-window) param by providing clearer user experience for link emitters that can't know up-front whether the user already has the workspace open.

### Why

I emit `vscode://` deeplinks from a docs viewer into the user's editor. Today, the same URL hijacks the active window (replacing it) if the file is not part of any open window, with no way for the link author to express intent:

- A file URL inside a path that matches an open workspace → reuses that window (good).
- A file URL whose path is *not* inside any open workspace → opens new window (good).
- A **folder URL** (no `:line:col` suffix) → can replace the currently active window's workspace (bad -- destroys the user's context / terminals / ephemeral state without warning).
- `windowId=_blank` always forces a brand-new window even when an existing workspace already contains the file (wasteful, fragments windows).

### Related

- microsoft/vscode-remote-release#10650 — force-new-window for remote URL scheme
- microsoft/vscode#215749 — open folder not reused window with multi-root (closed, not planned)
- microsoft/vscode#299540 — `git commit` with `core.editor = code --wait` opens in wrong window
canonical: A file or folder deep link can unexpectedly replace the active editor window instead of safely reusing an existing matching workspace or opening a new window, because the current link handling lacks a way to request non-destructive window reuse.

title: Feature Request: Horizontal Primary Side Bar (Activity Bar) — Explorer/Search/SCM/Debug/Extensions like modern VS Code forks
document:
Feature Request: Horizontal Primary Side Bar (Activity Bar) — Explorer/Search/SCM/Debug/Extensions like modern VS Code forks

## Summary

Please improve the **Primary Side Bar / Activity Bar layout** so primary views (Explorer, Search, Source Control, Run and Debug, Extensions) can work in a **horizontal top row inside the side bar**, similar to modern AI editors built on VS Code — not only as a vertical icon strip on the far left.

This is a **layout / workbench UX** request first. Improved Source Control commit UX (grouped CHANGES, inline generate message) fits naturally **below** that horizontal view switcher.

## Problem

In default VS Code, the Activity Bar is **vertical** on the far left:

- Explorer
- Search
- Source Control
- Run and Debug
- Extensions

In editors like **Cursor** (VS Code fork), the same primary views are **horizontal** at the top of the side panel. That layout:

- Uses vertical space more efficiently on wide monitors
- Keeps view switching and panel content (Explorer tree, CHANGES, etc.) in one visual column
- Matches what many developers now expect when switching from AI-native editors back to VS Code

VS Code already exposes `workbench.activityBar.location`, but:

1. **Default is still vertical** — most users never discover horizontal/top layout
2. **Top layout polish** does not match the integrated side-bar experience in forks (horizontal tabs + panel content feel like one unit)
3. **Source Control** in vertical mode feels disconnected from the commit box users see in modern layouts

## Screenshots (comparison)

See **comment below** for attached screenshots:

1. **VS Code (current):** vertical Activity Bar — Explorer, Search, Source Control, Run and Debug, Extensions stacked on the far left
2. **Cursor (reference):** horizontal row — same views aligned horizontally above CHANGES / commit UI

## Proposal

### 1. Horizontal Primary Side Bar mode (default or first-run prompt)

- Add or promote a **horizontal view switcher** at the top of the Primary Side Bar:
 - Explorer | Search | Source Control | Run and Debug | Extensions | …
- Make this the **recommended default** for new installs (or ask once in Welcome / Settings Sync setup)
- Keep vertical Activity Bar as opt-in via `workbench.activityBar.location: "default"`

### 2. Unified side bar chrome

When horizontal mode is active:

- Active view is clearly highlighted (tab/segment style)
- Panel content fills the area **below** the horizontal switcher (Explorer tree, Search results, SCM CHANGES, etc.)
- Optional compact mode: hide duplicate headers

### 3. Source Control panel under horizontal layout

When Source Control is selected in the horizontal row:

- Show **CHANGES** / staged-unstaged grouping prominently
- Commit message input + **Generate message** (when AI available) + **Commit ▾** directly below
- Placeholder: `Message (Ctrl+Enter to commit on "main")`

Related: #273271 (AI commit message generation), prior SCM UX discussions.

### 4. Settings



(Exact setting names up to the team; goal is discoverability + polish, not only a hidden flag.)

## User story

> As a developer switching between VS Code and Cursor, I want Explorer / Search / Git / Debug / Extensions as **horizontal tabs on top of the side bar**, with the active panel content below — without hunting for obscure settings or feeling like I'm using a different product paradigm.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Horizontal primary view switcher available and polished in core workbench
- [ ] New users can discover it without reading docs (Welcome, layout picker, or default)
- [ ] Vertical Activity Bar remains supported
- [ ] Source Control content layout works well under horizontal switcher
- [ ] No extension required for basic horizontal layout

## Environment

- VS Code: 1.127.0
- OS: Windows 11
- Comparison reference: Cursor (VS Code-based)

## Motivation

VS Code is upstream for many editors that ship horizontal side-bar navigation by default. Closing this layout gap reduces friction when developers move between VS Code and forks, and makes the workbench feel current without third-party customization.
canonical: The workbench defaults to a vertical activity bar and lacks a polished, discoverable horizontal primary view switcher in the side bar, making the explorer/search/source control/debug/extensions layout feel fragmented and less integrated than modern VS Code forks.

title: Chat image attachments are silently dropped when not signed in to GitHub, even with a configured multimodal provider
document:
Chat image attachments are silently dropped when not signed in to GitHub, even with a configured multimodal provider

- VS Code Version: 1.126.0
- OS Version: Windows 11 Pro 25H2 and Ubuntu Desktop 26.04 — reproduces on both

When using the built-in VS Code Chat without being signed in to a GitHub account, image attachments are not delivered to the configured model. The image appears in the chat composer, but the moment the request is sent, the model responds as if nothing was attached.

Typical response from the model:

> No image was attached to your message. The `` section is empty. To get a description, please attach the image (drag-and-drop, paste, or use the paperclip icon) and resend your request.

This happens with **every** method of attaching an image:

- Drag-and-drop an image file from the OS into the chat input
- Copy an image to the clipboard and paste it into the chat input
- Click the `+` button and link an image file from disk (including files inside the currently open workspace folder)

I have configured the chat to use a multimodal model (e.g. a local / BYOK multimodal model such as `Minimax M3` and a multimodal `Qwen 3.6 27B` served locally), so the model itself is fully capable of consuming image input. The issue therefore appears to be on the client side — the attachment is stripped from the request before it reaches the model provider.

The same workflow works correctly when signed in to a GitHub account (see the [Control experiment](#control-experiment-signed-in-vs-signed-out) below), which is strong evidence that image attachment handling is gated on **Chat-side authentication** rather than on the configured provider, the model, the network, or extensions.

## Control experiment (signed-in vs. signed-out)

To rule out the model, the network, and the host machine as the cause, I ran the **exact same** sequence of steps back-to-back, changing **only** the Chat sign-in state:

| | **A — Signed OUT of GitHub** | **B — Signed IN to GitHub Copilot** |
|---|---|---|
| VS Code build | 1.127.0 (Insiders) | 1.127.0 (Insiders) |
| OS | Windows 11 Pro 25H2 | Windows 11 Pro 25H2 |
| Workspace | `c:\SERVER\TEMP\empty` | `c:\SERVER\TEMP\empty` |
| Active provider/model | `Minimax M3` (multimodal) | `Minimax M3` (multimodal) |
| Image attached | `LocalImage.png` (drag-and-drop) | `LocalImage.png` (drag-and-drop) |
| Prompt | "Please describe the image" | "Please describe the image" |
| **Model's response** | "No image was attached to your message. The `` section is empty." | Image content described correctly |

**Result:** the image is delivered to the model in **B**, silently dropped in **A**. The only variable that changes is the Chat sign-in state, so that is the deciding factor.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Install VS Code on Windows 11 or Ubuntu Desktop.
2. **Do not** sign in to a GitHub account.
3. In Chat settings, configure a multimodal model as the active provider/model (e.g. a local or BYOK multimodal endpoint such as `Minimax M3` or `Qwen 3.6 27B`).
4. Open a new Chat session.
5. Attempt to attach an image using **all three** of these methods:
 - Drag-and-drop an image file into the chat input.
 - Copy an image to the clipboard and paste it into the chat input.
 - Click the `+` button and pick an image file from disk.
6. Type any prompt (e.g. "Please describe the image") and send.
7. Observe that the model responds as if no image was attached.

## Expected behaviour

Because the configured provider is a multimodal model, the attached image should be transmitted to the model alongside the text prompt, and the model should be able to see and respond to the image content.

## Actual behaviour

The image attachment is silently dropped client-side. Only the text prompt reaches the model, which then responds that the `` section is empty.

## Additional context

- Affects **both** Windows 11 and Ubuntu Desktop.
- Affects multiple multimodal providers/models — not a single provider bug.
- The image preview *does* render correctly in the chat composer, which means the UI is receiving the attachment; the failure happens at request dispatch.
- Disabling all extensions **does not** fix the issue — confirmed by launching with `code --disable-extensions` and reproducing the same symptom, which rules out third-party extension interference.
- **Control experiment confirms the failure is gated on Chat sign-in state** — see the [Control experiment](#control-experiment-signed-in-vs-signed-out) section above for the side-by-side comparison. This rules out the model, the network, the OS, and extensions as causes.
- Screenshot showing the symptom attached to this issue.
- Related discussion: https://www.reddit.com/r/vscode/comments/1uk1pit/chat_attach_image_help/
canonical: Image attachments are silently stripped from chat requests when the user is not signed in, even though a multimodal provider is configured, because attachment transmission is incorrectly gated on chat account authentication rather than the selected model’s capabilities.

title: In Paste Confirmation Dialog: "Cancel" Still Pastes, and "Paste" Pastes Twice

In Paste Confirmation Dialog: "Cancel" Still Pastes, and "Paste" Pastes Twice

When I paste content into VS Code, a confirmation dialog appears with two options: "Cancel" and "Paste". However, both buttons behave incorrectly:

Clicking "Cancel" still pastes the content (it should abort the operation and leave the editor unchanged).

Clicking "Paste" pastes the content twice (it should paste only once).

Expected behavior:

"Cancel" → aborts the paste operation, editor content remains unchanged.

"Paste" → pastes the content exactly once.

Actual behavior:

"Cancel" → pastes the content once.

"Paste" → pastes the content twice.

Copy some text from outside VS Code.

In VS Code, trigger the paste action (Cmd+V / Cmd+V).

When the confirmation dialog appears, click either "Cancel" or "Paste".

Observe that "Cancel" still pastes, and "Paste" inserts the content twice.

Screenshots:

Environment:

ElectronBuildId: 14159160
canonical: The paste confirmation dialog is handling the two choices incorrectly, causing cancel to still insert the clipboard content and paste to be applied twice.

title: Feature Request: 'code --install-extension' should disambiguate target when multiple VS Code forks are on PATH (Windows)
document:
Feature Request: 'code --install-extension' should disambiguate target when multiple VS Code forks are on PATH (Windows)

## Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

## VS Code Version: 1.126
## OS Version: Windows 11 Enterprise (26200)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. On Windows, install VS Code (Stable), then install a fork such as Cursor or VSCodium that ships its own `code` / `code.cmd` shim on `PATH`.
2. The fork's `code.cmd` ends up earlier on `PATH` (Cursor's installer prepends its `bin` dir).
3. From any terminal, run:
 
4. The extension installs into the **fork's** extension directory (`%USERPROFILE%\.cursor\extensions\`), not VS Code's (`%USERPROFILE%\.vscode\extensions\`). Same command was intended to target VS Code.

## The problem

`code` on Windows is a `code.cmd` shim that unconditionally invokes `Code.exe --ms-enable-electron-run-as-node ` from the directory it lives in. When multiple VS Code-family editors are installed, the CLI has no way to say **"install into VS Code Stable's extension host specifically"** other than fully qualifying the path to the correct `code.cmd`.

This bites in three real workflows:

1. **Automation scripts** (dotfiles, `install-extensions.ps1`, CI runners) that assume `code --install-extension` targets VS Code — they silently install to whichever fork's shim won the `PATH` race.
2. **Corp-managed machines** where VS Code was installed by SCCM/Intune and a user later installed Cursor — the fork wins because it prepends `PATH` in user scope.
3. **Fresh laptop bootstrap** — `code --list-extensions > extensions.txt` on the old machine, `Get-Content extensions.txt | ForEach-Object { code --install-extension $_ }` on the new one, and the extensions land in the wrong editor.

There is no diagnostic — the fork's CLI happily accepts VS Code extension IDs. The user only notices when the extensions don't appear in VS Code.

## Proposal

Add one or more of:

**(a) `--target` flag** on the CLI (`code --install-extension --target stable | insiders | `) that resolves to the correct extension host regardless of which shim was invoked. Default = the shim's own host (today's behaviour).

**(b) Print a one-line banner** when the invoked `code.cmd` belongs to a non-Microsoft fork, e.g. `note: 'code' resolved to Cursor at C:\Users\...\AppData\Local\Programs\cursor\bin\code.cmd`. Users could then re-run with a fully qualified path. Low-risk, no config surface added.

**(c) Environment variable** `VSCODE_CLI_TARGET=stable|insiders|/abs/path` that a wrapper script or user profile can set to disambiguate. Same effect as (a) without a new flag.

Even just (b) — printing the resolved shim path when a fork owns `code` — would save hours of "why isn't my extension loading" debugging.

## Related but not duplicate

- #40239 asks for a way to enable/disable extensions from a config file, not CLI targeting across forks.
- #5595 asks for platform-scoped settings, not CLI target selection.

Searched issue tracker for `code CLI install-extension fork`, `code shim PATH conflict`, `code --install-extension wrong binary` — no existing report. Happy to close if I missed one.
canonical: The CLI installs extensions into whichever VS Code-family shim appears first on PATH, so `code --install-extension` can silently target the wrong editor instead of the intended VS Code instance.

title: Feature Request: Allow telemetry.telemetryLevel to be overridden at workspace scope (or new workspace-scope setting)
document:
Feature Request: Allow telemetry.telemetryLevel to be overridden at workspace scope (or new workspace-scope setting)

## Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

## VS Code Version: 1.126
## OS Version: Windows 11 Enterprise (26200) — corporate managed

## Steps to Reproduce

1. In a project workspace that handles customer/regulated data, open .vscode/settings.json.
2. Add "telemetry.telemetryLevel": "off".
3. Observe the setting is ignored — value only takes effect when placed in User (application) settings.

## The problem

telemetry.telemetryLevel is registered with ConfigurationScope.APPLICATION and restricted: true (see [src/vs/platform/telemetry/common/telemetryService.ts](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/platform/telemetry/common/telemetryService.ts)). This means:

- A developer working on multiple projects on a corporate-managed machine cannot say "telemetry off for **this** workspace (which contains customer data under NDA) but leave the org-wide policy value in place for other projects."
- The only escape hatches today are the org-wide GPO/enterprise policy (TelemetryLevel), or flipping the user setting each time you switch projects — both are heavy hammers.

Peer editors solve this: JetBrains IDEs allow project-level opt-out of Data Sharing that stacks over the application default. VS Code's own http.proxy, git.enabled, and other sensitive settings support workspace scope with the restricted flag gating trusted-workspace behaviour — so there is precedent for this pattern.

## Proposal

Either:

**(a) Widen the scope** of telemetry.telemetryLevel to ConfigurationScope.WINDOW (or RESOURCE), keeping restricted: true so only trusted workspaces can lower the level. The value would monotonically **decrease** telemetry (workspace can only set ≤ application level) — never raise it above what the user/policy allows.

**(b) Add a new sibling setting** e.g. telemetry.workspaceOverride (enum: inherit | off, default inherit, scope WINDOW, restricted: true). off clamps the effective level to off for the window; anything else inherits the app-level value.

Option (a) is cleaner if the monotonic-decrease guarantee is acceptable. Option (b) is safer if you want to keep the primary setting application-scoped and add opt-in workspace clamp.

## Why this matters for corp/enterprise use

Enterprise VS Code users with mixed public/regulated projects on the same laptop currently either:
- Accept org-wide telemetry level for all workspaces (may leak metadata about regulated project structure).
- Manually toggle the user setting each context switch (error-prone).

A workspace-scoped clamp gives per-project rigor without disturbing the org-wide baseline.

## Not a duplicate of

Searched for telemetry workspace scope, telemetry.telemetryLevel workspace, telemetry per workspace project — no existing issue captures this ask. #260375 covers **tool** enablement, not telemetry scope. If I missed one, happy to close.
canonical: Telemetry cannot be turned off for a single workspace because the telemetry level setting is application-scoped, so it should be allowed to be overridden at workspace scope for trusted workspaces.

title: Renderer crashes with V8 heap OOM (~4 GB cap) — regression introduced in 1.125 (runtime unchanged from 1.124)
document:
Renderer crashes with V8 heap OOM (~4 GB cap) — regression introduced in 1.125 (runtime unchanged from 1.124)

# Renderer crashes with V8 heap OOM (~4 GB cap) — regression introduced in 1.125 (runtime unchanged from 1.124)

## Type: Performance Issue / Crash

**Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled? — Not yet fully confirmed.**
The OOM is in the **renderer** process; my heaviest extension (Sapling SCM) runs in the
separate **extension host**, so disabling extensions may not be conclusive. However, the
regression is reproducible on the *same machine and same workspace* purely by changing the
VS Code version (see A/B below), which points at workbench renderer code, not an extension.

## VS Code Version

- Version: **1.126.0** (also reproduced on 1.125.x)
- Commit: `7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a`
- Date: 2026-06-23
- Electron: 42.3.0 / Chromium: 148.0.7778.180 / Node: 24.15.0
- OS: macOS 26.5.1 (25F80), Apple M4 Pro, **48 GB RAM**

**Last known good: 1.124.2** (identical Electron/Chromium/Node — see "Runtime is unchanged").

## Summary

The workbench **renderer process's V8 JavaScript heap grows until it hits V8's ~4 GB
per-isolate ceiling, then aborts**. The window dies with:

`code: 5` is how Electron surfaces a V8 fatal OOM abort at the window level — the Crashpad
minidump confirms it is an out-of-memory (annotations below).

Physical RAM is irrelevant (48 GB free headroom); V8 caps each renderer heap at 4 GB. GPU
acceleration is **disabled**, so this is not a GPU/WebGL issue. Large integrated-terminal
output (e.g. `ls -la` on a big directory) reliably triggers the final crash, but the heap is
already sitting near 4 GB — the terminal output is the last allocation, not the root cause.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a large multi-root / multi-window workspace (I run ~8 windows; heavy SCM activity).
2. Work normally for a while so the main renderer heap climbs (watch it in
 **Developer: Open Process Explorer** — the window renderer approaches ~4000 MB).
3. Produce a burst of integrated-terminal output (e.g. `ls -la ~/Library`).
4. The renderer crashes; the extension host then exits cleanly ("renderer closed the MessagePort").

Repeats reliably: **20 of 20** recent Crashpad dumps carry `v8-oom` annotations.

## Runtime is unchanged — so this is a VS Code JS regression

Per the community version tracker (ewanharris/vscode-versions), **1.124, 1.125 and 1.126 all
ship the identical runtime**: Electron 42.3.0, Chromium 148.0.7778.180, Node 24.15.0.

Combined with the controlled A/B below, the increase in renderer heap consumption is in
**VS Code's own workbench JavaScript shipped in 1.125**, not in Chromium/V8.

## Controlled A/B (same machine, same workspace, same synced settings)

| VS Code | Result |
|---------|--------|
| 1.124.2 | Stable, no crashes |
| 1.125.x / 1.126.0 | Renderer OOM-crashes; recurs within a session |

A second laptop with the **same synced settings/extensions** does not crash — consistent with
Settings Sync not replicating open windows/folders/panels or on-disk workload, i.e. the second
machine's renderer simply never accumulates to 4 GB.

## Evidence — Crashpad minidump heap annotations (renderer, main isolate)

Extracted from `~/Library/Application Support/Code/Crashpad/completed/*.dmp`:

Embedded V8 GC log at the moment of death (GC freeing nothing, efficiency → 0):

## Interpretation (what to look at)

- **`detached_contexts: 0`, `native_contexts: 3`** → not a leaked-webview / detached-DOM leak.
- **`external_memory: ~24 MB`, `malloced: ~1 MB`** → not ArrayBuffers / external buffers.
- Therefore ~3.88 GB is **ordinary JS objects on the managed V8 heap in the main workbench
 renderer** — a growing in-heap data structure in workbench JS. Given the terminal trigger,
 the integrated-terminal buffer/scrollback or an accumulating workbench cache are prime
 suspects. A live heap snapshot (retainer graph) is needed to name the exact structure; the
 minidump doesn't carry the object graph.

## What I ruled out

- GPU / WebGL — `terminal.integrated.gpuAcceleration` already off; crash is JS-heap.
- Physical memory — 48 GB, crash is at V8's 4 GB per-renderer cap, not OS OOM.
- Settings / extensions config — identical (synced) on an unaffected machine.
- Chromium/Electron/Node — byte-identical across 1.124–1.126.

## Ask

Please investigate what in the 1.125 workbench renderer increased steady-state / peak JS heap
usage enough to reach the 4 GB V8 ceiling. Happy to attach a `.heapsnapshot` and full Crashpad
dumps on request.
canonical: A regression in the workbench renderer causes JavaScript heap memory to grow continuously until the renderer hits V8’s per-process limit and crashes, likely due to an accumulating in-memory cache or terminal-related data structure introduced in the newer release.

title: Telemetry: `agentHost.turnCompleted.permissionLevel` is provider-specific and shouldn't live on the generic turn event
document:
Telemetry: `agentHost.turnCompleted.permissionLevel` is provider-specific and shouldn't live on the generic turn event

### Problem

The `agentHost.turnCompleted` telemetry event (`IAgentHostTurnCompletedEvent` / `IAgentHostTurnCompletedClassification` in `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostTelemetryReporter.ts`) includes a `permissionLevel` field. Unlike the rest of that event (`timeToFirstProgress`, `totalTime`, `result`, `model`), **`permissionLevel` is not generic across agent-host providers** — it is only ever populated for Copilot AH and is empty for Claude and Codex AH.

### Root cause

The value is sourced in shared code from the platform `autoApprove` config key:

Only the Copilot provider writes `SessionConfigKey.AutoApprove`. The other harnesses model permissions/approvals with their own, namespaced config keys:

- **Claude** — `ClaudeSessionConfigKey.PermissionMode` (`permissionMode`): `default` / `acceptEdits` / `bypassPermissions` / `plan` / `auto`
- **Codex** — `CodexSessionConfigKey.ApprovalPolicy` (`codex.approvalPolicy`), plus `codex.sandboxMode`, `codex.networkAccessEnabled`, `codex.webSearchMode`, etc.

So the shared turn-completed path bakes in a Copilot-specific assumption (`autoApprove`), and `permissionLevel` comes back empty for every Claude/Codex turn.

### Why it doesn't belong on the generic event

`agentHost.turnCompleted` is otherwise a harness-agnostic *turn performance* event. `permissionLevel` is a *provider-specific config snapshot*, and there is no lossless normalization across harnesses — Codex's `sandboxMode × networkAccess` axes and Claude's single `permissionMode` axis don't map cleanly onto Copilot's `default` / `autoApprove` / `autopilot`. Putting one provider's config field on the shared classification makes the metric look generic when it isn't, and silently yields empty data for 2 of 3 providers.

### Proposed direction

- Remove `permissionLevel` from the shared `agentHost.turnCompleted` event so it stays purely harness-agnostic (timing / result / model).
- Emit permission/config telemetry from **provider-specific code** instead (e.g. `agentHost.copilot.*`), so each harness reports its own config faithfully and the values are accurate per provider. This mirrors the provider-namespaced pattern (e.g. the proposed `agentHost.claude.subagentResolved`).

Internal context / scorecard gap: microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog#8205.

### Code references

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostTelemetryReporter.ts` — `permissionLevel` on `IAgentHostTurnCompletedEvent` + classification
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentSideEffects.ts` — `_getTurnTelemetryContext` reads `SessionConfigKey.AutoApprove`
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostTurnTracker.ts` — plumbs `permissionLevel` through `turnStarted` → `turnCompleted`
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/common/sessionConfigKeys.ts`, `common/claudeSessionConfigKeys.ts`, `node/codex/codexSessionConfigKeys.ts` — the divergent per-provider key namespaces
canonical: The generic turn-completed telemetry event incorrectly includes a Copilot-specific permission setting, which makes the field empty or misleading for other providers because their approval and permission modes are configured differently.

title: Polish: chat tab bar visibility for closed chats and diverged chat title
document:
Polish: chat tab bar visibility for closed chats and diverged chat title

Polish item tracking the chat tab bar visibility behavior in the Agents window session header.

## Context

Addressed in #323789. Previously the chat tab strip disappeared once only a single open chat remained, hiding closed-but-reopenable chats and any chat whose title had diverged from the session title.

## Behavior after the PR

The tab strip (a single shared observable `IActiveSession.shouldShowChatTabs`) is shown when:
- the session has more than one chat (counting closed, non-tool chats), or
- its single remaining chat's title diverged from the session title.

Chat-to-chat navigation (next/previous chat, the `Ctrl+Tab` switcher) is gated separately on `SessionHasMultipleOpenChatsContext` (more than one *open* tab), so those chords stay a no-op / fall back to session navigation when only a single open chat remains.

## Possible follow-up polish

- [ ] Validate the visual transition when the strip appears/disappears (no layout jump in the header).
- [ ] Confirm the trailing **New Chat** / **Conversations** controls move cleanly between the header and the strip across all the visibility transitions (closed chats, diverged title, drafts).
- [ ] Double-check keyboard navigation precedence (`Ctrl+Tab`, `Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+]`/`[`) in the single-open-chat edge cases.
- [ ] Verify behavior with tool-origin (subagent) chats present.

Ref: #323789
canonical: The chat tab strip can disappear when only one open chat remains even though there are closed reopenable chats or a chat whose title no longer matches the session title, causing those chats to become hidden.
