GitHub Copilot model selector doesn't indicate model context window size if unconfigurable in v1.126

In v1.126 supposedly there is a [unified model customization picker](https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_126#_unified-model-customization-picker) that allows you configure thinking effort and context window size in the same panel. But the corresponding [simplified model hover](https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_126#_simplified-model-hover) does not show any indication of context window size.

**Thus for models that don't allow customization, there is no longer any indication of context window size.**

1. Click the model selector.
2. Hover over "Claude Haiku 4.5".

**Expected:** Some indication of the context window size of "Claude Haiku 4.5".

**Actual:** No indication of the context window size of "Claude Haiku 4.5".

I personally happen to remember the context window size of Claude Haiku 4.5, but I can't (or shouldn't need to) have all of them memorized.

This is especially important if I'm working with another more powerful model and I want to "drop down" to a lesser model; I need to know its context window size to know if it can support the current context!
canonical: The model selector no longer shows a model’s context window size for models that cannot be customized, making it hard to tell whether a model can handle the current conversation context.

title: "Try Again" in Chat does not allow selection of new model
document:
"Try Again" in Chat does not allow selection of new model

ElectronBuildId: 14500164

Feature: Chat
Selected Model: Any

1. Configure a custom Chat model with editor-scoped/per-model configuration, such as a custom endpoint model with reasoning effort or other model-specific settings.
2. Start a Chat request using that custom model and confirm that the initial request uses the expected selected model configuration.
3. Trigger a resend/follow-up path such as “Try Again”, a confirmation response button, a sample request, slash command sample, or another Chat UI flow that sends using a different widget model.
4. Observe that the UI still appears to have the same model selected, but the follow-up request is not routed to the newly-selected model.

## User story

I use VS Code Chat with custom language models configured through `chatLanguageModels.json`. Some of these models require per-model settings, such as reasoning effort or other model-specific options.

While testing a custom endpoint, I found that a normal first request used the expected model configuration. But after using retry/follow-up flows, the request no longer behaved as if the selected model configuration was being honored. From the user’s perspective, the Chat UI still appeared to have the correct model selected, but the request sent by the retry/follow-up path was not equivalent to a fresh send with that same visible model selection.

This is confusing because the model picker state suggests one thing, while the request options used by some resend/follow-up paths can behave differently.

## Expected behavior

Any Chat flow that sends or resends using the currently selected model should preserve both:

- the selected model ID
- the editor-scoped model configuration associated with that model

The visible model picker state should match the request options used for retry/resend/confirmation/follow-up flows.

## Actual behavior

Some call sites pass only `userSelectedModelId` from the widget’s selected model state. They do not also pass `userSelectedModelConfiguration`.

That can drop editor-scoped configuration and cause follow-up requests to use configuration that does not match the visible model picker state.

## Technical notes

`IChatSendRequestOptions` supports both `userSelectedModelId` and `userSelectedModelConfiguration`.

The service layer can fall back to global model configuration when only a model ID is provided, but that is not equivalent to preserving editor-scoped configuration from the active Chat widget.

Several widget-backed resend/confirmation paths construct request options independently. This makes it easy for some paths to pass the model ID without the associated scoped configuration.

A safer pattern is to centralize construction of selected model request options so the model ID and scoped configuration are always passed together.

## Impact

This affects custom model providers and any flow where per-model configuration matters. It is especially visible with custom endpoints where configuration controls important runtime behavior such as reasoning effort, context/output settings, or provider-specific options.
canonical: Chat retry and follow-up actions ignore the currently selected model’s scoped configuration, so a resend can use the right model ID but lose per-model settings and route the request differently than the visible picker implies.

title: Async subagents can be blocked
document:
Async subagents can be blocked

Symptoms:

A session stops and says it's waiting for an async subagent to complete. The subagent never completes. If you check the events.jsonl "Open Copilot CLI State File", the last entry is a `permission.requested` that is never resolved.

The bug involves
- Main session is unsubscribed by client
- It goes idle while waiting for the async subagent
- It's evicted from AH memory
- Subagent completes and tries to wake up the main agent again
- This doesn't work when it has been evicted

This looks really similar to https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323738 but they are not the same
canonical: An async subagent can leave the main session permanently waiting when the parent session unsubscribes, goes idle, gets evicted from memory, and is then unable to be woken back up when the subagent finishes.

title: Agent tools picker restores default tools after deselection, causing unwanted tool payloads
document:
Agent tools picker restores default tools after deselection, causing unwanted tool payloads

## Summary

In VS Code Insiders, deselecting built-in agent tools in the Configure Tools picker does not persist.

After deselecting all tools or reducing the selection to Codebase only, confirming with OK, and reopening the picker, the default built-in tools are active again.

Adding additional tools is remembered. Removing default tools is not.

## Expected behavior

The tools selected or deselected in Configure Tools should determine which tools are available for the current request, as documented.

## Actual behavior

The tool picker restores a large built-in default set after deselection.

Chat Debug View confirms that the actual request still contains 28 tools, including unrelated notebook, browser, task, edit, terminal, and extension tools.

## Reproduction

1. Open Chat and select Agent.
2. Open Configure Tools.
3. Deselect all default tools, or leave only Codebase selected.
4. Confirm with OK.
5. Reopen Configure Tools.
6. Observe that the default tools are active again.
7. Send a request and inspect it in Chat Debug View.

## Observed request impact

The Chat Debug View showed:

- `tools (28)`
- `prompt_tokens: 10180`
- `maxPromptTokens: 10240`
- model: `qwen3-coder:30b`
- endpoint: `http://localhost:11434/v1/chat/completions`

This leaves almost no input budget for a local model and makes a controlled single-tool test impossible.

The same behavior also affects cloud-model workflows, where unexpectedly retained tool definitions and context can increase input-token and AI-credit consumption.

## Environment

- VS Code Insiders: `1.127.0-insider`
- Commit: `4fe60c8b1cdac1c4c174f2fb180d0d758272d713`
- Windows: `10.0.26200`
- Workspace size: more than 20,000 files
- Custom Endpoint model: local Ollama `qwen3-coder:30b`
- Copilot Chat file logging enabled

## Notes

A direct Ollama OpenAI-compatible API request with one simple tool returned valid structured `tool_calls`.

The issue appears to be in tool-selection persistence or request construction inside VS Code/Copilot Chat, not in Ollama’s basic tool-calling support.

I can provide redacted Chat Debug View exports and screenshots.
canonical: Deselecting built-in agent tools does not persist, so reopening the tool picker restores the default tool set and the request is sent with unwanted extra tools, likely due to incorrect tool-selection state persistence or request construction.

title: Copilot agent session worktree creation silently fails when repository is in detached HEAD state
document:
Copilot agent session worktree creation silently fails when repository is in detached HEAD state

## Bug Report

**VS Code version:** 1.123.0
**Copilot Chat version:** 0.51.0
**OS:** Windows

---

### Describe the bug

When the workspace repository is in detached HEAD state, starting a new Copilot CLI agent session fails to create an isolated worktree. The session silently falls back to `isolationEnabled: false` with no visible error to the user.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Have a git repository where the main working directory is in detached HEAD (e.g. `git checkout `)
2. Start a new Copilot agent session that triggers worktree creation

### Expected behavior

The worktree is created successfully (the underlying `git worktree add` command succeeds), and the session runs with isolation enabled.

### Actual behavior

The Copilot Chat extension log shows:

…and the session starts with `isolationEnabled: false, worktreePath: undefined`.

Notably, the `git worktree add` command itself **succeeds** — the worktree directory is created on disk — but the result is discarded.

### Root cause

In `ChatSessionWorktreeService._createWorktree`, after `gitService.createWorktree()` succeeds, the result is only returned if `activeRepository.headBranchName` is truthy:

In detached HEAD, `headBranchName` is `undefined`, so the condition fails even though the worktree was created successfully. However, `headBranchName` is only actually used as a fallback when `baseBranch` is not provided:

The guard is overly strict — it should not require `headBranchName` when `baseBranch` is already supplied by the caller, or when the worktree was successfully created regardless.

### Suggested fix

Or more robustly, separate the guard from the fallback so that a missing `headBranchName` only affects `baseBranchName` computation rather than discarding the entire result.
canonical: Creating a Copilot agent session in a repository with a detached HEAD can silently disable worktree isolation because the successfully created worktree is discarded when the current branch name is missing.

title: Agents window: model/agent selection is shared across chats in a session instead of per chat
document:
Agents window: model/agent selection is shared across chats in a session instead of per chat

## Describe the bug

In the Agents window, a single session can contain multiple peer chats (tabs). Changing the **agent** or **model** in one chat also changes it in the other chats of the same session. These decisions should be scoped per chat.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open the Agents window and start an agent-host session that supports multiple chats.
2. Add a second chat to the session (so the session has 2+ chat tabs).
3. In chat A, change the model (or the agent).
4. Switch to chat B.

**Expected:** Chat B keeps its own model/agent selection, independent of chat A.
**Actual:** The model/agent picker reflects (and is driven by) a single shared selection, so changing it in one chat appears to change it in the others.

## Root cause

The provider's write path (`setModel`/`setAgent`) already targets the active chat's resource, and each `IChat` carries its own `modelId`/`mode` observables. However, `VisibleSession` (the `IActiveSession` implementation that the Agents window model/agent pickers bind to) exposed the **session/default chat's** `modelId`/`mode` rather than the active chat's. So the read path ignored which chat tab was active, making the selection appear shared.

## Area

- Agents window (`src/vs/sessions`)
canonical: The Agents window incorrectly binds the model and agent pickers to the session-wide default chat instead of the active chat, causing changes in one chat tab to appear shared across all chats in the session.

title: Copilot completion feedback
document:
Copilot completion feedback

## Copilot Completion Feedback
### Describe the issue, feedback, or steps to reproduce it:

title: GitHub-managed custom model with Vision enabled is exposed as Tools-only and rejects image attachments
document:
GitHub-managed custom model with Vision enabled is exposed as Tools-only and rejects image attachments

### Related issue
Related to #316581, but this report is specifically about **Vision capability being lost/ignored** for GitHub-managed custom models.

### What happened?
I have **three different setups** for effectively the same Qwen 3.7 Plus vision-capable model family:

1. **GitHub-managed custom model -> LiteLLM proxy**
2. **GitHub-managed custom model -> Alibaba Cloud directly**
3. **Direct custom endpoint in VS Code**

The issue reproduces with the first **two** setups, and the third setup works.

In practice:
- the **GitHub-managed LiteLLM-backed model** is exposed as **Tools** only
- the **GitHub-managed Alibaba-direct model** is also exposed as **Tools** only
- but the **same model configured directly as a custom endpoint in VS Code** shows **Tools + Vision** and works with image input inside of vscode

So this does **not** appear to be specific to LiteLLM.

### Repro steps
1. Register a custom model in GitHub custom models preview against either:
 - an OpenAI-compatible LiteLLM endpoint, or
 - Alibaba Cloud directly.
2. Enable **Vision** in the model configuration.
3. Use the model from a GitHub-managed Copilot surface with an image attachment.
4. Observe that the image is rejected with messages like:
 - `The current model couldn't process this image's format.`
 - `Removed image attachment(s) from conversation history because the model rejected them (vision unavailable for this model or organization).`

### Expected behavior
If the model is configured with **Vision** and the backing endpoint supports image input, Copilot clients should expose and honor the Vision capability consistently.

### Actual behavior
The GitHub-managed model path appears to drop or ignore the Vision capability:
- GitHub custom model configuration shows **Vision enabled**
- VS Code model catalog shows the GitHub-managed models as **Tools** only
- the same model added directly in VS Code as a custom endpoint shows **Tools + Vision**
- image requests fail before they appear to reach the provider/backend

### Why this seems like a GitHub/Copilot integration issue
This does not look like a backend/provider issue:
- direct requests to the LiteLLM endpoint succeed with image input
- the same model configured directly in VS Code as a custom endpoint also succeeds with image input
- the issue reproduces for **both**:
 - GitHub-managed model backed by LiteLLM
 - GitHub-managed model backed directly by Alibaba Cloud

So this looks like a capability propagation/runtime bug in the GitHub custom model integration rather than a LiteLLM-specific or provider-specific issue.

### Screenshots

#### 1. GitHub custom model configuration shows Vision enabled

Description:
- Model examples include:
 - `qwen3.7-plus (alibaba_cloud)`
 - `qwen-3.7-plus (litellm)`
- Endpoint type: `Chat completions API`
- Capabilities enabled in the GitHub UI:
 - `Tool calling`
 - `Streaming`
 - `Vision`
 - `Thinking`

#### 2. Copilot CLI / chat rejection after image attachment

Description:
- After attaching an image, Copilot reports:
 - `The current model couldn't process this image's format.`
 - `Removed image attachment(s) from conversation history because the model rejected them (vision unavailable for this model or organization).`

#### 3. VS Code capability mismatch between GitHub-managed models and direct endpoint

Description:
- The GitHub-managed custom model entries appear as:
 - `qwen-3.7-plus-litellm` -> **Tools** only
 - `qwen3.7-plus` -> **Tools** only
- A direct custom endpoint entry for the same backing model appears with **Tools + Vision**.
- Same model family and capability, but different Copilot capability exposure depending on whether the model is routed through the GitHub-managed custom model path or added directly as a custom endpoint.
canonical: GitHub-managed custom models are not propagating the enabled vision capability to Copilot clients, so models that support images are exposed as tools-only and image attachments are rejected before reaching the backend.

title: Session file corrupted: negative data.tokensRemoved rejected by SDK ("Number must be greater than or equal to 0")
document:
Session file corrupted: negative data.tokensRemoved rejected by SDK ("Number must be greater than or equal to 0")

## Bug Description

The Copilot CLI/SDK session loader (`@github/copilot` SDK, used by the Copilot Chat extension's Agents window) rejects an entire session file when an event's `data.tokensRemoved` value is **negative**, with:

The schema enforces `tokensRemoved >= 0`, but the SDK itself can write **negative** `tokensRemoved` deltas into `events.jsonl` during context compaction/token accounting. The session is then unloadable — the whole conversation history is lost over a single accounting field.

This is the same root-cause family as #312793 (`data.compactionTokensUsed.input: Required`) and #319244 (`batchSize: Number must be greater than 0`): strict Zod validation on **token-accounting metadata** rejects the entire session rather than the conversation turns. #315692 is the user-visible "session file is corrupted" symptom.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run a long Copilot CLI / Agents session that goes through one or more context compactions (large sessions, tens of thousands of events).
2. The SDK writes one or more events with a negative `data.tokensRemoved` value into the session `events.jsonl`.
3. Open/resume that session in the VS Code Agents window (or via the Copilot Chat extension).
4. Loading fails: `Session file is corrupted (line N: data.tokensRemoved: Number must be greater than or equal to 0)`.

## Actual Behavior

- The session cannot be opened.
- Because of the fallback path described in #319244, the user may instead silently get an empty (0-turn) session — all history lost.

## Expected Behavior

- `tokensRemoved` should never be persisted as a negative number, OR
- The loader should floor it at `0` (clamp) rather than reject the whole session, since this is non-essential accounting metadata, not conversation content.

## Root Cause / Suggested Fix

Two layers, either fixes it:

1. **SDK serialization (preferred):** clamp `tokensRemoved` (and sibling token-accounting fields) to `>= 0` when writing events, so corrupt records are never produced. The delta going negative looks like an off-by accounting bug in compaction.
2. **SDK loader / Zod schema:** make token-accounting fields tolerant — `.catch(0)` / `.transform(v => Math.max(0, v))` instead of a hard `min(0)` rejection — so a metadata glitch can't make an entire multi-turn session unloadable. This also resolves the broader family in #312793 / #319244.

## Workaround (for already-corrupted files)

Floor the negative values to `0` in the session file (`~/.copilot/session-state//events.jsonl`), back up first:

The session then loads normally. (In one real 60k-line session this affected 12 records.)

## Environment

- VS Code Agents window + GitHub Copilot Chat extension, Copilot CLI sessions (`@github/copilot` SDK).
- macOS (Darwin); the field/schema is platform-independent.

## Related

- #312793 — `compactionTokensUsed.input: Required` (same family)
- #319244 — corrupted session silently loads as empty instead of surfacing the error
- #315692 — "session file is corrupted" symptom in Agents window
canonical: The session loader rejects an otherwise valid conversation history because the SDK writes negative token-accounting values into session event data, and the strict validation treats that metadata error as fatal.

title: Per-session VSCODE_TARGET_SESSION_LOG template variable sits inside the cached system prefix and cold-busts BYOK Anthropic prompt caching
document:
Per-session VSCODE_TARGET_SESSION_LOG template variable sits inside the cached system prefix and cold-busts BYOK Anthropic prompt caching

### Type
Bug / performance + cost regression (volatile per-session value inside the cache-controlled prefix).

### Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?
Yes — Copilot Chat agent-mode prompt assembly with a BYOK Anthropic model.

### Version
- VS Code: 1.126.0 (commit 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a)
- GitHub Copilot Chat: 0.54.0
- OS: macOS (expected on all platforms)
- Model path: BYOK Anthropic (Claude Opus 4.8) via `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`

### Summary
Copilot appends a "template variables" section to the **end of the single cached system block** so the model can resolve `{{VSCODE_*}}` references:

`VSCODE_TARGET_SESSION_LOG` resolves to a **per-conversation** debug-log path whose `` changes per conversation — and, in practice, **multiple times within a single conversation**. Because it sits **before the system block's lone `cache_control` breakpoint**, every UUID change invalidates the entire ~227K-char cached prefix, forcing a full Anthropic **cache write** (1.25x) instead of a **read** (0.1x).

### Impact
On a real BYOK Anthropic workload (~227K-char cached system prefix):
- **29.5% of turns** were full-prefix cache writes; those accounted for **~95% of all `cache_creation` tokens**.
- The UUID rotated **2-9x within single conversations** (25 distinct UUIDs across the capture window).
- Reuse factor (cache_read / cache_creation) sat at **~2.2x** vs the 20-25x a well-tuned Anthropic prefix achieves.

Representative consecutive turns (only the UUID changed; system prefix byte-identical after masking it):

### Steps to reproduce
1. Use Copilot agent mode with a BYOK Anthropic model and a sizeable cached prefix.
2. Capture consecutive raw `/v1/messages` request bodies (e.g. a transparent local forward proxy on `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`).
3. Find a turn where `cache_creation_input_tokens` spikes and `cache_read_input_tokens` is 0.
4. Diff the system block against the previous turn: the only divergence is the `debug-logs/` segment in the `VSCODE_TARGET_SESSION_LOG` line.

### Root cause (from decompiling the shipped extension, 1.126.0)
`buildTemplateVariablesContext` appends the block to the cached system text. The resolver:

`getSessionDir -> _getDebugLogsDir` computes the path **unconditionally** from `storageUri ?? globalStorageUri`; it is **not** gated by `github.copilot.chat.agentDebugLog.fileLogging.enabled` (that setting only changes the logger's flush/buffer setup). The value is also **not** a `process.env` override despite the `VSCODE_` name (it is a resolver Map key; zero `process.env['VSCODE_*']` reads in the bundle). So no setting or env var removes it.

### Why this is questionable beyond cost
- **Eager, not lazy:** declared on every request even when no loaded skill references `{{VSCODE_TARGET_SESSION_LOG}}`.
- **Info leak:** a per-machine absolute filesystem path is sent to the model provider for no functional benefit when unused.
- **Volatile-in-cached-region:** the actual defect — a value that changes per conversation placed inside the static cached prefix.

### Requested fix (any one of)
1. **Only inject a template variable when a loaded skill/instruction actually references it** (lazy resolution), or
2. **Place the template-variables block AFTER the `cache_control` breakpoint** (or in a separate uncached block), or
3. **Keep `{{VSCODE_TARGET_SESSION_LOG}}` as a stable logical token and resolve it client-side** after the response rather than baking the resolved per-session path into the request, or
4. Make the session-log path **stable for the workspace** rather than per-conversation when it must be sent.

Option 2 fixes the cost issue with no loss of capability.

### Related
Same byte-stability principle as microsoft/vscode#313920 (cache_control sentinel must not change the byte-exact prefix across turns), different trigger. This is one of three independent BYOK Anthropic cache-busters we found (workspace-tree injection and non-additive breakpoint re-anchoring are the other two).
canonical: A per-session debug-log path is being injected into the Anthropic cached system prefix, so the prefix changes between turns and repeatedly breaks prompt cache reuse.

title: Copilot Chat (Agent Mode, Opus 4.8) output stream degenerates into infinite single-word repetition ("court" x100+) mid-session during large file read, agent unable to self-recover
document:
Copilot Chat (Agent Mode, Opus 4.8) output stream degenerates into infinite single-word repetition ("court" x100+) mid-session during large file read, agent unable to self-recover

During an active agent-mode session reviewing a large PHP file (~13,000 lines), Copilot Chat's output stream began repeating a single unrelated word ("court") dozens of times in a row, interleaved with brief narrative text claiming the repetition was a benign "output glitch" and instructing itself to "ignore it and continue." The agent did continue issuing tool calls (file reads) between repetition bursts, but the chat transcript became unreadable and unreliable as a record of what the agent was actually doing. This closely matches prior reports of agent-mode repetition loops but in this case the model also generated self-referential commentary dismissing the artifact rather than stalling outright, making it harder to tell whether underlying reasoning was still intact.
canonical: The agent chat can enter a self-reinforcing repetition loop during long file analysis, repeatedly emitting the same unrelated word and adding misleading self-justifications instead of recovering.
The issue is that the application can fail to load or update existing data correctly because the expected content is missing or inaccessible, likely due to a broken or mismatched data source.

title: bug: TreeView selection becomes corrupted when a context key update changes item context menu visibility
document:
bug: TreeView selection becomes corrupted when a context key update changes item context menu visibility

Updating a context key that affects TreeView item context menu visibility causes TreeView selection corruption.

A minimal reproduction extension is attached.

In this reproduction, a context key is updated when `selection.length === 5`. The update is intentionally delayed to separate it from the original selection change event and demonstrate that the issue is triggered by the context update itself rather than by selection processing.

When the context key changes and the context menu contribution visibility changes by `when` expression:

- The visual selection becomes corrupted (only one item stays selected).
- `onDidChangeSelection` is not fired for this change.
- Internally, `TreeView.selection` still reports the correct items.
- Subsequent selection changes are no longer reliably reflected.
- The original selection is eventually lost.

[treeview-selection-contextchange-repro.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29488333/treeview-selection-contextchange-repro.zip)
canonical: Updating a context key that changes TreeView item context menu visibility corrupts the visible selection and desynchronizes it from the underlying selection state, likely because the context update triggers a tree refresh that drops the multi-selection.

title: Feature Request: Auto-encrypt apiKey on save, group-level defaults, and auto-discover models for BYOK (chatLanguageModels.json)
document:
Feature Request: Auto-encrypt apiKey on save, group-level defaults, and auto-discover models for BYOK (chatLanguageModels.json)

﻿## Feature Request: Three enhancements for BYOK (`chatLanguageModels.json`) configuration experience

### Suggestion 1: Auto-detect and auto-encrypt plaintext `apiKey` on file save / Provide a command to bulk-set encrypted `apiKey`

**Problem:**

When users manually edit `chatLanguageModels.json` to configure custom providers, they may write the `apiKey` in plaintext (e.g., when migrating from third-party tools or batch-importing many providers). Currently, VS Code's BYOK system only encrypts `apiKey` via `SecretStorageService` when provider groups are created through the **UI wizard or programmatic API** — the `_resolveLanguageModelProviderGroup` method handles this, but it is never triggered on file save.

For users managing many custom endpoints, manually clicking through the UI for each API key is impractical.

**Desired improvement — auto-encrypt on file save (plaintext in file → auto encrypted):**

After saving, the system should automatically:
1. Detect plaintext `apiKey` values in provider group configurations.
2. Migrate them to `SecretStorageService` (as it already does in `_resolveLanguageModelProviderGroup`).
3. Rewrite the file with encrypted placeholder references (e.g., `"${input:chat.lm.secret.}"`).

Additionally, exposing a **command** (e.g., `workbench.action.providers.setApiKey`) accessible via `Ctrl+P` would allow external tooling and batch scripts to programmatically set encrypted API keys.

**Relevant code:**
- `_resolveLanguageModelProviderGroup` in `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/languageModels.ts`
- `LanguageModelsConfigurationService` in `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/languageModelsConfigurationService.ts` — file watcher exists but no auto-encryption.
- `updateLanguageModelsProviderGroupApiKey` — existing UI-only command.

---

### Suggestion 2: Add `apiUrl` and `defaults` at the provider group level to avoid per-model duplication

**Problem:**

When multiple models share the same base endpoint and capabilities under one provider group, each model must still repeat `url`, `maxInputTokens`, `maxOutputTokens`, `toolCalling`, `vision`, etc. This creates excessive boilerplate:

**Desired improvement — inherited defaults at group level:**

The resolution order should be: **per-model field > `defaults` at group level > provider-level fallback**. This would dramatically reduce boilerplate and make bulk editing much easier, especially for self-hosted endpoints with many models.

**Relevant code:**
- `CustomEndpointModelProviderConfig` / `_CustomOAIModelConfig` in `extensions/copilot/src/extension/byok/vscode-node/customEndpointProvider.ts` and `customOAIProvider.ts` — per-model `url` is currently required for each entry.
- `getAllModels` in those same files — builds per-model info from `configuration.models`.

---

### Suggestion 3: Add `apiListModels` endpoint to auto-discover and sync available models

**Problem:**

Currently, when a user configures a new provider (especially a self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoint), they must manually enumerate every model ID in the `models` array. For endpoints that support model listing (e.g., `GET /v1/models`), this is redundant. The user has to keep the list in sync manually whenever the upstream adds or removes models.

**Desired improvement — auto-fetch from model list endpoint:**

When `apiListModels` is specified, the system would:
1. Fetch available models from the endpoint (similar to how `AbstractOpenAICompatibleLMProvider.getModelsFromEndpoint` works).
2. Merge the server-side model list with the user's local `models` array.
3. Use the user-configured `hidden` property (or a new `show`/`hide` toggle in the model picker UI) to control visibility, rather than requiring the user to maintain a complete list.

This approach — **maintain model list automatically, let the user just toggle show/hide** — would be far more efficient and user-friendly for self-hosted providers that frequently add or update models.

**Relevant code:**
- `AbstractOpenAICompatibleLMProvider.getModelsFromEndpoint` in `extensions/copilot/src/extension/byok/vscode-node/abstractLanguageModelChatProvider.ts` — already fetches models from a discovery URL; could be extended to support user-provided `apiListModels`.
- `LanguageModelsService.setModelHidden` / `setGroupHidden` in `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/languageModels.ts` — existing show/hide infrastructure for models.
- `getAllModels` in `customEndpointProvider.ts` — when `configuration.url` is set, falls back to `super.getAllModels()` which fetches from endpoint; this pattern could be extended.
canonical: Plaintext API keys are not automatically migrated to secret storage when a provider configuration is saved manually, and the current model/provider configuration requires repetitive per-model settings instead of inheriting group defaults or discovering available models from the endpoint.

title: Font width issue with zoom level
document:
Font width issue with zoom level

I'm currently running a virtualization lab on RHEL 9.8, using VSCode and Cascadia Code for the font on linux, certain zoom modes force the font to look super skinny and warped. I could provide screenshots, but this issue reporter does not appear to have that in here anywhere.

So, steps to reproduce:
1) install VSCode on linux (would hope u guys know how)
2) install Cascadia code fonts
sudo curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/microsoft/cascadia-code/releases/latest | grep browser_download_url | grep zip | cut -d '"' -f 4 | xargs sudo curl -L -o /tmp/cascadia.zip
sudo unzip /tmp/cascadia.zip -d /usr/share/fonts/cascadia
sudo fc-cache -f -v
fc-list | grep -i cascadia
3) update settings.json to target Cascadia Code
"editor.fontFamily": "Cascadia Code"
4) use CTRL + and CTRL - to cycle through zoom settings.

I asked copilot what it thinks and it gave me a bunch of useless suggestions that either didn't work, or were trying to resolve it elsewhere using DPI type commands.

It's not an XRDP issue, nor an issue with the font itself, the renderer for the editor is squeezing the font by like 25-33%? I can't even tell what zoom setting it's on, but the font issue occurs over Hyper-V window, OR using RDP window.

I like this font, I always use it, I always wind up installing a bunch of Microsoft stuff on linux, edge, vscode, extensions... would be nice to have a solution for this particular issue.
canonical: Zooming the editor on Linux causes Cascadia Code to be rendered with incorrect horizontal scaling, making the font appear unnaturally narrow and warped.

title: Add enterprise policies for terminal agent sandbox settings
document:
Add enterprise policies for terminal agent sandbox settings

Several terminal agent sandbox settings are restricted preview settings but do not all have enterprise policy registrations. This makes it harder for administrators to centrally disable or lock down sandbox-related behavior, especially when chat preview features are disabled.

This issue tracks adding policy metadata/default restricted values for the terminal agent sandbox settings, including:

- Windows sandbox enablement
- Network and unsandboxed-command allowances
- Retry-with-network behavior
- Auto-approval behavior
- Linux/macOS/Windows filesystem sandbox settings
- Advanced sandbox runtime settings

The policy values should default to disabling or emptying these settings when `chat_preview_features_enabled` is false.
canonical: Enterprise policy support is missing for several terminal agent sandbox settings, preventing administrators from centrally disabling or locking down sandbox behavior when preview features are turned off.

title: Add enterprise policies for terminal agent sandbox settings
document:
Add enterprise policies for terminal agent sandbox settings

Several terminal agent sandbox settings are restricted preview settings but do not all have enterprise policy registrations. This makes it harder for administrators to centrally disable or lock down sandbox-related behavior, especially when chat preview features are disabled.

This issue tracks adding policy metadata/default restricted values for the terminal agent sandbox settings, including:

- Windows sandbox enablement
- Network and unsandboxed-command allowances
- Retry-with-network behavior
- Auto-approval behavior
- Linux/macOS/Windows filesystem sandbox settings
- Advanced sandbox runtime settings

The policy values should default to disabling or emptying these settings when `chat_preview_features_enabled` is false.
canonical: Enterprise policies are missing for several terminal agent sandbox settings, preventing administrators from centrally disabling or locking down sandbox behavior when chat preview features are turned off.

title: Cache breakpoint re-anchoring rewrites (instead of extends) the BYOK Anthropic prompt cache in long agent sessions
document:
Cache breakpoint re-anchoring rewrites (instead of extends) the BYOK Anthropic prompt cache in long agent sessions

### Type
Bug — cost regression (prompt-cache write churn from non-additive breakpoint movement) on the BYOK Anthropic path.

### Environment
- VS Code: 1.126.0 (commit 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a)
- GitHub Copilot Chat: 
- OS: macOS (expected on all platforms)
- Model: BYOK Anthropic (Claude Opus 4.8) via `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`
- Occurs with all other extensions disabled: yes

### Summary
Anthropic prompt caching is explicit and capped at **4 `cache_control` breakpoints** per request.
As an agent conversation grows, the Copilot prompt renderer re-distributes those 4 breakpoints —
generally moving the last one toward the end so the bulk of accumulated history stays cached. That
intent is correct. The **implementation is not byte-stable**: when the breakpoint re-anchors, the
prefix bytes *before* the new anchor change, so Anthropic treats it as a **cache write of the entire
prefix** (`cache_creation_input_tokens` spikes, `cache_read_input_tokens` drops to 0) instead of an
**incremental cache extension** of the already-cached region.

The specific trigger observed is a **synthetic whitespace anchor block** appearing/disappearing. The
renderer injects `{"type":"text","text":" ","cache_control":{"type":"ephemeral"}}` when the last
content block isn't a valid cache anchor, and removes/relocates it on a later turn. That single
structural change to the cached region forces a full re-write.

### Impact
On a real BYOK Anthropic workload (Claude Opus 4.8), over a clean-slate window of 155 consecutive
captured requests:
- ~8.4% of turns were full-prefix cache **writes** (`cache_creation` > `cache_read`), distinct from
 a separate workspace-tree bust (filed as the companion issue).
- These re-write turns appeared on **long conversations** (request bodies ~1.8 MB) and cost a full
 `cache_creation` of the entire prefix (observed up to ~560K tokens at 1.25× input rate) where an
 incremental extension would have cost almost nothing.
- Reuse factor (cache_read ÷ cache_creation) on the affected window sat at ~7.9× versus the 20–25× a
 well-tuned Anthropic prefix achieves.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Use agent mode with a BYOK Anthropic model and a long-running conversation (many turns, large
 accumulated history).
2. Capture consecutive raw `/v1/messages` request bodies (e.g. via a transparent local forward proxy
 on `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`).
3. Find a turn where `cache_creation_input_tokens` spikes and `cache_read_input_tokens` is 0.
4. Diff that body against the previous turn. The first divergence is **inside the message history**
 (not the system prompt), at a `cache_control` marker — typically a
 `{"type":"text","text":" ","cache_control":...}` block present in one turn and absent/moved in the
 next.

### Root cause (from the shipped extension, 1.126.0)
The Anthropic wire-format renderer places `cache_control` on the last cacheable block and walks
**from the end backward** to position the (capped) breakpoints:

// place the breakpoint on the last non-deferred block
for (let a = n.length - 1; a >= 0; a--) if (!n[a].defer_loading) { n[a].cache_control = r; break }
// …and a synthetic whitespace anchor when the tail isn't cacheable:
r && c1n(r) ? r.cache_control = {type:"ephemeral"}
: e.push({ type:"text", text:" ", cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} })

Because the 4-breakpoint cap forces re-distribution as history grows, and the synthetic `" "` anchor
is added/removed depending on the tail block type, the **bytes of the cached prefix change between
turns**. Anthropic caches the longest *identical* prefix, so any change before the anchor
invalidates everything after it → a full re-write rather than an additive extension.

### Why "just pin the breakpoint early" is not the fix
Anthropic only caches *up to* the last breakpoint; everything after it is billed at full input rate
every turn. Pinning the breakpoint early would keep the small static prefix cached but leave the
entire **growing** history uncached and re-billed at 1× every turn — worse than current behavior in
long sessions. The correct behavior is to move the breakpoint **forward additively**, preserving the
already-cached prefix bytes, so re-anchoring extends the cache instead of rewriting it.

### Requested fix
1. Make breakpoint re-anchoring **byte-stable / additive**: when moving the trailing breakpoint
 forward, do not alter any bytes before the previously-cached anchor, so Anthropic extends the
 existing cache rather than re-creating it.
2. Specifically, stop inserting/removing the synthetic `{"type":"text","text":" "}` anchor in a way
 that mutates the cached prefix across turns; use a stable anchor position.
3. Consider pinning one of the 4 breakpoints permanently at the end of the **static** system/tools
 prefix (that region never changes) and using the remaining breakpoints for the growing tail — so
 the large static prefix is never re-written even when the tail re-anchors.

### Workaround
Keep sessions short (start a fresh chat per task). A shorter conversation has a smaller tail and
fewer message blocks competing for the 4 breakpoints, so re-anchoring happens less often and
invalidates less when it does.

### Related
- **#313920** ("BYOK chat providers receive an internal cache_control LanguageModelDataPart sentinel
 that's not in the public API", Open, `model-byok`) documents the **same `cache_control` /
 `"ephemeral"` sentinel** from a different trigger (the sentinel leaking into serialized tool-result
 text). It states the same root principle this issue relies on:
 > "A leaked `{"$mid":24,...}` substring on one turn changes the byte-exact prefix and misses every
 > subsequent turn's cache — defeating the whole point of the sentinel."
 Same sentinel, same byte-stability failure class, two distinct triggers. Relevant first-party
 converter: `extensions/copilot/src/extension/byok/common/anthropicMessageConverter.ts`.
- Companion issue (workspace-structure tree injected ahead of the cached prefix) — the other
 independent cache-busting cause on the BYOK Anthropic path: .
canonical: The BYOK Anthropic prompt cache is being rewritten instead of incrementally extended because the renderer re-anchors cache breakpoints by changing the byte-exact cached prefix, especially when it inserts or removes the synthetic whitespace anchor.

title: Agent-mode workspace directory tree is injected ahead of the cached prefix and breaks BYOK Anthropic prompt caching
document:
Agent-mode workspace directory tree is injected ahead of the cached prefix and breaks BYOK Anthropic prompt caching

### Type
Bug — performance/cost regression on the BYOK Anthropic path.

### Environment
- VS Code: 1.126.0 (commit 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a)
- GitHub Copilot Chat: 
- OS: macOS (expected on all platforms)
- Model: BYOK Anthropic (Claude Opus 4.8) via `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`
- Occurs with all other extensions disabled: yes

### Summary
In agent mode the prompt includes an injected workspace **directory structure tree**
("I am working in a workspace that has the following structure:") placed **near the head of the
request**, ahead of the large otherwise-static cached prefix (instructions / `AGENTS.md` / tool
definitions).

That tree is regenerated each turn and reflects the live workspace at **depth ≤ 2**. When any
directory at depth ≤ 2 of a workspace/repo root is **created or removed mid-session** (very common —
per-ticket scratch dirs, screenshot folders, test output, downloads), the tree text changes.
Because it sits **before** the cached prefix, the byte change invalidates the entire downstream
prefix, forcing a full Anthropic **cache write** (1.25× input rate) instead of a **cache read**
(0.1×).

There is no setting to disable or stabilize this injection.

### Impact
On a real BYOK Anthropic workload with a ~270K-token cached prefix:
- Measured reuse factor fell to **~4×** (well-tuned Anthropic caching is 20–25×).
- **~22% of turns** were full-prefix re-writes, and **94% of those re-writes happened within 5
 minutes of the prior turn** (i.e. not TTL expiry — a structural bust).
- Captured the exact moving byte: a single `artifacts/p12-NNNN/` directory appearing/disappearing
 at **byte ~800** of a ~669KB request body, cold-rewriting the ~270K-token prefix after it.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Use agent mode with a BYOK Anthropic model and a sizeable cached prefix (instructions +
 `AGENTS.md` + tools).
2. Capture two consecutive raw `/v1/messages` request bodies (e.g. via a transparent local forward
 proxy on `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`).
3. Between the two turns, create or remove a directory at depth ≤ 2 of a workspace root
 (e.g. `mkdir artifacts/some-dir`).
4. Diff the two bodies: the first divergence is inside the "following structure:" tree near the
 head; `cache_creation_input_tokens` spikes and `cache_read_input_tokens` drops to 0 on the
 second turn.

### Root cause (from the shipped extension, 1.126.0)
The tree is emitted by an unconditional `PromptElement` (minified class `LP`):
canonical: Agent mode prepends a live workspace directory tree before the otherwise stable cached prompt prefix, so any workspace folder change invalidates Anthropic prompt caching and forces expensive full-prefix rewrites instead of cache reads.

title: Support Multi-Chat in Codex harness
document:
Support Multi-Chat in Codex harness

Summary

Add Multi-Chat support to the Codex harness so it matches the Copilot experience.

Background

- The Multi-Chat feature (previously called Sub-Sessions) is currently available for the Copilot harness and works in AHP sessions for Copilot.
- Team feedback and user studies have shown confusion around the feature and its name; the UX should be consistent across harnesses rather than Copilot-only.
- Codex AHP sessions do not currently show the Multi-Chat UI.

Proposal

- Implement Multi-Chat in the Codex harness with parity to the Copilot implementation and UX.
- Ensure Multi-Chat is available in AHP sessions for Codex as it is for Copilot.
- Minimize agent integration effort (sessions should be transparent to agents where possible), and document any agent-facing changes required.
- Update tests, e2e scenarios, and relevant documentation to reflect Multi-Chat availability in the Codex harness.

Acceptance criteria

- Multi-Chat UI appears and behaves the same in the Codex harness as it does in Copilot.
- Multi-Chat is available and functional in AHP sessions for Codex.
- Agent/handler code for Codex either requires no changes or any required changes are documented and minimal.
- Relevant tests and documentation updated.

Implementation notes

- Verify whether sessions need to be linked or if the agent surface can remain unchanged (transparent sessions).
- Coordinate UX changes with the Copilot harness team to ensure consistency and avoid repeating prior confusion around naming/behavior.
- Add testing steps to validate in manual and automated tests across the Codex harness and AHP sessions.

---

Split from #322776 (Claude). This issue tracks the Codex harness.

[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C09MVCZKLP4/p1782230045706259?thread_ts=1782230045.706259&cid=C09MVCZKLP4)
canonical: The Codex harness does not support the Multi-Chat feature in AHP sessions, and the fix is to add the same UI and behavior it already has in the Copilot harness with minimal agent-side changes.

title: Increase Settings Sync limit for `keybindings.json` to 200kb
document:
Increase Settings Sync limit for `keybindings.json` to 200kb

## Problem
When using comprehensive keyboard layout extensions or modal editing wrappers like [Master Key](https://haberdashpi.github.io/vscode-master-key/), the `keybindings.json` file size can drastically increase due to the sheer volume of generated shortcuts for various modes and contexts. 

Currently, VS Code strictly enforces a 100kb limit on the keyboard shortcuts file for Settings Sync, throwing the following error and disabling keyboard settings synchronization:
> Disabled syncing keyboard shortcuts because size of the keyboard shortcuts file to sync is larger than 100kb. Please open the file and reduce the size and enable sync

This hard limit penalizes power users who rely on heavy keybinding configurations or ecosystem-altering extensions.

### Describe the solution you'd like
I would like VS Code to increase the maximum allowed file size for `keybindings.json` in Settings Sync from 100kb to 200kb (or higher). This slight overhead would easily accommodate complex, extension-generated keybinding maps without risking server performance.

### Describe alternatives you've considered
* **Manually stripping keybindings:** Not viable as it breaks the functionality provided by key-mapping extensions.
* **Disabling keybindings sync:** A frustrating workaround since keyboard shortcuts are a core part of an engineer's workflow synchronization across multiple machines.

### Additional context
#86178
canonical: Settings Sync disables synchronization of keyboard shortcuts when the keybindings file grows beyond its current hard size limit, and the underlying issue is that complex or extension-generated keybinding setups can exceed that limit.

title: Autopilot/Bypass silently skips user-requested high-risk actions with no opt-out
document:
Autopilot/Bypass silently skips user-requested high-risk actions with no opt-out

## Type
Feature request / behavior gap

## Environment
- VS Code: 1.126
- Copilot Chat: latest
- Permission level: Autopilot (`chat.permissions.default: "autopilot"`, `chat.autopilot.enabled: true`)
- Also set: `chat.tools.global.autoApprove: true`, terminal `/.*/` catch-all auto-approve, `chat.tools.terminal.ignoreDefaultAutoApproveRules: true`

## Summary
Even at the **most permissive** documented configuration (Autopilot), the agent **silently skips** irreversible/destructive tool calls that I *explicitly asked it to perform* (e.g. removing a file, removing a database row). The agent returns:

> Autopilot skipped this tool call because it was automatically assessed as high-risk: ... The action was not performed.

There is **no documented setting** to opt out of this secondary risk gate.

## Why this is confusing (docs vs. behavior)
The [approvals docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agents/approvals) state that Autopilot and Bypass Approvals:

> "...bypass manual approval prompts, **including for potentially destructive actions like file edits, terminal commands, and external tool calls.**"

But in practice a *separate* risk classifier re-blocks destructive actions **after** the permission level is applied. So the documented "approve everything" promise is not honored, and there is no setting (documented or surfaced) to disable this second layer.

## Repro
1. Set permission level to **Autopilot** (or Bypass Approvals).
2. Set `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` to a `/.*/` catch-all and `chat.tools.terminal.ignoreDefaultAutoApproveRules: true`.
3. Ask the agent to remove a file (e.g. "remove scripts/foo.py"), or to remove a row from a database via an MCP tool.
4. Observe: the tool call is auto-skipped as "high-risk" and never executes, despite the explicit request and the most permissive settings.

Notably, the classifier appears to key partly on surface tokens: a command literally containing the removal keyword gets blocked, while a functionally-identical script with a neutral name slips through, so the gate is inconsistent rather than a reliable safety guarantee.

## Expected
One of:
- Autopilot/Bypass honor an **explicit** user request to perform a destructive action (matching the documented behavior), **or**
- Expose a setting (e.g. `chat.tools.allowHighRiskActions` or per-tool `eligibleForAutoApproval`) to opt out of the high-risk gate, **or**
- At minimum, fall back to a **single confirmation prompt** instead of silently skipping with no way to proceed.

## Related
- #313210: "Copilot easily breaking out of sandbox mode" (the inverse concern; shows the team is actively tuning this line)
- #303174: "autopilot mode should allow curl in terminal" (same theme: autopilot more restrictive than expected)
canonical: Autopilot can silently refuse user-requested destructive tool actions because a separate high-risk safety classifier overrides the permissive approval setting, with no way to opt out or confirm the action.

title: Feature request: React-Grab-like customizable element-selection context for the integrated browser
document:
Feature request: React-Grab-like customizable element-selection context for the integrated browser

I’d like to propose a feature for the integrated browser that lets a repo or page provide custom context when a user selects an element and chooses “Add Element to Chat”.

In short, it would enable [react-grab](https://www.react-grab.com/) like functionality through a hook with the code defined in repo. There are a lot of "grab" libraries designed for different web frameworks: [react](https://www.react-grab.com), [angular](https://angular-grab.com/), [svelte](https://meshkore.com/agent/heicg-svelte-grab), [solidjs](https://github.com/omniaura/solid-grab), [vue](https://vue-grab.vercel.app/), etc... This feature would enable these tools to work inside the integrated browser directly. Each framework would have its own npm package to enable the hook on the browser side.

Today, the flow in `browserEditorChatFeatures.ts` builds a standardized snippet from the selected DOM element (HTML, path, dimensions, computed styles, etc.) and sends that into chat. That is useful, but it is not flexible enough for repos with specialized needs.

As a frontend developer, I want to be able to customize the text snippet that gets attached to chat so I can provide richer, repo-specific context to agents. This should make agent-driven edits more accurate and reduce the need for manual follow-up.

Example use case:
- In a React repo, I want the browser selection flow to call custom code that inspects the React virtual DOM / component tree and returns a structured component-context summary.
- I also want to customize CSS output, for example to de-transform CSS module class names before they are attached to chat, or to remove hashed griffel class names.

Proposal:
- When an element is selected in the integrated browser, VS Code should attempt to invoke a page-provided hook that can customize the context payload.
- The hook should be available for both:
 - the manual “Add Element to Chat” flow from the integrated browser UI, and
 - the browser-tool / agent-driven selection path, so the same repo-specific context can be provided regardless of how the selection was triggered.
- The hook could be exposed as an optional global function on the page, for example:
 - `window.__vscodeIntegratedBrowserGetContext?.(elementData)`
- The hook should be able to return either:
 - a string snippet to append to the existing context, or
 - a richer structured payload that VS Code can render into the chat attachment.
- If the hook is not provided, the current built-in behavior remains unchanged.
- For discoverability, we could add a small docs/help entry or info popover near the element-selection button in the integrated browser UI.

Why this is valuable:
- Different repos need different context
- Frontend teams often want framework-specific summaries
- Better context should lead to better agent suggestions and edits

Possible follow-up work:
- Support repo-specific injection via a script already loaded in the page
- Provide a documented API shape and examples
- Add a UI hint/docs link near the selection action
- Add some default formatters for common libraries like react

---

You can contact me directly on Teams at my Microsoft alias
canonical: The integrated browser cannot use repo-defined hooks to customize the context attached when an element is selected for chat, so it always sends the same built-in DOM-based snippet instead of framework- or app-specific information.

title: Proposed API: opt-in, consent-gated camera/microphone access for extension webviews
document:
Proposed API: opt-in, consent-gated camera/microphone access for extension webviews

### Problem

Extension webviews currently can't use `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia` at all — the webview iframe carries no `allow="camera; microphone"`, and the Electron permission handler denies `media` for `vscode-webview://` origins. That's intentional ("media must not be silently accessible", #113916), but it leaves **no opt-in path** for legitimate, user-initiated scenarios: live device/camera preview, audio/video capture, QR scanning, in-editor calls, voice/accessibility input, hardware diagnostics.

Today the only workaround is to spawn `ffmpeg` in the extension host and stream frames back into the webview — heavy and platform-specific, just to reach hardware the OS already exposes.

### Precedent

There's now an active precedent: consent-gated, per-origin, revocable camera/mic/location permissions for the integrated browser (#299521 / PR #322639), **currently in Insiders**. This proposes extending that same safety model to extension webviews.

### Proposed shape (designed to answer the "no silent access" concern)

- Extension opts in via a proposed API (`webviewMediaDevices`) and declares devices on the webview — `mediaDevices: ['camera', 'microphone']` — visible at install time, off by default.
- The iframe `allow` is injected **only** for declaring webviews (per-device).
- First `getUserMedia` triggers a **one-time consent prompt**; the grant is stored **per extension** and is revocable.
- No declaration → no `allow`; no consent → no access.

### Working PoC (desktop), behind the proposed API

Verified end-to-end (declare → consent prompt → `getUserMedia` → live camera/mic in the webview). Deliberately minimal; happy to rework to your conventions, not asking to merge as-is:
https://github.com/chrisdp/vscode/commit/5b9064c626cbc87554c65f0639fa9062d86b72e6

The net-new piece vs the browser work is mainly the iframe `allow` injection (on both the outer webview iframe and the inner content frame, for Permissions-Policy delegation) plus the per-extension grant; the consent UI/store could be reused.

### The ask

Would the team be open to this direction? If so, we'll productionize (persisted/revocable grants + management UI, renderer-brokered consent, remote/web/WSL hosts).

### Related
- #250568 — Permissions-policy violation report (microphone not allowed in a webview)
- #113916 — earlier decision that webview media should be blocked without a prompt
- #299521 / PR #322639 — consent-gated browser permissions (in Insiders), the model this builds on
canonical: Extension webviews cannot access camera or microphone today because media permission is blocked by default with no opt-in, consent-gated path, and the underlying cause is that webview frames are not granted the necessary allow policy and their origin is denied media access.

title: Agents window: "New Chat" button is a no-op from within a current session

1. Open the Agents window
2. Start / open a session (so you are in an active session)
3. Click the **+ New Chat** button (top of the session view)

## Expected

Clicking **+ New Chat** should start a new chat / session.

## Actual

Nothing happens — the button is a no-op when invoked from within a current session.

---
canonical: The New Chat button does nothing when clicked from an active session because the action to start a new session is not being triggered in that context.

title: Do not include `previous_response_id` in summary requests.
document:
Do not include `previous_response_id` in summary requests.

Including `previous_response_id` in the summarization request causes a conflict.
Since the summary request completes in a single turn, `previous_response_id` is not required.
I have confirmed this issue with the BYOK custom endpoint's Responses API.
I haven't confirmed it yet with anything other than custom endpoint.
canonical: Summary requests incorrectly include `previous_response_id`, causing a conflict because the summary completes in a single turn and does not need prior response context.

title: Isolation picker shows only 'Folder' (grayed out) when folder is not git-backed
document:
Isolation picker shows only 'Folder' (grayed out) when folder is not git-backed

- VS Code Version: main
- OS Version: 

1. Create or open a folder that is not a git repository.
2. Open the isolation picker (the UI that lets you choose how to open the workspace/isolation target).
3. Observe that only the "Folder" option is shown and it appears grayed out; the "Worktree" option is not shown and there is no indication why.

Expected behavior:

- The isolation picker should indicate why Worktree is unavailable when the folder is not git-backed. Preferably both options (Folder and Worktree) could be shown, and when the user selects Worktree, VS Code could offer to initialize a git repository in that folder (git init) or provide a clear action to enable worktrees.

Actual behavior:

- Only "Folder" is shown (grayed out) with no explanation, which is confusing for users who expect worktrees to be available.

Proposed fix / suggestion:

- Show both options in the isolation picker. If the folder is not git-backed, either:
 - Disable the Worktree option but show a clear message explaining that a git repository is required and offer an inline action to initialize git in the folder, or
 - Allow selecting Worktree and prompt to initialize a git repository (run `git init`) in that folder so the user can proceed.

Notes:

- This came up during internal testing of a new repository — the folder being new (no git) made the picker behavior confusing. Adding an explanatory message or an init action should improve discoverability and UX.

[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C09MVCZKLP4/p1782750547562709?thread_ts=1782750547.562709&cid=C09MVCZKLP4)
canonical: The isolation picker incorrectly hides the worktree option and gives no explanation when the selected folder is not a Git repository, likely because the UI only enables worktree choices for git-backed folders.

title: Copilot run_in_terminal sandbox dependency installer is hardcoded to apt, breaks on Arch/Linux non-Debian distros
document:
Copilot run_in_terminal sandbox dependency installer is hardcoded to apt, breaks on Arch/Linux non-Debian distros

### Context
Using Copilot Chat terminal tools on Arch Linux with sandboxed execution enabled.

### Problem
When the terminal tool enters the sandbox dependency install/remediation flow, it attempts to run:

`sudo apt install -y `

On Arch (and other non-Debian distros), this fails because `apt` is not available. The user-facing result is:

`Sandbox dependency installation failed (exit code ...). The command was not executed.`

### Repro
1. On Arch Linux, ensure `bwrap` and `socat` are installed (for example via `pacman`).
2. Use Copilot Chat terminal tool (`run_in_terminal`) with sandboxed execution flow enabled.
3. Trigger a path that invokes missing dependency installation/remediation.
4. Observe attempted use of `apt` and failure.

### Expected
Dependency installation/remediation should be distro-aware (e.g. `pacman` on Arch, `dnf` on Fedora, `zypper` on openSUSE, etc.), or avoid shelling out to distro package managers directly and instead provide actionable instructions.

### Actual
Installer command is hardcoded to `apt`.

### Source reference
In `src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminalContrib/chatAgentTools/common/terminalSandboxService.ts`, `installMissingSandboxDependencies(...)` currently uses:

`sudo apt install -y ${depsList}`

This appears to be the root cause for Linux distro mismatch.

### Suggestion
- Detect distro/package manager at runtime and choose appropriate command.
- If unsupported/unknown distro, surface a targeted prompt with a copyable command suggestion instead of assuming `apt`.
- Optionally allow a configurable package install command for enterprise/custom environments.

Thanks!
canonical: The sandbox dependency installer is hardcoded to use Debian-based package management, causing terminal sandbox remediation to fail on non-Debian Linux distributions because it assumes the wrong system package manager.

title: Intermittant failure launching debugging - GDB: Failed to set controlling terminal: Operation not permitted
document:
Intermittant failure launching debugging - GDB: Failed to set controlling terminal: Operation not permitted

- VS Code Version: 
- OS Version: 

No idea how to make it occur... it appears infrequently and randomly.

Trying to debug a C++ application using gdb from the Visual Code GUI.

Platform Ubuntu 26.04, visual code 1.122 (happens on 1.126 as well). gdb 17.1.

This is a very frustrating bug... when it occurs nothing seems to resolve it.... until what I discovered just now.

I had Visual Code just sat there with this error in the terminal window
**"&"\342\232\240\357\270\217 warning: GDB: Failed to set controlling terminal: Operation not permitted\n""**
while I was doing something else eventually gdb woke up and I could see I could step \ continue without issue.

I figured out that when you launch a debugging session, you get this error message., and then after about 400 seconds, gdb activity happens. gdb is trrying to obtain Ubuntu gdb symbols from a remove internet server.

As usual with a bug issue microsoft are useless. Instead of highlighting the solution they just complain its not our code.

So I found the working solution - see https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-cpptools/issues/13667 for overview.

The jist of which is... Prevent gdb from trying to talk to remote symbols servers by adding this into your "launch.json" file -->

I hope that saves others from wasting hours of time trying to fix this issue.
canonical: The debugger startup is delayed or appears to fail because GDB is trying to fetch external debug symbols over the network, which leads to a controlling-terminal warning and an apparent hang until the download finishes.

title: Mermaid Markdown preview flashes then disappears; parser receives rendered CSS/SVG instead of diagram source
document:
Mermaid Markdown preview flashes then disappears; parser receives rendered CSS/SVG instead of diagram source

### VS Code Version
1.127.0-insider

### OS Version
Windows host + WSL2 Linux

### Repro Steps
1. Open a Markdown file containing a Mermaid code block.
2. Open Markdown Preview.
3. Diagram appears briefly, then disappears.
4. In failing state, preview may show error:
 "No diagram type detected matching given configuration for text: #dmermaid-... @keyframes ..."

### Expected Behavior
Mermaid diagram remains visible in Markdown Preview.

### Actual Behavior
Preview flashes, then diagram is removed or parser fails because rendered CSS/SVG is treated as Mermaid source.

### Suspected Area
Builtin extension: mermaid-markdown-features, file markdown-preview-out/index.js
Function: renderMermaidElement + rerender lifecycle in renderMermaidBlocksInElement.

### Patch/Workaround That Fixed It Locally
I applied a local runtime patch in both VS Code runtime copies (server + UI runtime):

1) Avoid destructive global cleanup that removes existing rendered nodes during rerender.
- Removed cleanup loop over ".mermaid > svg"
- Removed cleanup loop removing parent IDs starting with "dmermaid"

2) Make source recovery robust when container text is polluted by rendered output.
- In renderMermaidElement, detect polluted text patterns (for example "#dmermaid-", "@keyframes", "flowchartTitleText").
- Preserve canonical Mermaid source in dataset.vscodeMermaidSource.
- Reuse canonical source (or prior vscodeContext.mermaidSource fallback) instead of parsing rendered CSS/SVG.

Pseudo diff idea:

### Notes
After this patch, Mermaid preview is stable and no longer disappears.
canonical: The Mermaid preview is being erased and re-rendered from its own generated SVG/CSS instead of the original diagram text because the rerender logic cleans up existing output too aggressively and then parses polluted container content as source.

title: Test: Windows agent terminal sandboxing
document:
Test: Windows agent terminal sandboxing

Refs #323224

- [ ] windows @vijayupadya

Complexity: 4

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323515%0A%0A&assignees=dileepyavan)

---

## Context

Windows agent terminal sandboxing uses the MXC runtime (`wxc-exec.exe`) . This test plan item validates that agent shell commands on Windows are wrapped through MXC when sandboxing is enabled, that workspace/temp filesystem access behaves as expected, configured filesystem rules are honored, and explicit unsandboxed execution does not happen silently.

## Setup

- Use a Windows machine preferebly a VM as this needs windows insider version. More details [here](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/windowsinsider/getting-started).
- Open a workspace folder.
- Enable agent terminal sandboxing:
 - `chat.agent.sandbox.enabled`: `on`
- For Agent Host testing, use the Agent Host/custom terminal tool path if available.

[Create issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%323515%0A%0A&assignees=dileepyavan)

## Test Steps

### 1. Verify a sandboxed shell command runs

1. Open Chat or the Agents Window.
2. Start a local Agent Host or Copilot agent session.
3. Ask the agent to run a simple shell command, for example:
 - `echo WINDOWS_SANDBOX_TEST`
 - `pwd`
 - `dir`
4. Verify the command completes successfully.

**Expected:**

- The command output is returned to chat.
- The command is reported/logged as sandboxed.
- On Windows, the wrapped command/config path uses MXC / `wxc-exec.exe`, not the POSIX `sandbox-runtime`.

### 2. Verify workspace write access

1. Ask the agent to create a temporary file in the workspace, for example:
 - `echo hello > .sandbox-windows-test.txt`
2. Ask the agent to read the file back.
3. Ask the agent to delete it.

**Expected:**

- Writing inside the workspace succeeds.
- Reading the file succeeds.
- Cleanup succeeds.
- The sandbox config exposes the workspace as writable.

### 3. Verify filesystem restrictions outside the workspace

1. Ask the agent to read or write a path outside the allowed workspace/temp locations, for example under the user profile.
2. If a custom sandbox filesystem setting is configured, include a specific denied/read-only path using the setting `chat.agent.sandbox.fileSystem.windows`

**Expected:**

- Disallowed writes are blocked or fail.
- Allowed read-only paths can be read but not modified.
- The agent reports failures clearly and does not silently rerun the command unsandboxed.

### 4. Verify unsandboxed execution confirmation

1. Ask the agent to run a command with explicit unsandboxed execution, or use a path that requires escaping the sandbox.
2. Test with unsandboxed commands allowed and disabled if possible.

**Expected:**

- If unsandboxed commands are allowed, VS Code asks for confirmation before running outside the sandbox.
- If unsandboxed commands are disabled, the command does not run unsandboxed and the failure is surfaced.

### 5. Verify shell/environment behavior

1. Run commands in PowerShell that depend on normal Windows environment setup, for example:
 - `$env:PATH`
 - `$env:USERPROFILE`
 - `Get-Location`
2. Optionally test with a non-default PowerShell path if available.

**Expected:**

- The shell launches with the expected environment.
- The working directory is the workspace.
- Basic PowerShell commands work inside the sandbox.
canonical: Windows agent terminal sandboxing is broken if shell commands are not routed through the Windows sandbox runtime, causing workspace access, filesystem restrictions, or unsandboxed fallback behavior to bypass the intended sandbox controls.

title: Explorer context menu command target depends on selection size
document:
Explorer context menu command target depends on selection size

I discovered a surprising Explorer behavior where the target of a context menu command depends on the size of the current selection.

When a context menu is invoked on a file that is not part of the current selection:

- No selection → command operates on the clicked file.
- Selection contains 1 different file → command operates on the clicked file.
- Selection contains 2+ different files → command operates on the selected files and ignores the clicked file.

The same interaction therefore produces different results depending only on the number of selected items. This feels inconsistent because the existing selection is either respected or ignored based solely on its size.

Besides being surprising, this can also lead to unexpected command execution. For example, a multi-selection may exist outside the current viewport and may not be visible when a user right-clicks another file, or the parent folder of a selection may be collapsed. In that situation, commands such as Copy, Cut, Paste, Delete, operate on the hidden selection. The selection may have been made some time ago and forgotten. 

To avoid this behavior, users must click the target file or press Esc to clear the selection before executing context menu commands. It feels that the selection should always be ignored when a context menu is invoked for an item outside the selection.

Is this behavior intentional? If so, what is the rationale for treating a single selected item differently from a multi-selection when the context menu is invoked outside the selection?
canonical: File Explorer context menu actions can unexpectedly apply to a hidden multi-selection instead of the item that was right-clicked, with the target determined differently for single-item versus multi-item selections.

title: Inconsistent plugin+mcp behavior in VS Code
document:
Inconsistent plugin+mcp behavior in VS Code

### Description

Two inconsistencies between VS Code Copilot and Copilot CLI / Copilot App:

a) Copilot CLI/App by default set the cwd of a plugin-installed MCP server to the plugin root. VS Code sets the cwd to the user home folder.

b) Copilot CLI/App supports {PLUGIN_ROOT} and {CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} in .mcp.json, and will replace either, regardless of the origin of the plugin (Copilot/Claude/Open Plugin). VS Code supports those as well, but will only do the replacement depending on the origin of the plugin (Open Plugin for the former, Claude Plugin for the latter). Thus it won't do any replacement for Copilot Plugins. Documented here: https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agent-customization/agent-plugins#_plugin-environment-variables

At the moment it is impossible for an MCP server installed as a Copilot plugin to run any local scripts, since it has no way to specify their location (which is relative to the location where the plugin is installed). E.g. the MCP definition below will work in CLI and GitHub App but not in VSCode. 

Suggested Fix (for consistency with CLI and GitHub app (and SDK():
a) VS Code: Execute MCP server commands using the plugin install location as the cwd
b) VS Code: Apply {PLUGIN_ROOT} and {CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} replacements regardless of origin of the plugin.

I would suggest having a conversation with the Copilot team responsible for Copilot Plugins to fully specify the expected behavior of plugins and MCPs to ensure all implementations are consistent.

### Issue Details

Item|Value
---|---
Issue Category|Bug
Target|Agents Window
VS Code Version|Code 1.126.0 (7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a, 2026-06-23T01:46:13Z)
OS Version|Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200
canonical: VS Code runs plugin-installed MCP servers from the user home folder and only substitutes plugin-root path variables for some plugin types, so Copilot plugins cannot reliably invoke local files relative to their install location.

- With Copilot AH ask `use a subagent to say hi in dutch and use the gpt-5-mini model for the subagent`

- Then ask `can you ask the subagent to say hi in dutch again but now use the gpt-5-5 model`

- Hover says: Session Cost 19.7 credits

- `0.6 credits` is already unclear. Should it be `credits used' or 'spent'? 
- Unclear if `5.2` is the cost of just Sonnet, or the full turn const; From the totals (5.2 + 14.5 = 19.7) I assume its the turn: Maybe call it `Turn cost: 5.2`. But given that it is right behind the 'Sonnet' maybe only list the Sonnet cost and make the gpt-5-mini cost more prominent
- It's interesting that the gpt-5-5 subagent call is so expensive and why the first sonnet call cost twice that the first one.
- Not sure what I should do with all these numbers. Maybe all that could be put in hovers? What seems important is that I know that expensive models have been used as subagents. Maybe the `$$$` could be added on such calls to warn.
canonical: The credit annotations are confusing because the session and per-model costs are ambiguous and poorly emphasized, making it unclear what was spent on the main turn versus subagent calls.

title: Add experiment-controlled setting for Markdown default editor in Agents window
document:
Add experiment-controlled setting for Markdown default editor in Agents window

The Agents window currently forces `vscode.markdown.editor` as the default editor for `*.md` files. We want to gate this behind an experiment so it can be rolled out gradually.

- New setting `workbench.editor.markdownDefaultEditorInAgentsWindow` (boolean, `tags: ['experimental']`, `experiment: { mode: 'startup' }`), default `false`.
- When enabled: `*.md` -> `vscode.markdown.editor`.
- When disabled (default): `*.md` -> `vscode.markdown.preview.editor` (prior behavior before #322703).

Tracking issue to monitor the experiment and decide on default rollout.
canonical: The Agents window should only open Markdown files in the Markdown editor when the experimental setting is enabled, otherwise it should keep using the Markdown preview editor by default.

title: Selecting a custom agent at session creation is ignored — session starts with the default agent
document:
Selecting a custom agent at session creation is ignored — session starts with the default agent

When creating a new session in the Agents window and selecting a custom agent (e.g. **Agents Window Developer**) for a Copilot session from Agent Host, the created session does **not** use the selected custom agent — it starts with the **default** agent instead.

## Steps
1. Open the Agents window → New Sessions view.
2. Select the **vscode** folder using a Copilot session from Agent Host (this is needed to see the **Agents Window Developer** custom agent), and a worktree.
3. Select the **Agents Window Developer** custom agent.
4. Create/start the session.

🐛 The session runs the **default** agent, not the selected custom agent.
canonical: Creating a new session with a selected custom agent ignores that choice and starts the session with the default agent instead, likely because the selected agent is not being passed through when the session is created.
